## Thread: Threads and NetExpress- Unresolved external — 13 msgs

### Re: Threads and NetExpress- Unresolved external

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m59ct$snl$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7m246l$vti$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7m2kpv$r9f$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
OK...We have narrowed this down to the line of code causing the unresolved
external...It has something to do with the cobol monitor(s)...


Set monitor-name to writing converting from browsing



is the statement causing the unresolved external reference...

Anyone out there use the cobol monitor(s)...???
```

#### ↳ Re: Threads and NetExpress- Unresolved external

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378630F8.72BB6511@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<7m246l$vti$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7m2kpv$r9f$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <7m59ct$snl$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken Mullins wrote:
> 
> OK...We have narrowed this down to the line of code causing the unresolved
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone out there use the cobol monitor(s)...???

If I fail to open the monitor I get a unresolved external, however if
I open the monitor it links okay....

For the purpose of testing the link stage I tried...

        working-storage section.
        01 monitor-name usage monitor-pointer.
        procedure division.
                open monitor-name
                set monitor-name to writing converting from browsing
                close monitor-name.

Have you opened the monitor?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Threads and NetExpress- Unresolved external

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m5ju2$rt4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7m246l$vti$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7m2kpv$r9f$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <7m59ct$snl$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <378630F8.72BB6511@merant.com>`

```
Stephen...

Many thanks...Seems the open monitor statement must be ""physically"" before
any set statement for the monitor...In our case, the monitor is opened, but
in a performed routine that is physically after the set monitor
statement...The following code causes an unresolved external in error...

         working-storage section.
         01 monitor-name usage monitor-pointer.
         procedure division.
                   perform open-monitors
                   set monitor-name to writing converting from browsing
                   close monitor-name.
                   exit program
           open-monitors.
                   open monitor-name
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Threads and NetExpress- Unresolved external

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3789CF92.C2088D83@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<7m246l$vti$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7m2kpv$r9f$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <7m59ct$snl$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <378630F8.72BB6511@merant.com> <7m5ju2$rt4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken Mullins wrote:
> 
> Stephen...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>                    open monitor-name

Hi Ken,

I too can reproduce the issue when you compile your application
above.  However the application should be compiled with either one of the
two reentrant (1/2) directives as this is the threaded application.

If you use reentrant"2" it works.  Hope this helps.

        $set reentrant"2"
         working-storage section.
         01 monitor-name usage monitor-pointer.
         procedure division.
                   perform open-monitors
                   set monitor-name to writing converting from browsing
                   close monitor-name.
                   exit program.
           open-monitors.
                   open monitor-name
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Threads and NetExpress- Unresolved external

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378B0304.462CF453@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<7m246l$vti$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7m2kpv$r9f$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <7m59ct$snl$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <378630F8.72BB6511@merant.com> <7m5ju2$rt4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3789CF92.C2088D83@merant.com>`

```
Stephen Gennard wrote:
> If you use reentrant"2" it works.  Hope this helps.
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>                    open monitor-name

I agree with what Stephen has said, however I should point out that
the purpose of the reentrant"2" directive is to make your DATA DIVISION
thread-local (ie, it's a way of introducing certain legacy modules into
a threaded environment without any program source changes).

Because of this, you would be using monitors to synchronise access to
thread-local data, which is pointless. As Stephen says in his other
message, use the CBL_ calls rather than COBOL syntax to manipulate your
monitors or install the appropriate checker WebSync when it is released.

Cheers, Kev.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Threads and NetExpress- Unresolved external

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "ber" <barry.rosetti@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mlako$5vm$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7m246l$vti$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7m2kpv$r9f$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <7m59ct$snl$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <378630F8.72BB6511@merant.com> <7m5ju2$rt4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3789CF92.C2088D83@merant.com>`

```
I just wanted to follow up on this issue so that those of you
that use the multi-threading features of MERANT Micro Focus
COBOL (either Net Express or Server Express) use the right
compiler directives and techniques.

First - the directives.
If your program is going to be executed in a multithreaded
environment then you should use one of the following checker
directives:
  SERIAL    - this directive ensures that only one thread is active
              in your COBOL program at a time.  Any other programs in
              your COBOL application that are directly called _only_
              from the program compiled with the SERIAL directive
              are implicitly serialized as well.
              Access to working storage items do not need to be
              synchronized, however access to external data items
              will need to be.
  REENTRANT - this directive allows multiple threads to enter the program
              at the same time.
              All access to WORKING-STORAGE items or EXTERNAL data items
              must be synchronized with MUTEXes, MONITORS or some other
              technique.
REENTRANT(2)- this directive allows multiple threads to enter the program
              at the same time.  Working-storage is effectively
              THREAD-LOCAL (ie. persistant from call to call within a
              thread, but different for each thread).
              Access to EXTERNAL data items must be synchronized.

Programs compiled with none of these directives MUST be called only from
one thread at a time.

Second - the techniques (this unfortunate bug aside).
0) Read and understand the Multi-threading white-paper in the docs
   or have a good previous grounding in multi-threaded application
   development.

1) You should usually check that the run-time you are using really is
   the multi-threaded run-time system.  The code to do this looks like
   this:
      01  my-thread-id  usage thread-pointer.
      call 'CBL_THREAD_SELF' using my-thread-id
      on exception
          display "NO CBL_THREAD API SUPPORT"
          stop run
      end-call
      if  return-code == 1008
          display "CANNOT RUN MULTI-THREADED APPLICATION"
          stop run
      end-if

2) You must only initialize a synchronization object ONCE in a run-unit.
   This is usually done in 'first time in' code of a reentrant program
   that looks like this:
       01  my-monitor usage monitor-pointer.
       01  pic x value 'Y'.
           88  first-time-in    value 'Y' false 'N'.
       IF  first-time-in
           call 'CBL_THREAD_PROG_LOCK'
           if  first-time-in
               open my-monitor
           end-if
           call 'CBL_THREAD_PROG_UNLOCK'
       end-if
   If your MONITOR-POINTER is EXTERNAL then the 'first-time-in' flag
   must also be external.

3) MONITOR/MUTEX/EVENT-POINTERs can be provided explicitly to the
   matching RTS APIs.  In order to work around the bug that prompted
   this note, you would replace:
       set monitor-name to writing converting from browsing
   with:
       call 'CBL_MONITOR_BROWSE_TO_WRITE' using monitor-name
       if  return-code not = 0
           display "something bad has happened - return code " return-code
           stop run
       end-if

4) In a REENTRANT program, if possible use LOCAL-STORAGE data-items
   for work items, rather than THREAD-LOCAL-STORAGE or WORKING-STORAGE.
   There is a significant impact on program-entry speed when
   THREAD-LOCAL-STORAGE is used within that program.
   Using WORKING-STORAGE (of course) requires synchronization constructs.
```

```
The issues involved in going multi-thread are really no different than the
issues of going multi-user.  They consist basically of file lockout, plus
communication between simultaneous programs(if required).

If you have written stuff that runs on multiple machines, you probably know
everything that you need to know beyond the simple mechanics of starting new
threads.
```

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> The issues involved in going multi-thread are really no different than the
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Jimmy, Calgary AB

Well I did write 'stuff' but that was a loooong while ago on a Radio
Shack Model 16 using RM/COBOOL and Microsoft Xenix. (The company was
taken over by Corporation "X" who sent up an analyst to have a look at
the system. The office staff told me he was quite impressed with what
one person had achieved on a clunker like Radio Shack).

From a 'systems' point of view I question that my end-user needs to go
multi-thread. He operates away from base 500-600 miles north of Calgary
out in the boonies at isolated oil/gas plants, and although Calgary is
extremely high-tech, even cell phones don't perform well in the vast
North. About a year ago he mused about bouncing data from his PC in his
van, via satellite, to his home office in Calgary - he was serious.

With that kind of tecchie I'm sometimes asked to make PCs do cartwheels.

Essentially I've designed the current application so that he can access 
and edit "description files" from the Class/Program he is using without
needing to start up another thread. However, he could be editing for
Client A and at the same time want to create a thread which allows him
to print reports for Client B - and then as an afterthought he will want
to do some editing for Client B. Generally speaking he would be
accessing data files particular to a Client, although there are four
'generic' files applicable to all clients.

If it really is parallel to multi-user - then maybe it's no big deal
when I scrape the rust from my memory.

PS. Wasn't deliberate - Corporation "X" - big chemical corporation, the
name is on the tip of tongue - they had that misfortune in Bhopal,
India.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <378FA695.8C58236F@home.com>...

>If it really is parallel to multi-user - then maybe it's no big deal
>when I scrape the rust from my memory.
>ETC.

It sounds just the opposite of the type of thing I am doing, but the
multiple user issues are the same.  One of my typical systems has a single
user running six-seven CPU's.  Each is a separate but simultaneous process,
running in real time.

From a systems point of view, each process runs as an semi-invisible user on
a central data base.  However, the central data base is simply a common disk
area.  All the machines are within 5 feet of each other, and only the
sensors extend out into the field.  The one exception to that is short range
Ethernet-radio to the PC's mounted in moving equipment.

If you think of that model, though, the concept is identical to running two
threads. Each computer using the central data-base happens to have it's own
CPU. Threading just emulates that on a single CPU.

Once you start a second Thread, you have two Cobol programs running at once
in a common data space.
```

```
Hi Ken,

I have just spoken to the compiler group and asked them about
this issue.  I was told to inform you that a  RPI has been
raised to cover this issue.  The RPI number is : 516420 (Sev 1).

The checker guys are not happy with the code generated for this
syntax and would recommend you get a fix for the issue or use 
the appropriate CBL_ threading calls.
```

<a id="t-2068a53ac4"></a>
## Thread: Dreaded File Status 39 — 28 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Dreaded File Status 39

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net>`

```
I know there's no way around this but I'm just aggravated enough about
it to gripe on the newsgroup, especially since it USED to work with
the old OS/VS Cobol.

I'm using Cobol for MVS and VM on OS/390. I think it's Cobol version
2.2.1. I have an unknown quantity of RECFM=VB files with unknown
maximum LRECL's. The goal is to have a universal logging program to
record files coming from external company sources. I want to open the
files as input and read the records so I can log the record count
amoung other things.

Problem is, in order to open a 'VB' file the max record length in the
FD has to match the LRECL otherwise you get the dreaded file status 39
open error. :( This is just dumb in my opinion and I know I'm going to
get all kinds of contrary opinions from others why they think it's
great.  The old OS/VS Cobol used to let you put RECORDING MODE V and
have a record with PIC X(32756) and it would open any 'VB' file on a
single FD.  That's the way SHOULD work.  That's the point of having
variable length records isn't it? They're variable! Why make the
maximum fixed? Why should the program care? Pick it up dynamically
from the system.

The other thing that makes this especially annoying is that you CAN
do just that with fixed length RECFM=FB files. If you say RECORD
CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS with a record of PIC X(32760), it will pick
up the LRECL from the system and open ANY 'FB' dataset on a single FD.

So you can have variable - fixed length records but you can't have
have variable - variable length records.

I going to have to write an assembler subroutine to count records just
because of this one dumb limititation in Cobol.

.. Okay end of rant, I feel better.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39

```
>n article <7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net>, Cityboy@Concentric.Net 
>says...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think that the problem is that the compiler is attempting to optimize and
thinks it knows better than you.   I have found that RECORDING MODE IS V can
get overriden at compile time IF there is only one record definition.  The
compiler sees only one record length and will change/ignore your Variable
definition.
In past experience I have found it best to at least define a minimum record
size and a maximum record size to force the compiler to agree with the
RECORDING MODE defined.
```

```
The way I get around the idiocy that is supposedly required by the
unprintable standards committee in MVS COBOL is to code LRECL= maximum
expected in the DD statement for the file for SB (spanned Blocked) and
VB (variable blocked).  My opinion of ANSI is that you have a large
number of intelligent people who will kill COBOL because they don't
understand incremental development and timely provision of product.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m8lff$9h@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net>`

```
A few "bits" of information on this:

1) In some (not all) cases, switching from NOCMPR2 to CMPR2 will allow you
to avoid FS=39 (on IBM mainframe compilers) - but will also prohibit you
using many '85 Standard features.

2) The ANSI/ISO committee was *very* clear that it is up to each implementor
(not the Standard much less the committee) as to WHICH "fixed file
attributes" would be checked to give a FS=39.  Therefore, if you don't like
IBM's "choice" on this - blame IBM, not the Standards committee (although I
have seen IBM "in print" claim that it was the Standard that required this).

3) Record Mode is V; LRECL, DCB, etc ... are all IBM-things.   The POINT of
FS=39 was (is?) to make certain that when you create a file (using COBOL-x,
i.e. implementor X's Release Y.Z) with certain attributes and then go to
READ it in a program created by the same compiler, that you are actually
looking at the same file defined the same way.  The reason that the Standard
was "clarified" to make it clear that not all "fixed file attributes" needed
to be checked - was that not all files are even created by COBOL much less
the SAME COBOL.

4) The next Standard will allow "dynamic" changing of files associated with
an FD (already allowed in many implementations - but not IBM's).  HOWEVER,
it will not let you dynamically change the "attributes" associated with a
file.  If this is something that you think COBOL needs, then

   A) tell ANSI/ISO to add it in a future Standard
   B) tell your "vendor of choice" to add it soon as an extension (some
already do - but not IBM)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37896ade.50897404@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <7m8lff$9h@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Sat, 10 Jul 1999 18:35:24 -0500, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> 4) The next Standard will allow "dynamic" changing of files associated with
> an FD (already allowed in many implementations - but not IBM's).  HOWEVER,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> already do - but not IBM)

Burroughs Large System (now Unisys A Series) had this feature since
COBOL-68.  In the IBM world when you ask how to do some simple file
operation, such as changing the the external name, in COBOL they always
say "You can't do that in COBOL, you have to do that in BAL."  When you
ask _why_ you can't do that in COBOL they say "COBOL programmers don't do
that; only BAL programmer ever do that."  Well, duh.  

In Burroughs shops you only needed COBOL programmers.  You never needed
programmers who did another language, because everything was available in
COBOL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HRQh3.6976$5r1.53512@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net>`

```
Well, let's see if the Language Ref from IBM on the COBOL for MVS/VM can shed some LIGHT?

Language Reference
 
IBM COBOL Set for AIX
IBM COBOL for MVS & VM
IBM VisualAge for COBOL for OS/2
 
Document Number SC26-4769-02
 
5.2.4 BLOCK CONTAINS Clause
 
 PICTURE 131 Under AIX and OS/2, the BLOCK CONTAINS clause is not supported for line sequential files.  It is treated as  a comment for sequential, relative and indexed files. PICTURE 132
 
The BLOCK CONTAINS clause specifies the size of the physical records.  The characters in the BLOCK CONTAINS clause reflect the number of bytes in the record.  (For example, if you have a block with 10 DBCS characters, the BLOCK CONTAINS clause should say BLOCK CONTAINS 20 CHARACTERS.

....
....
....

When running under MVS, BLOCK CONTAINS 0 can be specified for QSAM files; the block size is determined at object  time from the DD parameters or the data set label.
 
If the RECORD CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS clause is specified, and the BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS clause is  specified (or omitted), the block size is determined at object time from the DD parameters or the data set label of the file.  

For output data sets, with either of the above conditions, the DCB used by Language Environment will have a zero block size value.  If you do not specify a block size value, the operating system might select a System Determined Block Size  (SDB).  See the operating system specifications for further information on SDB.
 
BLOCK CONTAINS can be omitted for SYSIN/SYSOUT files under MVS.  The blocking is determined by the operating  system.

5.2.5 RECORD Clause
 
The characters in the RECORD clause reflect the number of bytes in the record.  (For example, if you have a record with 
10 DBCS characters, the RECORD clause should say RECORD CONTAINS 20 CHARACTERS.)
 
Subtopics
5.2.5.1 Format 1
5.2.5.2 Format 2
5.2.5.3 Format 3

5.2.5.1 Format 1


     PICTURE 135 Under MVS, the RECORD CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS clause can be specified for input QSAM files 
    containing fixed-length records; the record size is determined at object time from the DD statement parameters or the 
    data set label.  If, at object time, the actual record is larger than the 01 record description, only the 01 record length is 
    available.  If the actual record is shorter, only the actual record length can be referred to.  Otherwise, uninitialized data 
    or an addressing exception can be produced.
 
    Note:  If the RECORD CONTAINS 0 clause is specified, then the SAME AREA, SAME RECORD AREA, or APPLY 
    WRITE-ONLY clauses cannot be specified. PICTURE 136
 
    Do not specify the RECORD CONTAINS 0 clause for an SD entry.

So, we see that the BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS causes the Run time to obtan the Block size from
the DSN Label,
AND The RECORD CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS to get the LRECL from the DSN label.


So, you could code BLOCK 0, RECORD 0 and 01  BIG-REC PIC X(32764) and check the data

OR, you could do BLOCK 0, RECORD VARYING from 1 to 32764 CHARACTERS DEPENDING ON WS-BYTES-IN-REC.
FD MYFILE
     RECORD CONTAINS 1 TO 32764 CHARACTERS
     BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS.
01  LITTLE-REC   PIC X.
01  BIG-REC       PIC X(32764).
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  WS-BYTES-IN-REC                 PIC S9(4) COMP.
P DIV.
OPEN INPUT MYFILE.
READ MYFILE
  AT END ??

MOVE BIG-REC (1:WS-BYTES-IN-REC)  TO WS-NEW-REC.  (REFERENCE MODIFICATION MOVE)


Hope this Helps

>I know there's no way around this but I'm just aggravated enough about
>it to gripe on the newsgroup, especially since it USED to work with
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net
- **Date:** 1999-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7maoom$k3f@journal.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <HRQh3.6976$5r1.53512@news3.mia>`

```
>Well, let's see if the Language Ref from IBM on the COBOL for MVS/VM can =
>shed some LIGHT?
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No it doesn't help a bit. I read all that in the manual too. First,
the block size is not in question. Coding RECORD CONTAINS 1 TO 32756
does not work either. That ONLY works IF the file is cataloged with
the high number as the LRECL. If the LRECL were for example 255 bytes,
then you'd get the file status 39 open error because Cobol expects the
LRECL to be 32756.

The consensus answer seems to be override the LRECL and force it to be
32756, then the coding will work. But there is no answer in pure Cobol
without 'fooling' the system first:

//STEP1.INFILE DD DCB=(LRECL=32756,BLKSIZE=32760,RECFM=VB)

I will test this when I get into the office tomorrow and post
the results.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qwdi3.2303$Gx.22624@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net>`

```
When in doubt, use another language or utility. My suggestion would be
SyncSort or DFSort. Trap the output from SYSPRINT and parse it up using
possibly COBOL, but I recommend REXX and capture the record counts displayed
in the output.

Your problem appears to be in the compiler you are using or the compiler
options used at your site. When confronted with these problems you only have
two choices, solve them with COBOL, which appears to be failing. OR use
another language/utility. You suggested Assembler, if that is easy for you,
more power to ya. But SyncSort and DFSort provide at least record counts and
most likely a record length which could then be used to create the few stats
you said you needed.

Furthermore with this information you could dynamically compile a COBOL
program with the record lengths from this output to give the record counts
or whatever further statistics you need. But that seems rather complicated.
You may also want to check into the LISTCAT TSO command. Using IKJEFT01 you
may be able to derive other important statistics.

Even yet another suggestion would be to use IEBGENER, IEBCOPY, or REPRO and
capture the SYSPRINT or appropriate output to get record count and record
length statistics. These utilities and more appropriately SyncSort/DFSort
would most likely get record count and lengths more efficiently.

Good luck.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39

- **From:** Alex Flinsch <aflinsch@njeb.att.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net>`

```


Cityboy@Concentric.Net wrote:

> I know there's no way around this but I'm just aggravated enough about
> it to gripe on the newsgroup, especially since it USED to work with
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> .. Okay end of rant, I feel better.

Actually you can use a single program, with 1 FD for both fixed and
variable record lengths. The problem is that you are are forcing COBOL to
assume that the file is VB, when actually you do not know the true DCB
attributes of the file. What you really need is

FD  INFILE01
        RECORD CONTAINS 0
        RECORDING MODE IS U.
 01  INFILE01-REC.
       05  INFILE01-REC-CHAR OCCURS 1 TO 32756.
             10  FILLER  PIC X.


The key here is RECORD CONTAINS 0 which forces attributes to be accepted
from either the execution JCL or the file itself  *and*  the RECORDING
MODE U clause which tells the compiler that the file might be fixed length
or variable length and that it might be blocked or unblocked. IOW - You
really don't know right now, but will tell the program at runtime.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mdsmu$qou@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com>`

```
Forcing the DCB with an override does work just fine:

   dcb=(lrecl=32756,blksize=32760,recfm=vb)

Cobol:

fd  infile
    label records are standard
    block contains 0 records
    record is varying from 1 to 32752 characters
    recording mode is v.
01  infile-record pic x(32752).


Created an allocation subroutine which does LOCATE/OBTAIN to determine
the dataset attributes and if 'VB' allocate the file with dcb above
using dynamic allocation SVC 99. 'FB' or 'VB' is passed back to the
Cobol program so it knows which FD to open.  This is because the one
program needs to process an unknown number of files which are read
from a listcat output during any single execution.

Thanks to all for helpful suggestions and input.
```

```
>Forcing the DCB with an override does work just fine:
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

I've been making myself nuts trying to get Cobol to _write_
variable-length records.  Or rather, to write code that will, in a
given execution, write records of length L, but in another execution
write records of length L + n.  So all the records written in a given
execution are the same length, but from execution to execution, based
on a parm, write records of different lengths.

Regardless of the ways I've coded the FD and the DCB it always writes
records of the max length specified by the OCCURS DEPENDING ON	
in the record ( the object of the ODO is not in the record, it's a WS
field).

Could the compiler be deciding on my behalf that it will write max
length records?

Anyone have suggestions for Cobol and/or JCL to accomplish this?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mgrmt$skv@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com> <7mdsmu$qou@chronicle.concentric.net> <378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu>`

```
If  you are using a "current" COBOL compiler (VS COBOL II or later) use the

   RECORD VARYING IN SIZE

phrase in your FD - and I think you will be happy with the results.  Do
*not* use the RECORD CONTAINS n TO m phrase.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990713224055.06753.00004508@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu>`

```
In article <378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu>, pojpl@_ais.ucla.edu (Jim Lewis)
writes:

>Cityboy@Concentric.Net wrote:
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>

From what I see above the compiler only has one definiton within the FD from
which to determine the LRECL and as such forces everything to the max size.
It looks like you would need to have a different 01 level for each record
length to write -or- add the DEPENDING ON <some-field> to the FD and set this
field to the size you want written.  I usually have an 01 level that has the
record defined as PIC X OCCURS 1 to 32752 depending on ws-reclen, this allows
writing any size governed by an external parameter or runtime calculated size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378C8684.66F25B38@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com> <7mdsmu$qou@chronicle.concentric.net> <378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu>`

```
Jim Lewis wrote:
> 
> I've been making myself nuts trying to get Cobol to _write_
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone have suggestions for Cobol and/or JCL to accomplish this?

FD .....
	
01  out-rec.
     05 bytes   pic x(01)
                occurs 1 to 255 times
                depending on outrec-len.


move 25 to outrec-len

write out-rec   ** writes 25 byte records **

Ken

PS:  Remember to set the outrec-len before any moves or you will
be sorry, I was very confused.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** pojpl@_ais.ucla.edu (Jim Lewis)

```
pojpl@_ais.ucla.edu (Jim Lewis) wrote:

>pojpl@_ais.ucla.edu (Jim Lewis) wrote:
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…

Well, I just goofed this one, and I'm sorry to have taken peoples'
time with this.
 
I finallly thought to dump the output file (using IDCAMS Print) and
see just what resulted.  The dumped records ARE variable-length, at
least as far as IDCAMS is concerned, and I'm prepared to believe it.

I have been misinterpreting what I have been seeing when I viewed the
output file in SPF, and what the meaning is of the system-assigned
LRECL of the output file.

The LRECL was always the max possible length of the variable-length
record.  I interpreted that to mean that the actual length of all the
records was the maximum possible length.  Not so.  It appears to allow
for JES (I suppose) to allocate buffers of the maximum possible length
to support records that may be that length.

SPF Edit and Browse display the records in a non-wrapping format, also
apparently allowing for the display on one line of the maximum
possible length record.  I was expecting to see the records wrap
around.  This may be a setting in SPF, I don't know yet.

Anyway,   thanks to all who tried to open my eyes.  I really learned
something with this one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ml7lk$qu6@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com> <7mdsmu$qou@chronicle.concentric.net> <378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu> <378df262.999062@news.ucla.edu>`

```
Please show us your latest FD's.

If you use

  RECORD VARYING IS SIZE Depending on ws-field

in the FD (not OCCURS Depending on *under* the FD), then it absolutely
positively MOST% write out a variable length field - of exactly the length
specified in your Working-Storage field "ws-field".

You don't need a RECORDING MODE statement or anything else to get this to
work.
```

```
Thank you Bill.  I'm embarrased to admit that the problem was with me
and not with the program.  Until this morning I had not done the
obvious and dumped the output file to see what was there.  I was
looking at the system-assigned LRECL and seeing it was always the
maximum record size, and looking at the file with SPF and with FileAid
and seeing the padding that seemed to be filling in the record out to
the maximum length.

Actually, the program WAS writing variable-length records, as I could
see when I dumped the file.  I had not clearly thought though the
meaning of the LRECL, and had been looking for the records to 
"wrap-around" in the SPF editor.  Just fuzzy thinking on my part.
Thank you for taking your time to help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fKsj3.4836$Gx.51353@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com> <7mdsmu$qou@chronicle.concentric.net> <378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu> <378df262.999062@news.ucla.edu> <7ml7lk$qu6@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <378e4bde.23907296@news.ucla.edu>`

```
I ran into this same problem not to long ago.

The two ways you can write VB records is to use the FD clause "RECORDS
CONTAINS 1 TO X CHARACTERS" or to not specify record length at all and use 2
different 01's under the FD. Both would have to be specified VB in the JCL.

When viewed in ISPF EDIT (ISREDIT) there should be a warning when the file
is opened. Saying something, not sure what the warning was. When the file is
saved by a PF3 or a SAVE command the rightmost empty bytes, greater than the
actual record length, will be padded with something, again not sure, but I
think it was low-values.

VB files are better viewed with FILEAID or in BROWSE/VIEW which won't change
the record length if the file is saved. Except VIEW which can't save to the
current file with just a SAVE or PF3. A CUT/PASTE or REPLACE command
sequence are the only ways I know of to cause this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mlmuf$4l1@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`

```

Jeff <a@a.com> wrote in message news:fKsj3.4836$Gx.51353@storm.twcol.com...
> I ran into this same problem not to long ago.
>
> The two ways you can write VB records is to use the FD clause "RECORDS
> CONTAINS 1 TO X CHARACTERS" or to not specify record length at all and use
2
> different 01's under the FD. Both would have to be specified VB in the
JCL.
>

Just because it has been almost 2 hours since I last said this <vbG>,

Please, PLEASE, do not use RECORD CONTAINS n TO m CHARACTERS when trying to
create variable length files.

Instead use the RECORD VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING ON phrase which *gurantees*
variable length files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378E99F5.FA281458@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com> <7mdsmu$qou@chronicle.concentric.net> <378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu> <378df262.999062@news.ucla.edu> <7ml7lk$qu6@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <378e4bde.23907296@news.ucla.edu> <fKsj3.4836$Gx.51353@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:
> 
> When viewed in ISPF EDIT (ISREDIT) there should be a warning when the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

ISPF lesson 1:  variable length records :-)

To check a variable length record use 'HEX ON'.  Where the hex
numbers run out so does your record.

ISPF lesson 2:  variable length records :-)

If you have a record padded at the back with spaces and you edit
it (and actually save it).  You delete trailing spaces.  The can
cause some interesting results when you end with a binary field
that happens to be X'40' or the next program uses a record code
and assumes the record is that long.  For example if your end with
PIC x(40) which has 'happy birthday' you drop the last 20 odd
bytes when you edit it.  The next program to run will see the next
record in sequence as part of the current record (whatever happens
to be in the buffer past the end of record)  the output can be
*very* interesting.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

```
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

Just to clarify, you don't actually need this in MVS just having
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dreaded File Status 39 <- End Result

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mm69k$qv8@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7m6jlp$ql6@journal.concentric.net> <3789E35E.B062BCCC@njeb.att.com> <7mdsmu$qou@chronicle.concentric.net> <378bb8c1.114780015@news.ucla.edu> <378df262.999062@news.ucla.edu> <7ml7lk$qu6@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <378E988D.8B720C80@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:378E988D.8B720C80@zip.com.au...
> William M. Klein wrote:
> >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

True today and probably true tomorrow - but not necessarily in the future.
(See FS=39 for "changes" in how IBM mainframes handle FDs versus DCB versus
file-labels).  The ANSI/ISO Standard (on the other hand) really does
GUARANTEE variable format files if you use RECORD VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING
ON - while NO other method "guarantees" variable format.
```

<a id="t-c7862e4bf2"></a>
## Thread: Print Control Character — 21 msgs

### Print Control Character

- **From:** Sam kingston <samkingston@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com>`

```
Hi all,

          I am a new learner of Cobol.
         Could anyone tell me what's the answer for the following
question?

         1) Print  Control Character
             When I generate a report with 133 characters long for each
record, the first byte is a print control       character.

              I declare something like
                 FD REPORT1
                        .
                        .
                        .
                 01 report-record
                      05 rpt pic x(132).

             Is this correct?


             Do I need to declare a print control character in the FD,
             eg,
                   FD report1
                            .
                            .
                            .
                    01 report-record
                         05 print-ctl pic x(01)
                         05 rpt          pic x(132)

              If so, could anyone tell me how to use the print control
character to control write statement
              I.E, how to rewrite the following statement?

                                 write report-record from ws-variable
after 2.


                 2) File status

            I know File status 00 means normal.
            What's the other possible code of file status?
            Do I need to check file status after I write each record?
           eg.
                  write file-record.
                  if file-status = '00'
                     continue
                  else
                     perfrom abend
                  end-if


       3) End-of file

            perform a10-process
            until file-status == '00'

            Is this the standard way? Do I need to check other code than
'00'?

       4) Centralise write

           d10-write1 section.
           d10-010.
                  write file-record from ws-var1
           d10-exit.
                  exit.

           d20-write1 section.
           d20-010.
                  write file-record from ws-var2
           d20-exit.
                  exit.

           d30-write1 section.
           d30-010.
                  write file-record from ws-var3
           d30-exit.
                  exit.

           The above three sectioin is perform under different
situations.
           If I want to avoid write statement everywhere in my program,
I should have one write section in my program.

Eg.

           ws-buffer pic x(133).

           d10-write1 section.
           d10-010.
                  move ws-var1 to ws-buffer.
                 perfrom w-rpt.
           d10-exit.
                  exit.

           d20-write1 section.
           d20-010.
                 move ws-var2  to ws-buffer.
                 perfrom w-rpt.
           d20-exit.
                  exit.

           d30-write1 section.
           d30-010.
                   move ws-var1 to ws-buffer.
                  perfrom w-rpt.
          d30-exit.
                  exit.

            w-rpt section.
            w-rpt-10.
                  write report-record from ws-buffer.
                  if file not (status = '00')
                     perform abend
                 end-if.
            ws-rpt-20.
                 exit.

            Is this correct?

Thank you in advance!
Sam
```

#### ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m6ds2$s19@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com>`

```
(Different people will probably answer different parts of this note)

A) If at all possible, when posting to comp.lang.cobol - use "Plain Text"
and NOT HTML (even if you can't get "pretty" red lines that way).

B) My guess is that you are running on an IBM mainframe.  The following
assumes that is true - if it isn't, then tell us what compiler and operating
system (and printers) you are using.

If you use the ADV compile option, then it is correct that you ONLY define
132 characters in your FD - and COBOL "puts" in the other byte.  You still
need to define your DCB for your output file as LRECL=133 *and* RECFM=FBA
(or FA or FBM or FM) - something to put that "control" character into.

If you use the NOADV compiler option, then you need to define 133 characters
under your FD and you have to "fill in" that 1st character yourself (and not
use the AFTER ADVANCING stuff).

C) Check your programming guide and/or LRM for a full list of file status
codes.  There are "lots" of useful information that you might want to get
from them.  (Again, if you are on an IBM mainframe, there is even a 2nd
status code field for VSAM files with even MORE information in it.)

Some programmers check each possible file status code - others use "AT END"
or "INVALID KEY" phrases still others (fairly rare) use DECLARATIVES.  There
are PROs and CONs for each (and a "religious war" can occur when you try to
convince someone to switch from one to the other).  However, I think that
most people will agree that you should NOT "mix" the various techniques.
OTOH, some people use a "phrase" to catch something other than "00" and then
do detail checking of various serious and semi-serious problems reflected by
other values.

D) Lots (most?) programmers do try to "centralise" their file I/O - and then
perform such routines (either a PARAGRAPH or a SECTION - but that is another
"religious war").  In older compilers this (sometimes) had a negative
performance impact - but often made program maintenance much easier and less
error-prone.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3788B3B2.4C23CE1F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com> <7m6ds2$s19@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> If you use the ADV compile option, then it is correct that you ONLY
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not quite true...

If you do not use advancing at all on a file then the ADV command
has no effect, if you define 133 then it writes 133.  The effect
only kicks in when you use the advancing commands (and it knows
about it).

If you use NOADV then you are (effectively) required to define 133
and the first byte is reserved.  You can put it in but if you
combine with advancing then the values you put in are lost. 
Probably good advice by Bill but not required as implied.

Adding to the discussion:

1  = skip to channel 1 (always top of page except sometimes)
+  = don't skip any lines (overstrike, bold, underscore)
' '= skip a single line (normal single line feed)
0  = skip two lines then print
-  = skip three lines then print.

When you look at it don't be surprised that your blank lines just
do not appear.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:3788B3B2.4C23CE1F@zip.com.au...
> William M. Klein wrote:
> >
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> do not appear.

Where I work there's a heck of a lot of code that still uses the
"WRITE...AFTER POSITIONING" stuff.  In many cases it's a major pain to
convert to "...AFTER ADVANCING".   Does this mean we can simply take out the
AFTER POSITIONING part and, as long as the control character is in the
correct place it will work?  If so, this would certainly make things a heck
of a lot easier when converting to COBOL II.

(BTW, we're a VSE shop, if that makes a difference.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7meclm$7jb@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com> <7m6ds2$s19@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3788B3B2.4C23CE1F@zip.com.au> <7me9nv$tms$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The short answer is (probably) NO -

The longer answer is that the VS COBOL II Migration Guide for VSE is "pretty
skimpy" about what to with "WRITE AFTER POSITIONING" statements (from DOS/VS
COBOL).  However, the newer (OS/390) COBOL Migration Guide seems like it
SHOULD help - even for VSE people.

I would suggest that you look on the web at:

   http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYMG201/4%2e1%2e7

and do a search for,
  "after positioning"

It gives you information on how to do it manually OR you can use the CCCA
(which is available on VSE now) to do it for you.
```

```
Anyway, I checked out that web page.  It doesn't really tell me anything I
didn't know.  It's still a pain, especially with some of our homegrown
programs that have writes inline all over the place.  We have some purchased
programs by one company where they use multiple performs of a paragraph that
contains the write, so there's generally only one place we have to make a
change.  That's fairly simply.  But our homegrown ones...argh!  I have a

Unfortunately we never purchased CCCA.  From what I've heard from people who
```

```
something like that) for their VisualAge COBOL (on the PC).  It *includes* a
variation on the CCCA product that will do these conversions for you.  I
have checked and it is still targeted at the OS/390 user - not the VSE
user - but IBM has "many happy customers" doing DOS/VS COBOL conversions
using it.

Let me know if you need additional information on where this product is
documented.
```

```
>> 1  = skip to channel 1 (always top of page except sometimes)

If I remember my VSE days, you would still need an "A" in the RECFM=
attribute that wouldn't get overridden by the program.  I recall seeing a
program which printed an already created printer dataset in which the WRITE
.. AFTER POSITIONING referred to the first byte of the print line.  Sure
enough, the assembler code the compiler produced moved that byte to itself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378D279B.BAB12B42@att.net>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com> <7m6ds2$s19@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3788B3B2.4C23CE1F@zip.com.au> <7me9nv$tms$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <I%Gi3.223$Wa1.18463@axe.netdoor.com> <7mgn97$31r$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <4W3j3.353$Wa1.24826@axe.netdoor.com>`

```
Warren Porter wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> >I don't know what RECFM even means.  I've seen reference to it here, having
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> //DNAME DD DSN=&&TEMP,DCB=(LRECL=133,BLKSIZE=1330,RECFM=FBA)

No longer a subfield (along with the other keyword parameters), so you
can code:

//FILE  DD  DSN=&&FILE,
//	RECFM=FB,
//	LRECL=133,
//	BLKSIZE=0 ...

BTW,

RECFM	Record format
LRECL	Logical record length
BLKSIZE	Blocksize

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> B) My guess is that you are running on an IBM mainframe.  The following
> assumes that is true - if it isn't, then tell us what compiler and operating
> system (and printers) you are using.

I've converted print files from 133 in IBM mainframe COBOL to
environments which don't show this character.  Usually this is trivial,
but sometimes the code will play with that character.  This is a good
argument for leaving that character alone.  (When I started programming,
we always played with that character).


> Some programmers check each possible file status code - others use "AT END"
> or "INVALID KEY" phrases still others (fairly rare) use DECLARATIVES.  There
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> other values.

The only time I have seen declaratives used for this purpose was to
catch everything not caught by the AT-ENDs.  They were mixed.


> D) Lots (most?) programmers do try to "centralise" their file I/O - and then
> perform such routines (either a PARAGRAPH or a SECTION - but that is another
> "religious war").  In older compilers this (sometimes) had a negative
> performance impact - but often made program maintenance much easier and less
> error-prone.

That has been standard some places for me, but I have never been able to
see the real difference between having PERFORM PRINT-ROUTINEs spread out
around the program, or having the print code itself spread around the
program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378B3E2B.C7B30351@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com> <7m6ds2$s19@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3789F6CB.432BD9C5@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> That has been standard some places for me, but I have never been able
> to see the real difference between having PERFORM PRINT-ROUTINEs
> spread out around the program, or having the print code itself spread
> around the program.

Note quite the same thought but...

I have written a format routine that output's multiple print
files.  Each print file has a header of x lines (including sub
header) and data of x lines.  You then call the 'print' routine
with a bunch of lines and it guarantees that they will be kept
together.  It also flicks from ANSI to ASI by run time option. 
(switching from ASI to PC cobol would be a snap just replace the
module, switching to Windows calls same).

The paging logic is in one module and is totally debugged not
spread amongst many programs and it is dead easy to group lines
(which is invariably buggy or inefficient code when redone).  It
works particularly well when the groups are non standard sizes. 
New page is forced by setting the skip count to large number.

It enforces standard report titles (eg program name left corner,
descriptive in middle, date, time and page number on right) by
just being dead easy to use.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378B448C.31F9187C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com> <7m6ds2$s19@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <3789F6CB.432BD9C5@NOSPAMhome.com> <378B3E2B.C7B30351@zip.com.au>`

```
We have called programs which are used to print like that, and others
which handle VSAM access.  Report Writer type features which plug into
Crystal Reports would be nice too.  But as you say, that isn't the same
as structuring a program to make it have one point of outputing a report
(when there are many points which access that point).

Ken Foskey wrote:
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3786BAAB.2636D0B5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com>`

```
Sam kingston wrote:
> 
>                  2) File status
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I'll defer to others about your print questions. Want my advice. ALWAYS,
ALWAYS check file-status on any file VERB. My experience is that most,
not all, file-status errors are found in developing your programs. Try
this - don't open a file and then try read next record etc...from that
file. You can get real frustrated when the program doesn't give you what
you want.

File status errors - your manual will contain a complete list of ANSI
file-status codes. Should you be using MicroFocus then they have
extended status codes which give you more detailed descriptions.

	if file-status <> "00"
           perform ( or invoke in OO ) my-file-error-routine
> 
>        3) End-of file
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
You can write this many ways, but a sort of 'traditional' approach is :-

01 my-fileEnd			pic 9.		pic x.
   88 FileNotFinished	 	value 0.	value "N".
   88 FileFinished		value 1.	value "Y".

	set FileNotFinished to true
	read CustomerFile next record
          at end set FileFinished to true

	Perform until FileFinished
	  if record satisfies conditions
	     then printit, calculateit, rewriteit etc...
	  End-if

          if file-status = "00"
             read CustomerFile next record
               at end set FileFinished to true
	  End-if

	End-Perform

Want specific help on printer control-codes; let the News Group know 
Name & Version of your Compiler, which Platform (Win98 etc..).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3787475A.389A0705@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com> <3786BAAB.2636D0B5@home.com>`

```
My general rule is either have a status code for a file and test it on
ALL i-o statements in the program or have no status code.  For non-VSAM
files (not-indexed and not relative) the current shop standard is to
have no status code for the file and accept the default system processes
(abnormal termination on error).     

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services - cmorris@fox.nstn.ca
    on assignment in New Brunswick, Canada - morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Sam kingston wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3789F830.169D6797@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com> <3786BAAB.2636D0B5@home.com> <3787475A.389A0705@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> My general rule is either have a status code for a file and test it on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (abnormal termination on error).

I generally do this too in the absence of standards.  I check for
abnormal I-O returns when there's something I should do which is
preferable to the standard abort.  Usually, this would involve
displaying a message before dividing by zero to make sure we get an
abort anyway!   With database errors, I always do this, but it's easier
for the operators to recognize and fix a file error with the standard
abort.
```

#### ↳ Re: Print Control Character

- **From:** "Rich Holgate" <rlh2d@cstone.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<931681095.368069@helium.cstone.net>`
- **References:** `<3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com>`

```
I assume your on a mainframe (ref to 133). Your second FD is right. The first byte of a report tells the system when to skip to top of page; advance 1, 2, 3, .... lines; or over print. It is a translation of the 'BEFORE or AFTER' clause of the WRITE statement for the report line.

If you use SDSF to view listings on the hold queue you can see these characters if you shift left (PF10) after bringing it up to view.  Also, in a IBM MVS setting you can modify the JCL DD line for the report from SYSOUT=<> to a output disk file and appropriate clauses with a DCB=(RECFM=FBA,LRECL=133,BLOCKSIZE=0). When browse it in ISPF. Then print it with an oldie but moldy IEBGENER. All of 4 lines of JCL.
                                    Hope it helps    Rich  
  Sam kingston <samkingston@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:3786A452.E88306@hotmail.com...
  Hi all, 
            I am a new learner of Cobol. 
           Could anyone tell me what's the answer for the following question? 

           1) Print  Control Character 
               When I generate a report with 133 characters long for each record, the first byte is a print control       character. 

                I declare something like 
                   FD REPORT1 
                          . 
                          . 
                          . 
                   01 report-record 
                        05 rpt pic x(132). 

               Is this correct? 

   
               Do I need to declare a print control character in the FD, 
               eg, 
                     FD report1 
                              . 
                              . 
                              . 
                      01 report-record 
                           05 print-ctl pic x(01) 
                           05 rpt          pic x(132) 

                If so, could anyone tell me how to use the print control character to control write statement 
                I.E, how to rewrite the following statement? 

                                   write report-record from ws-variable after 2. 
    

                   2) File status 

              I know File status 00 means normal. 
              What's the other possible code of file status? 
              Do I need to check file status after I write each record? 
             eg. 
                    write file-record. 
                    if file-status = '00' 
                       continue 
                    else 
                       perfrom abend 
                    end-if 

              
         3) End-of file 
    
              perform a10-process 
              until file-status == '00' 
    
              Is this the standard way? Do I need to check other code than '00'? 

         4) Centralise write 
    
             d10-write1 section. 
             d10-010. 
                    write file-record from ws-var1 
             d10-exit. 
                    exit. 

             d20-write1 section. 
             d20-010. 
                    write file-record from ws-var2 
             d20-exit. 
                    exit. 

             d30-write1 section. 
             d30-010. 
                    write file-record from ws-var3 
             d30-exit. 
                    exit. 
    
             The above three sectioin is perform under different situations. 
             If I want to avoid write statement everywhere in my program, I should have one write section in my program. 

  Eg. 
    
             ws-buffer pic x(133). 

             d10-write1 section. 
             d10-010. 
                    move ws-var1 to ws-buffer. 
                   perfrom w-rpt. 
             d10-exit. 
                    exit. 

             d20-write1 section. 
             d20-010. 
                   move ws-var2  to ws-buffer. 
                   perfrom w-rpt. 
             d20-exit. 
                    exit. 

             d30-write1 section. 
             d30-010. 
                     move ws-var1 to ws-buffer. 
                    perfrom w-rpt. 
            d30-exit. 
                    exit. 

              w-rpt section. 
              w-rpt-10. 
                    write report-record from ws-buffer. 
                    if file not (status = '00') 
                       perform abend 
                   end-if. 
              ws-rpt-20. 
                   exit. 

              Is this correct? 
    
  Thank you in advance! 
  Sam
```

## Thread: WHERE SHOULD I START? — 2 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [1999-06](1999-06.md#t-dd75abd226)._

#### ↳ Re: WHERE SHOULD I START?

- **From:** coboljit@aol.com (COBOLJIT)
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990709131954.27703.00004197@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990627010434.25275.00004107@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days is the best selling COBOL text book, used
across the world for training COBOL Programmers. This will get you off to a
good start as it has a well structured approach that takes a new programmer
step by step through learning the basics of COBOL. 

You'll need a compiler. Some of the other COBOL books on the market have
compilers included with them - some are crippled or have time outs on them.

The third edition of Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days will have a compiler
included. Or you can get the Student edition of the MicroFocus Personal COBOL
Compiler for $50.00.

Sometimes new programmers have trouble locating the errors in their code, and
if they do not have anyone helping them, this can be an insurmountable barrier,
so the disk of code for Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days and a File comparison
program ( to compare the students code to the lesson code and show you where
the errors are) can be useful until the eye becomes accustomed to seeing the
errors.

If you need a more structured approach, the COBOL, Just in Time course is very
useful.  See Http://members.aol.com/COBOLJIT.

All these are available from KCSI. 818-951-5240.  If you want to call me I'd be
happy to discuss. 
Helen

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
King Computer Services, Inc.
COBOL Training for the Year 2000
"COBOL, Just in Time" Crash Course
"COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000" Technical Reference.
818-951-5240
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WHERE SHOULD I START?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378976dc.15619907@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990627010434.25275.00004107@ng-cm1.aol.com> <19990709131954.27703.00004197@ngol08.aol.com>`

```
On 09 Jul 1999 17:19:54 GMT, coboljit@aol.com (COBOLJIT) wrote:

>Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days is the best selling COBOL text book, used
>across the world for training COBOL Programmers. This will get you off to a
>good start as it has a well structured approach that takes a new programmer
>step by step through learning the basics of COBOL. 
>

How can I resist? <G>  On the AMAZON.COM site, my book TY COBOL in 24
Hours does rank lower than 21 days, but on barnesandnoble.com - if you
click computer books, go on to programming, then on to COBOL you will
find that the number ONE book is mine.  I'm a very proud papa.  So
far, I have tons of "Thank You" you made things so clear notes.  I'm
very very pleased to have contributed.

>You'll need a compiler. Some of the other COBOL books on the market have
>compilers included with them - some are crippled or have time outs on them.

Just for general information, the compiler that comes with my book is
the full Fujitsu 3.0 starter kit - non expiring, and non crippled.
Obviously later releases of the compiler have more features, but the
full Ansi 85 COBOL is in there.  Following the tutorial that my book
is will get you up and running, from the START using modern,
structured, current COBOL techniques.


Not to take any credit away from the 21 days book - heck it's MY
publisher that publishes the book.  I jusst wanted to take this chance
to say how happy and proud I am of my efforts, and I appluad Mo
Budlong for attempting to keep his book current and contemporary.
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Working on Mainframe COBOL environments for as long as I have, I expect
jobs to last (with maintenance) for some time.

But in our new integrated environment, I keep finding people using
applications on MS-DOS, Windows 3.1, dBASE III, etc, which are necessary
for people's business needs, but which die unexpectedly without any way
of being fixed.

The claims made by OO advocates are unconvincing to me.  Are we moving
towards an age of disposable IS?  We throw away TVs instead of repairing
them, and I see people throwing away IS functions rather than repairing
them.

Any discussion?
```

#### ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m5fkc$17n$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Working on Mainframe COBOL environments for as long as I have, I expect
>jobs to last (with maintenance) for some time.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>them.

You are definitely correct in your description of the volatile nature of
MS-DOS and Windows 3.1, I have little experience with dBase III, so I won't
comment that one. However, what you should remember of MS-DOS and Windows
3.1 is that both is created for a processor architecture which were almost
outdated when it was released. The software ran away from MS-DOS a long long
time ago. Trying to accomplish tasks that the underlying hardware never was
intended to solve.

Another issue is that the intel platform is a low cost platform, users there
cannot afford to pay billions of dollars like mainframe users. As a result
of this, the quality follows.

I do however not see the connection to OO (I presume you mean Object
Orientation). Although both platforms may host OO based applications, there
is no connection otherwise.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m74st$dt1$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
There are times when it is best to throw away applications
without replacement, times to replace, and times to repair.
This has always been true; but it is becoming easier to
replace the applications with new technology than to repair
the old technology. Furthermore, the new technology may
provide additional features that may be used to enhance
the application.

There are three broad classes of PC-based business
applications: specialized, customized, and personalized.

A specialized application may have been new and very
cost effective for the business; but the original supplier
may have left the business leaving the application area
to others. Replacing the application makes sense.
Alternatively, the application may no longer be cost
effective. Disposal might be more appropriate.

A customized application may have been innovative for
its time; but now a few suppliers may have created
specialized applications that satisfy the general
requirements for the system. Replacement with the
specialized application should be considered.

The technology needed to create a system with
commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) software may not have
been available; so a customized system was built. The
technology is now available in COTS. Replacement
should be considered.

A personalized application may have been developed
by a department mangager so that she might better
manage the department. After she is promoted, the
new manager may be unable to use the application
because his view of the business is different from hers.
Thowing away the old system should be considered.
The new manager may then decide what, if any, new
systems should be put into place.

What makes replacement better than repair for some
PC-based applications is cost. Where appropriate, it is
far less expensive to use COTS technology on PCs than
to write and maintain custom software on any computer.
This also means that, in some cases, it is more cost
effective to do complete replacement with new technology
than to repair old technology, on PCs. Furthermore, the
relatively small investment in an existing PC-based system
has less effect on the decision to dispose of a system.
It is easier to throw out a $2000 system than a $2,000,000
system; even though disposal may be the best choice for
both.

Perhaps you could clarify your statement about OO, I don't
see the connection to disposable IS.
------------------
Rick Smith

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Working on Mainframe COBOL environments for as long as I have, I expect
>jobs to last (with maintenance) for some time.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any discussion?
```

#### ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378978d1.16121611@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Fri, 09 Jul 1999 12:08:02 -0600, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>But in our new integrated environment, I keep finding people using
>applications on MS-DOS, Windows 3.1, dBASE III, etc, which are necessary
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sure.  There is already a backlash (not always well directed, but then
the backlash never is) where companies are being told.  One Platform,
One Vendor, One solution, One Language.  We all know how silly this
is, but it is happening.

I see several trends:

1.  Take Out IT - let someone else handle the details you give me the
end results.

2.  Dine IN IT - You make it in the kitchen, I'll come and eat when I
want.

3.  I'm a gourmet and I'll make my own system - works until the
gourmet goes somewhere else and doesn't leave the recipie.

4.  BBQ - Where we are now.  Take it out back and burn it.  Anything
left edible is consumed.

I could go on and on and on.  I look forward to the conversation.
```

### Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m5gk6$qpv@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`

```
This is going to sound totally IRRELEVANT - but I have a "vague memory" of
an IBM APAR a while ago ...

Add a

    DISPLAY  "USELESS"

statement just before your sort statement.

At some stage the LE (maybe VS COBOL II) run-time was having problems with
doing a dynamic allocate of the "display" output file - which caused totally
"strange" results with SORTs.  If (by any chance), this does "solve" your
problem (get rid of the S0C4 - and gets you "useful"  SORT-OUT messages),
then check with IBM for the APAR information to correct their dynamic
allocation problem.
```

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37866792.D1F1F1D9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <931525296.905964@helium.cstone.net>`

```
Please explain the advantage your method has over the sort procedures we
are already comfortable with.

Also:
1.  What advantage is there in PERFORM THRU over PERFORM SECTION in this
instance?  To me they are equally dangerous.  Ever since I moved to a
COBOL which allowed SORTs to use paragraphs, I use neither option.
2.  I don't have the choice of compiler options once my applications
leave test.  Endeavor will compile with our standard options.  Do you
still recommend your method without the FASTSRT option?  What does this
option do?
3.  I had no idea you could have SORT USING code without having either a
RETURN or a RELEASE.  I assumed that the compiler would choke.

But my most important question is:  Why?  Why replace RETURN with READ
and RELEASE with WRITE?  You're talking about a fair amount of work to
replace something all COBOL programmers know with an unfamiliar
technique.  I infer there must be some kind of advantage in doing so. 
Does having the SORT loaded for the whole program use up THAT much
resources?  If the program is that big, I usually break it up into
multiple steps (with a stand alone sort).

Rich Holgate wrote:
> 
> The vendor is right. INPUT/OUTPUT SORT procedures were glorious things 15 or
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rich Holgate
```

<a id="t-079a5c82fb"></a>
## Thread: Elementary 0C4 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3786B9E9.1B5972D2@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <931525296.905964@helium.cstone.net>`

```
Gotta Disagree.  With the change Rich suggests, you are doing
double I/O.

Current: 1 read.
  COBOL reads record to memory
  SORT retrieves record from memory
  SORT places sorted record in memory

Proposed: 3 reads + 2 writes.
  COBOL reads records to memory
  COBOL writes records to file
  SORT reads records from file
  SORT writes sorted records to File
  COBOL reads sorted record to memory

Now that I count the extra reads and writes, it more than double!

As for "roll the application and sort program in and out", not
true.  These two programs exists concurrently in memory.  They are
effectively co-routines.  The MVS mechanisms for doing "call
attach" and COBOL SORT are the same.  Nothing new there.

Rich Holgate wrote:
> 
> The vendor is right. INPUT/OUTPUT SORT procedures were glorious things 15 or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> level gets added if there are any application calls in either procedure.
{snip}
```

---

<a id="t-95a0d4acc1"></a>
## Thread: Newbie Question: Indexed I-O File Status Error 43 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Newbie Question: Indexed I-O File Status Error 43

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3786090B.92818FCC@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<375AB8B4.A3C67AC5@intranet.ca> <3782a32a.21506278@news1.frb.gov>`

```
The Micro Focus NetExpress help file says, about 43:

43	Files in sequential access mode. The last I/O statement executed for
the file, before the execution of a DELETE or REWRITE statement, was not
a READ statement.

Mind the word SEQUENTIAL in this text!! Are your opening the file
sequential and did you not do a read. (F.e. a rewrite immediately after
the open)?

Hope this sets you on the right track to a solution.

The Frog
```

```
The E35 Routine is the COBOL SORT EXIT ROUTINE (Sort passes control to the
E35 Routine
AFTER it returns each record in the final phase.

MOST LIKELY - YOU FAILED to retrieve ALL the SORTED Records
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mdqgv$4mv@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7lfkt7$q1j$1@endevour.netg.se> <377BE098.51CC@compaq.com> <377CBF37.D1AF156A@NOSPAMhome.com> <377d352a.8529028@netnews> <#VOZzkRx#GA.306@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <377E06FA.666993E6@zip.com.au> <3782036B.9DA4E214@NOSPAMhome.com> <37834C0E.4BDB193@zip.com.au> <378356A2.D5CFB608@NOSPAMhome.com> <3783f0d3.90480471@news1.ibm.net> <3783FCB7.26967643@nbnet.nb.ca> <eD0MbZxy#GA.356@cpmsnbbsa05> <378A43D2.38CAD94B@IMN.nl>`

```

The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote in message
news:378A43D2.38CAD94B@IMN.nl...
> "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" wrote:
> >
> > Isn't there a Not on size error clause to provide the else to the on
size
> > error clause, just like Not at end?
>
> Yes, for every "on/at xxx" there is a "not on/at xxx"
>
>

Just to be "obscure",

Check out the CALL verb.

There is a
    "NOT ON EXCEPTION" phrase

but there is NO
    "NOT ON OVERFLOW" phrase

(Hence, ON OVERFLOW is going into the archaic or obsolete category in the
NEXT Standard).

I have heard the "historical" reasons for this, but it is just a bit of
trivia for your amusement.
```

```
Randall Bart <Barticus@att.spam.net> wrote in message
  <much snippage>
>
> No, if there's an ON OVERFLOW clause, you can't have [NOT] ON EXCEPTION.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

Check out item 4 in the "Archaic Language" list, page 815 of CD 1.5.
  (In case I confused anyone,  "ON OVERFLOW" is being made "archaic" in the
*next* Standard.  It is "fully" supported in the '85 Standard.)

P.S.  I will send a note to J4 telling them that it should get a "note"
added under the General Format - the same way that "NEXT SENTENCE" has one
under the IF statement.
```

### Looking for COBOL CGI Sample Programs

- **From:** "Mike Gerrie" <mgerrie@interlog.com>
