- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m933g$8dg$1@news.interlog.com>`

```
Does anyone have some sample COBOL CGI programs, for either Micro Focus or
Fujitsu. Documentation on this subject is very sketchy, at least the little
bit I have found to date.

Thanks.
Mike
mgerrie@interlog.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL CGI Sample Programs

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WL7i3.1598$M46.18089@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7m933g$8dg$1@news.interlog.com>`

```
Mike, this is a re-post. hope it helps.

MERANT extended ACCEPT/DISPLAY syntax to handle CGI. they also implemented
an EXEC HTML construct which is also very useful. both are very COBOL-like.

Gazaloo
gaz at home dot com

======

here's a simple example i have for an older MERANT compiler (Visual Object
COBOL). the example should work just fine with the current product (Net
Express).

a nice/slick alternative to using external-forms to output a page is to
simply embed the HTML into your program. in this case the 01 output-page
item is not needed and instead of the 'display output-page', you have:

exec html
    copy "output.htm"
end exec

well, something like that anyway. it's been a while. you can of course just
enter HTML directly; you don't have to use copybooks. anyway, pretty simple,
COBOL/SQL-like implementation.

==>start of cobolcgi source
identification division.

working-storage section.
01 input-page is external-form.
   03  name-field pic x(30) identified by "Name".
   03  phone-no   pic x(30) identified by "Phone".
   03  email      pic x(30) identified by "Email".

01 output-page is external-form identified by "output".
   03  name-field pic x(30) identified by "Name".
   03  phone-no   pic x(30) identified by "Phone".
   03  email      pic x(30) identified by "Email".

procedure division.

    accept input-page
    move corresponding input-page to output-page
    display output-page
    .
==>end of cobolcgi source

==>start of input.htm
<HTML>
<HEAD>
<TITLE>Sample Form for VISOC</TITLE>
</HEAD>
<BODY>

<P>Visual Object COBOL Input Form

<FORM METHOD="POST" ACTION="/cgi-bin/cobolcgi.exe"></P>
<PRE>
Name     <INPUT TYPE="TEXT" NAME="Name" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="30" >
Email id <INPUT TYPE="TEXT "NAME="Email" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="30" >
Phone    <INPUT NAME="Phone" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="12">
<INPUT TYPE="SUBMIT" VALUE="Send Form"><INPUT TYPE="RESET">
</PRE>
</FORM>

</BODY>
</HTML>
==>end of input.htm

==>start of output.htm
<HTML>

<P>*** SAMPLE OUTPUT FORM ***</P>
<P>Dear :name-field</P>
<P>blah blah blah</P>

</HTML>
==>end of output.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL CGI Sample Programs

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378A51A1.66D60692@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7m933g$8dg$1@news.interlog.com>`

```
I just brougth a job to acceptance testing stage: A full fledged web
(intranet) app written in COBOL + HTML and some JavaScript. All I needed
to know came from the Merant/Micro Focus docs that come with their
NetExpress product and from experience. The basic needs are:

* HTML and JavaScript knowledge.
  Having some years of script/GML experience, this is learnable in two
weeks.
* COBOL
  ... what need I say
* Accept groupItem from HTMLCOBOLdefinition
  Generated code and datastructures, using NetExpress' HTML editor.
* Exec HTML / End-Exec
* Understanding of pseudo-conversational programming like under CICS.

Within 2 months I had set up a working environment, including library
management (Visual Sourcesafe) for multi-user development, some
skeletons for HTML-pre- and post-page processing-programs and a state
maintaining mechanism.
Within two more months an app with 5 logical screens was written, using
frames, navigation via hyperlinks etc. etc. This app with separate I/O
modules.
This all accesses data via an application server coupling CICS on NT
with CICS in mainframe. The data comes from various databases (IDMS,
DB/2) on the main frame and from some SQL/ODBC (Access and SQL/server).
The 5th month was to cook it together, install the NetExpress RTS on the
web server and copy the app (CGI-exes, some statis HTML and GIFs, some
JavaScript and a bunch of DLL's) to it.

Lots of sample code with NetExpress too. Get their CD, its free to use
for 30 days, thereafter the IDE won't work, but the demo's stay on
harddisk.

NetExpress turned out buggy (for web-app development), but giving great
tools. Version 3 recommended!

Proud Frog

Mike Gerrie wrote:
> 
> Does anyone have some sample COBOL CGI programs, for either Micro Focus or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> mgerrie@interlog.com
```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3786CB0C.82CBAFA5@home.com...

> Well, I don't work in Dallas but Calgary is the Canadian oil/gas

There's a lot of gas in Dallas, but no oil (J.W. Ewing was an
urban legend). Dallas has banking, insurance, a six-mile
light rail line, buxom cheerleaders,  is the home of Governor
George W. Bush (or, as we call him, "Governor Bush")
and Southwest Airlines.

Ft. Worth has cattle (and the gas they produce) and the state's
only liberal columnist (Molly Ivins) (and the gas she produces).

Halfway between Dallas & Ft. Worth is the stadium where the
aforementioned cheerleaders hang out.

Houston is big in petrochemicals (exploration, production,
refining), space, medicine, and foreign trade. Houston used to
have a football team, might have light rail someday, and is
the largest city in the world with no zoning.
```

<a id="t-6121632ce2"></a>
## Thread: Elementary 0C4 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** vjcer@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m7u7p$9n9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <3785FC46.A984A462@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I also had a problem like this when our shop was
upgrading to a new compiler and sort release.
What was happening to my newly compiled program
was that sort was "falling through". The cause was
a missing "SECTION" on the OUTPUT-PROC.
Make sure your OUTPUT-PROC reads:

OUTPUT-PROC SECTION.
and not just
OUTPUT-PROC.

Just a thought.

In article <3785FC46.A984A462@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> I remember a case like this where the problem
was caused by faulty
> drop-through logic.  Double check to make sure
the program doesn't drop
> through into INPUT-PROC or OUTPUT-PROC by
mistake.  Until recently,
> COBOL sort procedures had to be sections.  I
always put in place holder
> sections followed by a REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM
SECTION, to avoid
> paragraph/section confusion by future
maintenance programmers.
>
> R Teutsch wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > MOVE lh 2,124(0,13) next instruction to
execute
> >
> > Sort msgs indicate the SORT completed albeit
with an invalid RC in the E35
> > exit. Sort vendor
> > claims it isn't vendor code because IGZESMG is
an IBM routine and he is
> > outta there,
> > given the sort msgs.
> >
> > So, if the sort completed and you are just
about to do the MOVE of the
> > SORT-RETURN
> > code (which is zero in the TGT), how would you
proceed to debug this?
> >
> > IE, please suggest a debugging
hornbook/methodology/class/training wheels.
>



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-a9a15f8256"></a>
## Thread: Elementary 0C4 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990710120912.26266.00006414@ng-cd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`

```

The phrase 
"Abend 0C4 in IGZESMG"
is not consistent with notes to 
the effect
<<
    l 15,496(0,2)     v(igzesmg)
    la 1,3191(0,10)
    balr 14,15
MOVE lh 2,124(0,13) next instruction to execute
>>

If the load half-word (lh) is the next instruction to execute, then the Branch
and Link on Register (balr 14,15) did not work. That means you did not get into
IGZESMG.
So you are not abending 'in' that module.

The module address load instruction
    l 15,496(0,2)     v(igzesmg)
failed. That would be for environmental reasons. Since we are looking at IGX
modules, this is COBOL II, and the environment can be extremely tricky. but the
solution is numbingly simple.

In your test or training environment either
1) systems programming needs to place the COBOL runlibs onto the linklist (if
you are a newbie, don't ask why, just ask if so), or
2) it may be your shop standard to get the COBOL runlib into your execution via
a JOBLIB DD statement, or 3)  it may be your shop standard to get the COBOL
runlib into your execution via a STEPLIB DD statement, in the program execute
step.

If you are a newbie, just ask for an example of a working execution JCL model
(of course, I am assuming this is in batch, just switch all the DD
recommendations  to ALLOC commands if in TSO). 

If an uninformed person has twiddled with the concatenation sequence of the
JOBLIB or STEPLIB statement, do your best to get back to the original
cannonical sequence that systems programming provides. ((look for overrides of
the nth STEPLIB DD in PROC invocations for example)).

The modern COBOLs pay attention to LINKLIST, JOBLIB and STEPLIB concatenation
sequences only temprorarily. They load the intial part of the execution
environment, but are then are on their own for resolution of the vector of
COBOL service routines.

Divide 496 by 4, get 124: The instruction
    l 15,496(0,2)     v(igzesmg)
is trying to get the 124th address off of a vector of addresses (a list of
addresses).
This is really involved.  But really you just need a working example of proper
JCL (or CLIST), and stick to it.

Hope that helps,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

Quote :-

The Jargon Dictionary : Terms : The C Terms : COBOL

COBOL

COBOL /koh'bol/ /n./ [COmmon Business-Oriented Language] (Synonymous
with evil.) A
weak, verbose, and flabby language used by card wallopers to do boring
mindless things on
dinosaur mainframes. Hackers believe that all COBOL programmers are
suits or code grinders,
and no self-respecting hacker will ever admit to having learned the
language. Its very name is
seldom uttered without ritual expressions of disgust or horror. One
popular one is Edsger W.
Dijkstra's famous observation that "The use of COBOL cripples the mind;
its teaching should,
therefore, be regarded as a criminal offense." (from "Selected Writings
on Computing: A Personal
Perspective") See also fear and loathing, software rot. 

End Quote :

Yeah, Yeah. We all know about Dijkstra. He's the guy that spoke only
Double-Dutch - took people like Dan McCracken to translate his thoughts
into English and give us Structured COBOL Programming.

My millenium calendar of events, included in the Calgary Herald - shows
that this is the day Lady Jane Grey started her 10-day reign; then she
finished up in the Bloody Tower. Judging from your web address - that's
where you should be matey !

Jimmy, Calgary AB

<a id="t-da9e67b4ae"></a>
## Thread: Elementary 0C4 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37879611.1790228C@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`

```


R Teutsch wrote:
> Code
> SORT SORT-WORK ASCENDING KEY WORK-ITEM
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> given the sort msgs.
...

Surprised none of the other replies seems to have mentioned the obvious:

Barring some unusual inter-language interfacing problems, the most
common ways (without compiler or run-time library bugs) that an MVS
COBOL program can ever produce a S0C4 ABEND are:
(1)Table subscripts out of range; or
(2)Attempt to do input or output to a file that it not currently in OPEN
status (the address of the I/O access method routine is "null" at that
point).
(3)Use of static subroutine linking with an unresolved reference at
linkedit time (resulting in a run-time attempt to branch to 00000000).

Look for violations in these areas in your INPUT-PROC and OUTPUT-PROC,
although the code fragment you show is actually suggestive of (3), and
could suggest either a missing SYSLIB at program linkedit time or
possibly even some error in the way the SORT product was installed. 
```

## Thread: Comp-3 Fields — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Comp-3 Fields

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#4fs0Qxy#GA.60@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<7lth3k$l4k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
If it's not too late, the best way to convert data from one platform to
another is often to (using a utility if possible or writing a program if
necessary) convert all binary (including BCD) fields to display numeric
with sign leading separate on the From platform, transferring the resulting
character-data-only file to the To platform (with EBCDIC to ASCII
conversion as necessary) via a simple utility, and then converting the data
back to its original Cobol format on the To platform. That way the
differences in platform and compiler implementation become irrelevant. Or
it can easily be used to feed an SQL loader for tables.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7lfkt7$q1j$1@endevour.netg.se> <377BE098.51CC@compaq.com> <377CBF37.D1AF156A@NOSPAMhome.com> <377d352a.8529028@netnews> <#VOZzkRx#GA.306@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <377E06FA.666993E6@zip.com.au> <3782036B.9DA4E214@NOSPAMhome.com> <37834C0E.4BDB193@zip.com.au> <378356A2.D5CFB608@NOSPAMhome.com> <3783f0d3.90480471@news1.ibm.net> <3783FCB7.26967643@nbnet.nb.ca> <7m17rj$a1b@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <3784B3D4.8A10E00B@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Interesting point. Even inline performs can be used for this, although the
compiler might complain. You can also use them to provide scope termination
for old statements like ON that were dropped from newer Cobol, if you're
not on the mainframe where ON is now prohibited entirely you can make up
for the lack of an End-On statement by e.g. (using _ for leading blanks)

...
Perform
__On ...
____...
__Else
____..
End-Perform
...

IDMS is far from dead.  NEXT SENTENCE clauses work with multiple ANSI
compilers.  If they add END-IFs, then they would need to have more than
one pre-compiler.  Maybe they will do this, either way, my periods will
still be safe making sure that their expanded code is terminated before
I go on to my continued logic.

## Thread: Sharing a file across sub-modules — 1 msgs

### Re: Sharing a file across sub-modules

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fgaj3.10527$qw.61471@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<01bec757$66dd8bb0$2cef53a0@ws030318>`

```
Well, let's look at GLOBAL:
5.3.7 GLOBAL Clause

The GLOBAL clause specifies that a data-name is available to every program
contained within the program that declares it, as long as the contained
program does not itself have a declaration for that name.  All  data-names
subordinate to or condition-names or indexes associated with a global name
are global names.

A data-name is global if the GLOBAL clause is specified either in the data
description entry by which the
data-name is declared or in another entry to which that data description
entry is subordinate.  The GLOBAL clause can be specified in the
Working-Storage Section, the File Section, the Linkage Section, and the
Local-Storage Section, but only in data description entries whose
level-number is 01.

In the same Data Division, the data description entries for any two data
items for which the same data-name is specified must not include the GLOBAL
clause.

A statement in a program contained directly or indirectly within a program
which describes a global name can  reference that name without describing it
again.

Two programs in a run unit can reference common data in the following
circumstances:

1.  The data content of an external data record can be referenced from any
program provided that program   has described that data record.

2.  If a program is contained within another program, both programs can
refer to data possessing the global   attribute either in the containing
program or in any program that directly or indirectly contains the
containing  program.


AND     E X T E R N A L

5.2.2 EXTERNAL Clause

The EXTERNAL clause specifies that a file connector is external, and permits
communication between two  programs by the sharing of files.  A file
connector is external if the storage associated with that file is
associated with the run unit rather than with any particular program within
the run unit.  An external file can
be referenced by any program in the run unit that describes the file.
References to an external file from
different programs using separate descriptions of the file are always to the
same file.  In a run unit, there is only one representative of an external
file.

In the File Section, the EXTERNAL clause can only be specified in file
description entries.

The records appearing in the file description entry need not have the same
name in corresponding external  file description entries.  In addition, the
number of such records need not be the same in corresponding file
description entries.

Use of the EXTERNAL clause does not imply that the associated file-name is a
global name.  See the IBM COBOL Programming Guide for your platform for
specific information on the use of the EXTERNAL clause.


This should answer your questions, and this works the same on Honeywell,
Wang, IBM (all)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and Delphi

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mdhk7$neb$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37891066.B605728F@globalnet.co.uk> <7mbvb3$77h$1@news.cerf.net> <378A368D.23E07A9E@globalnet.co.uk>`

```
Stu Talbot wrote in message <378A368D.23E07A9E@globalnet.co.uk>...
>Nothing in particular, just thought I might use Delphi instead of a
>screen section for user input and pass the data to a cobol "database"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>items ?

Remember this is viewed from Acucobol <> Delphi, I do not know Fujitsu.

First of all, make your form into a .DLL, Delphi contains reasonable good
support on how to do that, just enter the help and index for DLL.

Make a frontend procedure of displaying your form which specifies the data
items you want.

Let's assume MyDLL.DLL and MyProc(String1, String2);

Acucobol does not have NULL terminated strings, so what I have used to do is
to make sure that I fill the strings (PIC X(xx)) with low-values, such that
the entire trailing area is filled with ascii 0.
Like:
    STRING "Whatever" LOW-VALUES DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO my-var.

Inside the Cobol you will then:

Loadlibrary MyDLL.DLL (In Acucobol this is done by: CALL "MyDLL.DLL")

Set your data

CALL MyProc USING
    BY REFERENCE string1,
    BY REFERENCE string2.

CANCEL "MyDLL.DLL".

If you want your data returned from the DLL, you may use StrPCpy to the
variable from Cobol.
Remember a few things:

    - In 32 bits windows, function calls are case sensitive
    - For numeric items, it is necessary to send BY VALUE
    - Be aware that Delphi by default does cdecl  parameter convention. You
may however alter this
      if Fujitsu does not.

Let me know if this just made you more confused or gave you a path to go.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: Elementary 0C4 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** "R Teutsch" <teutschr@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m9ue1$m90@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The programming changes offered (and I do appreciate the effort of those
suggestions) reside outside my opportunities to implement them.

This is a "production" system from 1986 ("no subsequent maintenance"). Hmm,
well a little from the Y2K remediation team.

The "correction" takes the following path. The original programmer had coded
an output procedure (SECTION) which looks essentially like

s1 section.
    if more then perform lookatit.
s1-exit.
    exit.
lookatit.
    yadayada.
    at end more = y.
    perform other-stuff.

In effect, lookatit (and everything it performs) executes twice each time
the sort output procedure is entered because the s1-exit statement in some
senses is a NOP. It doesn't terminate the section.

Changing lookatit to a section name solves the problem in a COBOL II
environment. The maintenance programmer changed all the performed paragraphs
related to the output procedure to section names successfully.

Here's the subtle part.

Based on the indirect comment from Howard Brazee (thank you) "Until
recently, COBOL sort procedures had to be sections. " a simple recompilation
with Cobol for MVS may solve the problem with no source code changes. I have
NOT tested this. The "recompilation" suggestion arises from reading
SC26-4769-01 page 389 because of Brazee's comment and with less directness
page 464 Gary DeWard Brown's Third edition ISBN 0-471-31481-1.

To the point, Cobol for MVS apparently removes the COBOL II restriction that
the output procedure must be a SECTION. This could be a drastic behavioral
change, perhaps limited to those programs which had been "miscoded" in the
first place.

Oops, tongue bite! Another note from William M. Klein "This does NOT make
sense to me..." So I went to
http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYL1101/3%2e14#HDREXI
T etc and found that the my comments about Cobol for MVS are incorrect, that
is, the usage did not change from Cobol II to Cobol for MVS. Apparently if
the original paragragh "S1 Section." had been defined as
"S1." instead of "S1 SECTION.", the problem might not have occurred or
perhaps we really needed
SORT ... output S1 thru S1-EXIT.

Put simply,  S1 should have been a paragraph name instead of a section name?
Is that all there is to it? Some other Cobol programmer's knowledge(from
1986)  was as obsolete as mine?

To return to the original post: IE, please suggest a debugging
hornbook/methodology/class/training wheels. Here's why. The discussion above
depends on access to the source code, perhaps the opportunity to fiddle
somewhat with the environment, the opportunity to make more error prone
changes to the environment, but the discussion does not suggest how I would
know what steps (from the dump) to avoid the spurious exercises of

Changing region sizes
Changing sort core size -- leave
Running the steplib concatenation check for endless discussions about the
RES/NORES, DYNAM/NODYNAM changes because of remediation team changes to
called subroutines
Changing MSGDD

For example.

The 0C4 shows the next instruction as being that MOVE statement, but the
SYSUDUMP indicates IGZESMG as part of the COBPACK is in control.

How do you know what a COBPACK is and what IGZESMG is and what OUGHT to be
in the TGT for the address of the IGZESMG workarea at what time?
How do you know this is not a relevant question?

For example, I took my call to the SORT vendor to be unsatisfying because
the technician denied any knowledge of the IGZESMG module name. As I
understand it, poorly, obviously, IGZESMG IS the Cobol SORT interface. So
why would the technician claim no knowledge of the module name? What, in the
dump,  would lead me to believe him? The vendor does not provide a WEB
interface to reference manuals in any form and to the best of my knowledge
the manuals do not exist in ANY Bookmanager or PDF form. I will order the
documentation, but, in this instance, at this time, I did not have a means
of perusing the documentation beyond QuickRef to suggest a means of
eliminating the SORT vendor path. What ever happened to PLMS?

In my calls to our abend monitor vendor, done because of the IGZESMG and the
fact of the abend, that product said the sort information areas were not
available. Does the address of zeros in the TGT entry for the IGZESMG
workarea (barring a storage overlay) let the SORT vendor and the ABEND
vendor off the hook so that I do not make those calls wasting their time and
mine.

How (thanks, William M. Klein, your memory isn't any worse than mine at
least because I have the same recollection) do you iterate through case
elimination that YES, SORTMSG SYSOUT can switch DCB characteristics from
COBOL for the DFSORT and SYNCSORT facilities (FB conflicts with VB in the
DCB) and arrange in each case to eliminate this possibility and WHEN do you
know to run through this exercise and how do you do the exercise?

The suggestions from RKRayhawk and Joel C. Ewing are marvelous, much
appreciated and respected, to some degree understood, wrong perhaps because
of the little information I provided, but neither suggests that I should
read through the COBOL II debugging manuals (nor that the locations of
information changed from one manual to another in the change from Cobol II
to Cobol for MVS). How would I know, from the dump, to eliminate their
suggestions out of hand?

#### ↳ Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7me4cr$peg@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7m9ue1$m90@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(all original stuff snipped - but I think I can "help" with a couple of
issues)

1) Let's look at what the REAL coding problem was (and why the program - as
it executed under OS/VS COBOL had a "bug")

If you have

   Sort XYZ
      Usign ABC
      Output Procedure   LMN
        .       <<<<< notice this period or full-stop
    Do something else

What this means is *supposed* to be the same in the old COBOL and the newer
COBOLs - however, (as pointed out before) in ANS'74 COBOL (e.g. OS/VS COBOL)
"LMN" was supposed to be a SECTION while in newer COBOLs (VS COBOL II, or
IBM COBOL for-this-and-that) it can be EITHER a section or a PARAGRAPH.

But the meaning is the same; it says -
   "Perform LMN" exactly once (no more, no loop, no until, no nothing else)"
Then come back and "do something else".

If LMN is coded as:

LMN Section.
   some code with or without a paragraph header
LMN-exit.
    Exit.
 Another-paragraph-header.
       More code
 ...  end of program

Then, the SORT Output Procedure has been told to PERFORM *everything* under
the LMN Section header.  Remember that LMN-Exit is a paragraph-header and is
NOT a section header that would "terminate" the LMN Section.

Therefore, if you change
   LMN-exit to
   LMN-Exit Section

       *OR*
you change
   LMN Section to
   LMN (without the word SECTION)

Your SORT output procedure will *only* perform the one paragraph or section
that you want it to.

  Is this clear what the original problem was - and WHY adding the word
"SECTION" solved the problem?

        ****

2) Sort ERROR MESSAGES -  where do they go and why can't I always find them?

  - As I indicated previously, there WAS a bug that IBM "fixed" (but that
your site may not have applied) that was stopping IBM from "putting" out
SORT messages (good, bad, or whatever) in certain cases.  This has an EASY
solution - get your shop to apply the current maintenance to your run-time
and you will CORRECTLY get the messages in some output file - for your
debugging purposes.

 - COBOL (VS COBOL II and later) have a compiler option called "OUTDD" which
allows you to specify which DDNAME you want COBOL "DISPLAY" output to go to.
If you use this compiler-option and set it to something LIKE

   OUTDD(MYMSGS)

then, you will get COBOL messages in "MYMSGS" and get OTHER products'
messages in the "traditional" SYSOUT DDNAME.  Some sites find this useful -
and others hate it.

Alternatively, LE has a run-time option (MSGFILE) which can ALSO allow you
to specify the name where LE messages go.

Finally, SyncSort (and possibly DFSORT) allow you to specify in a $ORTPARM
statement, a DDNAME for SORT messages.

Bottom-line:
   Everyone WANTS to tell you what is happening - but they all "default" to
something like SYSOUT while allowing you to "confuse yourself and your
operators" with various alternative DDNAMEs.  I *highly* recommend that your
shop establish a "standard" for who gets which DDNAME and try to enforce it
as much as possible.

    ***

Does this help???
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mfk99$ks8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7m4jdh$prr@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7m9ue1$m90@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <7me4cr$peg@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill Klein wrote:

> Finally, SyncSort (and possibly DFSORT) allow you
> to specify in a $ORTPARM statement, a DDNAME for >
> SORT messages.

Yes, DFSORT does allow this.  (DFSORT's equivalent of
$ORTPARM is DFSPARM, but you can change to using
$ORTPARM instead of DFSPARM with the PARMDDN=$ORTPARM
installation parameter.)

You can specify something like:

//DFSPARM DD *
   MSGDDN=MYOUT
/*
//MYOUT DD SYSOUT=*

In this case, your DFSORT messages will go to MYOUT.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Elementary 0C4

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990711211854.28954.00001629@ng-fm1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7m9ue1$m90@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
With the code you posted,it is probable that the output procedure has been
executing twice for each sort output record since the begining.

You posted the comment 
<<...because the s1-exit statement in some
senses is a NOP. It doesn't terminate the section.
>>

More particularly the EXIT verb in that paragraph is a NOP, always has been,
always will.  A thru clause as in a PERFORM THRU is an ALTER GO TO device,
sufficiently transparent to make EXIT look to many programmers like they do
something; never have, never do, never will.

In your new environment, your old duplicious program was caught .... it did not
start misbehaving recently, it has been strange all along.  

A talented (intentionally or accidentally) programmer can actually turn a tail
recursion into a loop, the sort RETURN function would not know the better of
it. The OUTPUT PROCEDURE can be a perform loop, a go to magnificence, or a
two-up staccato bumbling fallthru extravaganza. Whatever... you get one back at
a time from each  RETURN verb executed.

Unless, ...

Unless you munge up the registers that point to something important (like
service module vectors!). Then whatever your loop-dee-loop, it will stumble.

When doing two at a time via the time proven fallthru to optimistic re-RETURN,
there is a chance that code generated by new compilers did not leave registers
in the same condition as the old compiler generated code.

The instruction
    l 15,496(0,2)     v(igzesmg)
works on the normal iteration, but the second half fallthru probably has
strange value (by the time of the fallthru) in register 2. If you look real
hard and can not find a visible stomping of R2, then it is safe to assume that
igzesmg does it for you. And although igzesmg may be successfully instantiated
at run environment intialization, it is 496 off of the correct base value,
which is nolonger in register 2 upon fallthru.

Concerning your many important comments and questions about methods for
debugging this.  The answer is, and I mean this respectfully, all of the above.
By definition in a Charlie-Four situation, you do not even know where you are. 
All of the things that point to where things ought to be are suspect of having
been stepped on.

In an 0C4 situation, question everything, especially the source code. 

Many people think EXIT does something, never has, does not, never will.  A THRU
clasue alters a goto which invisibly resides after a paragraph (be it an 'EXIT'
paragraph or not.  The THRU clause is an ALTER GO TO. Dr. Grace Hopper
perfected these kind of techniques long ago because they did not have linkers
back then, and they did not work with a stack model of the machine. The
executable source code of COBOL is a trafic cop.

When you get an 0C4 in a new environment with an old program ... look at the
subscripts and look at,,,, you asked for it ... the THRU clauses or the absence
there of.  Old strangeness may make a difference in a new environment.

In the revealed code, register 2 is being used to manage the vector of  service
module addresses. It is sensitive, and it got clobbered. So don't clobber it.

As has been commented occasionally in this newsgroup, it can be believed that
mixing sections with paragraphs is inherently dangerous.  A section is kind of
like a pervasive THRU clause.  A way out is to make everything a section or to
make everything a paragraph. The implicit THRU-ness (pardon my koineage) of a
section is foreshortened by abutting it most agencently with another section.

So in da charly four things, check subscripts, check THRU clauses or lack there
of, and check for those fun mixtures of sections and paragraphs.

An 0C4 will teach you to question everything, most especially the integrity of
the register content.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL programmers

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378A9C5C.AB7A7375@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<7mcvah$184@nnrp4.farm.idt.net>`

```
Had a boss one who professed love for blind newspaper ads (only a
PO Box number given as the reply address).  He liked to just throw
away the resumes he didn't like; no need to acknowledge or decline
as politeness requires.  

We told him that most people wouldn't reply because they did not
know who would see their resume.  Ironically, a couple of weeks
latter we found out that a colleague had indeed replied to a blind
ad, and it turned out to be his/out employer.  Boy did he dance!

The rule about blind ads applies here to postings without rates. 
It is not in anyones's long term interest, or even some people's
short term interest, to reply to these things.

Charles Lee wrote:
> 
> Looking for COBOL Programmers in San Jose & Atlanta area.  If you live in
> the one of the mentioned area, please dorp me a line via return e-mail for
> more info.  Charles.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL programmers

- **From:** akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent)

- **References:** `<7mcvah$184@nnrp4.farm.idt.net> <378A9C5C.AB7A7375@techie.com>`

What I have done in the past is to write a somewhat frank EMail to the
poster, and told the individual in no uncertain terms that they MUST post
the rate range.  I emphasize this point, especially if they expect
responses from indiduals with a significant amount of experience.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL programmers

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<khJi3.135$Zq3.10104@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7mcvah$184@nnrp4.farm.idt.net> <378A9C5C.AB7A7375@techie.com> <akent-1207992043500001@o26.dialup.seanet.com>`

```
Excuse me, but where does anyone get off making "rules" in a public
newsgroup?

Ok, so if you are not making rules, where does anyone get off offering
unsolicited business advice to those who post ads?

If Mr Lee, or anyone for that matter,  wants to utilize a particular
advertising methodology, who is to say he should not?

And if, in one's opinion, the advertisement is not an effective one (i.e.,
one that does not excite the individual reader),or does not include data one
might like to read (e.g., geographic location or  rates), then don't answer
it. (I understand this concept may be difficult for some of the
technodweebs, but it can be mastered).

Fortunately, I have the advantage of having been a full-time IT consultant
less than five years.

"Advantage?" you say?

Yes! Because it allowed me to spend twenty years on the other side of the
desk, hiring people like, well, the collective you and me.

Trust me, the river looks different when viewed from opposite shores.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
Arthur M. Kent wrote in message ...
>What I have done in the past is to write a somewhat frank EMail to the
>poster, and told the individual in no uncertain terms that they MUST post
>the rate range.  I emphasize this point, especially if they expect

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mfvu6$rvo@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7mcvah$184@nnrp4.farm.idt.net> <378A9C5C.AB7A7375@techie.com> <akent-1207992043500001@o26.dialup.seanet.com> <khJi3.135$Zq3.10104@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
FYI,
  DD's "advice" is quite consistent with the statement in the COBOL FAQ
(which went thru quite a bit of "word-smithing" before it was finalized).
Clearly, DD (by your own logic) can express is own views whenever he wants
(and as often).   However, just so you understand what is "behind it" (or at
least is consistent with it), the following is the wording from the FAQ that
is posted to this NG every 15 days.

"18.2 Can I post job openings in the newsgroups and if so what should I
include?

These newsgroups are an excellent place for posting job openings. Some
participants (those with jobs) often wish that less of the bandwidth was
spent on job postings, but, for those who are looking for
positions/contracts, these postings are quite useful. There are, however,
some guidelines that you should follow when posting positions - unless you
like getting abusive and non-responsive replies to your postings.

Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or your best information on
what rates are available. Phrases like "based on experience" and
"competitive" are bound to receive replies questioning your motives. The
assumption is that ALL such posts are "trolling" for resumes or rates and
not serious job searches.

Try to give the best summary of job location and desired expertise in the
subject line of your posting. This will assist you in attracting readers
that are actually potentially interested in your openings.

The newsgroups are international in nature, but are dominated by Americans.
If you are posting a position outside the US, please make it clear whether
foreigners are or are not welcome to apply - and if so what visas and other
paperwork is required If you are posting for a job with specific citizenship
requirements, please make that clear from the start.

In general, the more information that you provide in your postings, the
better the match will be from those who reply. If, however, you are
"attaching" a job description, please make certain that you make it clear
what type of document-reader is required to process it AND make certain that
you have virus scanned the document before you post it.

NOTE: For those looking for jobs, you might want to check out Dice at

http://www.dice.com "
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XoQi3.602$Zq3.28819@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7mcvah$184@nnrp4.farm.idt.net> <378A9C5C.AB7A7375@techie.com> <akent-1207992043500001@o26.dialup.seanet.com> <khJi3.135$Zq3.10104@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7mfvu6$rvo@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I did receive and file the FAQ dated June 18, 1999; however, I admit that I
had not yet read through it until today.

Your post was quite useful, and I really do appreciate you taking the time
to make it. Consequently, I shall no longer rag on anyone who responds to
recruitment ads with comments about missing rates, provided their response
makes some reference to the FAQ and the URL where it may be located. (Which
would have been a most useful addition to some of the 'bot-like replies).
(By the way, pointing out a lack of geographic location in those replies
would have also been a nice addition for those inclined to comment on the
absence of rate information. That, too, is in the FAQ).

However, I shall rag on you, as you own the copyright on the FAQ.

Section 18.2 posits:
>Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or your best information
on
>what rates are available. Phrases like "based on experience" and
>"competitive" are bound to receive replies questioning your motives. The
>assumption is that ALL such posts are "trolling" for resumes or rates and
>not serious job searches.

So, my original argument stands: this "suggestion" presumes that the author
of 18.2 knows better than the recruiter;
that a recruiter would never, of his own volition,  "troll" for resumes or
rates; that the only possible "serious" search is that which is initiated
with rates and geography clearly specified; and that recruiters do not
expect responses which question their motives. These premises I consider
unrealistic.

That, or the wording should be redone to reflect your true meaning, or at
least redone so that a poor geek such as I could read the section without
misunderstanding.

Pray send me by e-mail your true intentions and I shall propose to you
less-confusing verbage.

Regards,
Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> So, my original argument stands: this "suggestion" presumes that the
author
> of 18.2 knows better than the recruiter;
> that a recruiter would never, of his own volition,  "troll" for resumes or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
  <some snippage>

(First, the COBOL FAQ is "out-of-date" and the online version is a little -
but not very - more "current" - so I am interested in "updates")

Would you be happier with the sentence,

"The assumption is that ALL such posts are "trolling" for resumes or rates
and not serious job searches"

    being changed to

"The assumption made by many newsgroup readers is that ALL such posts are
"trolling" for resumes or rates and not serious job searches"

Does this address your concern?

I should point out to all readers here that section 18.2 is under a general
section 18 which states,

"18. What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?

Both comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol are UN-moderated newsgroups.

This means that there is no controlling authority regulating the content of
the posts in those groups. Anyone is able (but most assuredly not welcome)
to post anything, relevant to COBOL or not. Off-topic posts (and
particularly prolonged off-topic threads)are discouraged, but it is
impossible to prevent them. Different readers of these newsgroups have
different thresholds for tolerating off-topic comments. The more that you
post, the more likely it is that some readers will simply apply a "killfile"
to all of your posts - often after sending you (and possibly the newsgroup)
rather pointed comments on your use/abuse of the newsgroup.

You should, therefore, apply the following guidelines that will help you get
the most out of both your viewing and postings at these sites.

 - Comp.lang.cobol is the preferred site to use - especially for technical
issues related to COBOL.

 - Although threads do stray from the topics, the more targeted your inquiry
is - and the more closely it relates to COBOL, the more likely you are to
receive a useful and prompt response. (If you know that you are leaving the
current subject, consider changing the subject line in your post.)

 - When asking about a specific structure, error message, or situation, it
is always best to specify both the compiler that you are using and the
operating system where you are working. (What you may think is a general
COBOL question may actually be very specific to your current environment.)

Note: For those who are interested, the "charter" for the comp.lang.cobol
newsgroup can be found at:

ftp//ftp.uu.net/usenet/news.announce.newgroups/comp/comp.lang.cobol"
```

## Thread: Help on MF PCW — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help on MF PCW

- **From:** "zenster" <zenster@zenxsite.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com>`

```
Somehow I can't seem to execute through this simple move.  This same program
passed with flying colors on my school's MVS mainframe.

It compiled fine on Personal Cobol yet gets jammed only at this move
statement at execution (runtime 163).  Here are some pertinent lines:

01  IN-REC.
      05 NUM-IN            PIC X(4).
      05 NAME-IN          PIC X(10).
      05                            PIC X.
      05 PREV-BAL-IN   PIC 9(3)V99.
      05                            PIC X.
      05 CHARGES-IN OCCURS 10 TIMES  PIC 9(5).

01  OUT-REC.
       05                           PIC X(2).
       05 NAME-OUT     PIC X(10).
       05                           PIC X(2).
       05 NUM-OUT       PIC X(4).
       05                           PIC X(2).
       05 TOT-CHRG-OUT     PIC $$$,$$$.99.
       05                           PIC X(2).
       05 PREV-BAL-OUT     PIC $$$$.99.
--------
100-BEGIN.
           INITIALIZE OUT-REC.
200-LOOP.
       MOVE NAME-IN TO NAME-OUT.
       MOVE NUM-IN TO NUM-OUT.
>err MOVE PREV-BAL-IN TO PREV-BAL-OUT.    >>>  ERROR!!!!!<<<

Pretty straight forward right?  There has to be something I'm missing.  If
someone has any insight I'd be much obliged.

thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LNvi3.617$TJ4.32841@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com>`

```
If PREV-BAL-IN is not initialized to ZERO or some other value, its contents
are undefined, and may result in the illegal MOVE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7me24e$fbu@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com> <LNvi3.617$TJ4.32841@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
You may want to add

INITIALIZE IN-REC

in addition to

INITIALIZE OUT-REC

(or you might need to "validate" the data in IN-REC if it is coming to you
in a file or other input medium)
```

```
dont used INITIALIZE ....
INITIALIZE command only applicable to PIC X and PIC 9
when you try to initialized a numeric-edited field or comp or comp-3..etc...
it will give you nothing in compile time, but it will gives you a
unpredictable
message in runtime.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mjhm5$5gh@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com> <7mjetg$mc$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Roque Bruno <roque69@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:7mjetg$mc$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net...
> dont used INITIALIZE ....
> INITIALIZE command only applicable to PIC X and PIC 9
> when you try to initialized a numeric-edited field or comp or
comp-3..etc...
> it will give you nothing in compile time, but it will gives you a
> unpredictable
> message in runtime.
>
>

I don't know what makes you say this - it isn't true.  If you use INITIALIZE
record-name (with no other phrases) it will initialize *all* numeric fields
to their "correct" zero values (with or without signs, with or without
packing, etc).

If you have a compiler that doesn't work that way - it has a BUG.

FYI,
   There are methods of using the INITIALIZE verb to get values other than
spaces or zeroes - but the simplest format of the verb is what people
USUALLY want.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uZUY7Tqz#GA.364@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com> <7mjetg$mc$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <7mjhm5$5gh@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
        I have found that the DOS 16bit MF compiler is hopelessly
naive with INITIALIZE.  Judging from the program size, it seems to generate
a move for each item in the group.  In addition, it can be slow when
running INT code, if the group contains a LOT of items.  I have had better
luck with a move spaces followed by initialize xxx replacing numeric by
zero.

    This generates move ONLY for numeric items.  If most of the
group are pic X, this can save a lot.  Of course, if the group is mostly
numeric, do not bother.



>FYI,
>   There are methods of using the INITIALIZE verb to get values other than
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mkpfs$nra@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com> <7mjetg$mc$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <7mjhm5$5gh@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <uZUY7Tqz#GA.364@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> wrote in message
news:uZUY7Tqz#GA.364@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com...
>         I have found that the DOS 16bit MF compiler is hopelessly
> naive with INITIALIZE.  Judging from the program size, it seems to
generate
> a move for each item in the group.  In addition, it can be slow when
> running INT code, if the group contains a LOT of items.  I have had better
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The 16-bit MF product is fairly old now. l think (but am not positive) that
more recent Micro Focus products *do* optimize the INITIALIZE verb.
However, this may well (again) be a case of comparing run-time performance
vs "maintainability".

In most (not all) cases, you use the INITIALIZE verb once per record (in the
entire program - not per loop).  Therefore, a little run-time performance
degradation is rarely significant in the entire program's run-time.  On the
other hand, having to do explicit MOVEs of ZERO to each of the numeric
sub-fields (elementary items) means that you need to REMEMBER to update this
when you add additional fields.   I am not saying that you shouldn't
consider the performance ramifications of this verb, but I do think that you
need to consider all the ramifications PRO and CON before dismissing it in
general.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

```

better

>> zero.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>In most (not all) cases, you use the INITIALIZE verb once per record (in
the
>entire program - not per loop).  Therefore, a little run-time performance
>degradation is rarely significant in the entire program's run-time.  On the
>other hand, having to do explicit MOVEs of ZERO to each of the numeric
>sub-fields (elementary items) means that you need to REMEMBER to update
this
>when you add additional fields.   I am not saying that you shouldn't
>consider the performance ramifications of this verb, but I do think that
you
>need to consider all the ramifications PRO and CON before dismissing it in
>general.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
    All true.  But when I complained about performance, I was referring to
an "int'd" program when initializing a "large" (will the gentlemen with the
14 Meg array stop laughing please) of 20,000-30,000 numbers in a multi
level array.  And the comment about having a move spaces for each pic x
item is of course a guess (although I could view generated assembler if I
wanted).

    Also many performance issues are much harder to notice now
that we have such fast pc's.  A code segment that took 1/2 second on a 33M
386
is not noticeable on a 400M pentium.

    I agree that doing a manual initialize is out of the guestion, unless
you get lucky
with a move low-values.  That can work with binary (MF COMP or COMP-X or
COMP-5)
data items.

    My orginal complaint concerned group items with a significant number of
pic x fields,
where the naive behavior of INITIALIZE was too glaring.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ml8mo$r9r@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com> <7mjetg$mc$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <7mjhm5$5gh@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <uZUY7Tqz#GA.364@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com> <7mkpfs$nra@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <eaao1tuz#GA.306@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
If you check out DejaNews (or whatever it is called this week), you will see
that over the last year or so (that I know of) there have been performance
discussions about INITIALIZE, STRING, UNSTRING, and almost every COBOL verb
more complicated than CONTINUE :-)

When members of the NG have tried, they have been able to "hand-code" more
efficient versions of EVERY individual task that they took on - on every
platform - with every compiler - when they tried.   In general (semi-gross
generalization), any programmer SHOULD be able to code a "task-specific"
piece of logic that is more efficient than a "generalized" COBOL verb.
HOWEVER, this still doesn't mean that the better performing piece of logic
is the "best" solution.  On the other hand,  cases like yours (where a
"simple" MOVE SPACES to record-name - followed by an INITIALIZE record-name
REPLACING NUMERIC by ZEROES) shows significant performance differences
*does* provide an excellent opportunity for a "paying customer" to ask the
vendor to "improve" their optimization.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help on MF PCW

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378DE18F.C392AD5@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<e1ti3.789$J71.41779@monger.newsread.com> <7mjetg$mc$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <7mjhm5$5gh@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> I don't know what makes you say this - it isn't true.  If you use INITIALIZE
> record-name (with no other phrases) it will initialize *all* numeric fields
> to their "correct" zero values (with or without signs, with or without
> packing, etc).

Unfortunately, it isn't obvious to somone not in the know what their
"correct" values are.  To me, ANSI defined this verb in a
less-than-useful manner.  Fortunately, in the future we will be able to
replace the naked INITIALIZE command with one more useful.
```

## Thread: Finally ready: a new course — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Finally ready: a new course

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990711235408.27928.00010710@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Well, I have finally finished the course I've been developing. Many people had
some great suggestions for a title. My favorite was "Hot Catholic High School
Girls in Bondage". 

But I guess I'll chicken out and go with a more conventional title:

Inter-Language Communication in OS/390

I'm trying to be sensitive to issues of bandwidth and promotion, so let me just
point to our website for folks who might be interested (go to
www.trainersfriend.com; follow the links from the home page to Topics -> LE ->
Inter-Language Communication in OS/390; this is a course description; at the
bottom of the page is a link to the course objectives; at the bottom of this
page is a link to a detailed topical outline).

At one point, someone expressed incredulity (sp?): "What? A course on Calling?
That's like having a course on breathing!" I thought I could whip out a two day
course in a couple of weeks. Instead, it's taken me nearly six months!

A quick teaser then I'll sign off: Assembler, COBOL, PL/I, C, argument list
construction (variable numbers of args, omitted args, pass by reference, pass
by value, functions), parameter list retrieval, working with null-terminated
strings, static calls, dynamic calls, alternate entry points, shared external
data, object formats (OBJ, XOBJ, GOFF), executables (load modules, program
objects, DLLs); coding DLLs for the mainframe.

Thanks for your time and forebearance.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Finally ready: a new course

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3789F190.DA6CBA0B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990711235408.27928.00010710@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
> At one point, someone expressed incredulity (sp?): "What? A course on Calling?
> That's like having a course on breathing!" I thought I could whip out a two day
> course in a couple of weeks. Instead, it's taken me nearly six months!

Good point.   I would love it if school kids learned to use slide
rules.  They wouldn't stand for it, but a slide rule is one of the best
tools for all the math that 95% of the  population ever needs to learn -
which most of those 95% do NOT learn:

1.  Proportion.  A is to B as C is to D...   Use this to calculate
mileage, etc.  Also used for non mathematical logic (boy is to man as
girl is to woman).
1a.  Inverse proportion.

2.  Estimation.  Since slide rules don't show the decimal points, slide
rule users have to have some idea what their answer should be.  With
this skill, people should develop the common sense to know when their
assumptions and calculations are wrong.  Even if the numbers come from a
computer!!

Here's a Java slide rule: 
http://www.comcen.com.au/~adavie/javaslide/javaslide.html

3.  Significant figures.  Look at a hole in the ground which is about 1
yard across.  It must be 3.1415926589 yards around, right?  Hey, pi to
one significant digit is 3.
```

- **References:** `<378A1056.8BAE51AE@home.com> <7md8tn$p0g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
mmaz250@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> In article <378A1056.8BAE51AE@home.com>,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mike M.

I'm now as happy as a pig in ....... I'm back into AnswerLab. Thanks
Bill Klein and the other MicroFocus folks who did it for me.

Mike - my saga, not sure which came first the chicken or the egg :-

	- got corrupted Response Key
	- fed-up with MSN/ATT&T Canada - 2 hour download to get happy
	  message "Hello there, you've been disconnected" switched to
	  a direct connection - moving from jjgavan@msn.com to 
	  jjgavan@home.com
	- Got message new thrity-day trial and Toronto gave me a
	  new response key. It failed, showed V2 whereas I'm
	  using V3. (Presumably something to do with the Registry)
	- So de-install, re-install and ask Toronto for new response
	  key which worked. Then download fixpacks.
	- Foolish me - using Netscape but thought I'd download latest
	  Intertnet Explorer - you're downloading from M/S then we 	
	  assume our M/S product is the one you're using as your 		  default
browser. Wiped out my homepage until I got into 		  properties and
restored Netscape
	- Now I got prompted by Answerlab with a blank screen - couldn't
	  remember password.

MicroFocus had re-acted to my "Helps!" but posted to @msn.com. I had
told Websync that it was now @home.com - but it would appear that
doesn't automatically filter through their database.

NO! Don't use pita.microfocus.com  You've heard of a firewall - it gives
you one, a pretty picture of red bricks. Got problems, Alan Wheeler
advised :-

> If you have future problems with the access to the AnswerLab, you can > use the AnswerLab@merant.com mail-to address on the Rules of Operation > page (the I Agree page) to contact us and let us know of the problems 
> that you are encountering.

Did you get that survey sheet; one question was "Is the Response Key a
nuisance ?" Surprise, surprise, I want to give the author an update to
my returned questionnaire.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-a49f9e0571"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus Cobol and Windows98 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Microfocus Cobol and Windows98

- **From:** lhauenstn@aol.com (LHauenstn)
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990712183616.08941.00005528@ng-fn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<uL4#og3x#GA.248@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
At present I'm working with a client who, among other software is using
MicroFocus COBOL.  Fortunately I'm not working with the product (I'm in the
PeopleSoft/SQR space at present).  However, the 2 folks working in MicroFocus
COBOL have been using NT as the platform.  They had no luck at all getting the
'beast' to run in Win 95 even, much less Win 98.

My experience in the past with MicroFocus COBOL is that you have to 'arm
wrestle it' to get it to do anything any other reasonable compiler can do.  And
the documentation, written by lawyers perhaps?  But what is it, only $1500 a
copy so I guess it's worth it, right!!

Gee, sorry I didn't mean to do that.  But it feels better.  Hey, I even like
COBOL, when it works.

Lee
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol and Windows98

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mdrci$s6e@dfw-ixnews19.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<uL4#og3x#GA.248@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <19990712183616.08941.00005528@ng-fn1.aol.com>`

```
What release of the Micro Focus COBOL product are they using?  There are
certainly issues with MF (support, documentation, cost, etc) - but I sure
have heard NO real problems with using their *current* products on any of
the current Windows variations (NT, 95, or 98).

P.S.  Just in case there is some confusion, I do *not* currently work for
them - but did at one time.
```

<a id="t-f4d0c03162"></a>
## Thread: Cobalt 74 - plumbs database — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: Cobalt 74 - plumbs database

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3788AF93.971C7E9E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7m3029$2pde@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
David Milliron wrote:
> 
> An agency has a plumbs database -- a Cobalt 74 application -- and
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> david@milliron.com

OK so it's mainframe.  Generally mainframe applications link the
output to a device or file using an external language like JCL in
MVS's case.

If they can proide a printout then they can modify the job
commands to put that data to a file and once they have the file
put in on a compatible device or FTP it to your server.  Sounds
like a lot of fun interpretting this stuff.

If they are MVS and have a report using SDSF try:

((preallocate a file named TEMP)

PRINT ODSN  TEMP * SHR
PRINT
PRINT CLOSE

(FTP temp to wherever)

Good luck
ken
```

directory on the NT Server.

Manny.

vendor for the inconvenience they cause ;-)

Newbury earlier in the year  to whine at some of the development team
directly. The topic of protection schemes was part of the discussion. I
don't think Merant will do away with licensing schemes, given their pricing
and international distribution issues, but they might make it possible to
exchange keys via the internet instead of by fax.

Keys & that fax form .......

I have got Merant to agree to request/response keys by e-mail, direct to
"the keeper of the keys". On my site they have (currently) 38 licenced
users, and on occassions we have machine swaps - hence revoke keys as well.
There was no way I was entering all that onto their cruddy fax form. They
seem quite happy doing this, and normally turn them around in an hour or so.

I guess you should get agreement first, so I won't post Matthew's (doh!)
e-mail address. Upset the keeper at your peril !!

Pete Jones
Birmingham - the real one, UK :-)

<a id="t-15b53cc141"></a>
## Thread: Printing Alphabetical Lists — 12 msgs

### Printing Alphabetical Lists

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com>`

```
I want to print a list of names alphabetically, 3 columns in landscape.
(8.5 x 11). The easiest way is to print :-

A.........	B...........	C.........
D........	E..........	F.........
G.........	H...........

But I would prefer to print :-

A..........	D.............	G..........
B..........	E.............	H..........
C.......... 	F.............

and of course, page-change after x maximum lines.

I can come up with all sorts of convoluted solutions - like reading the
file once to get a record-count, then re-reading and giving each record
a table position.

As I'm using OO I could create a SortedCollection (alpha sequence), then
invoke :- 

	01 ls-size		pic x(4) comp-5.

	invoke os-SortedCollection "size" returning ls-size

then perhaps use a callback to calculate each record's position in a
table having determined from ls-size the (a, b) positioning.

There's got to be a simple way. Any neat suggestions ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6HQi3.628$Zq3.30077@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com>`

```
Build a table = one page size. You don't really want to run across pages
(i.,e, the "A"s are in the left hand columns of pages one, two, and half of
page three; the "H"s start in the left column of page 22, and continue in
the middle column of page one, etc.), do you? Fill the page table before
printing anything, then print the whole page.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378C0821.4032A177@home.com>`
- **References:** `<378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com> <6HQi3.628$Zq3.30077@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Build a table = one page size. You don't really want to run across pages
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Thanks Michael,

I think you've given me the solution. Within Page x I want to "snake"
down column 1, then thru column 2 then thru column 3. I should have been
a bit more specific - I'll be reading an indexed file sequentially, no
alternate keys.

Combination of what OO can give me and what you suggest - read file
sequentially producing SortedCollection by name. Assuming page lines are
30, "snip" the SortedCollection at each increment of 90 and fill the
print-table as you suggest.

My thinking got blurred because I was concentrating on filling and
printing one line at a time.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

I don't know about your platform, however SYNSORT (also invoked in COBOL
sorts) allowed for 'alternate collating sequences' to be defined and used.
It didn't translate the data or anything, just the sorted order of the
output file.

                    Just a thought, hope it helps   Rich Holgate

#### ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e20j3.345$Wa1.23976@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com>...
>I want to print a list of names alphabetically, 3 columns in landscape.
>(8.5 x 11). The easiest way is to print :-
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>and of course, page-change after x maximum lines.


Build a table of size maximum lines * 3 to hold all the info you want to
print.  After the table is filled with sorted information, divide by number
of entries in the table by 3 (last page may not be filled) to determine the
max number of lines on that page.  In a perform of your subscript from 1 to
max lines, move your table entry (subscript) to your left print area, table
entry ( maxlines + subscript) to the middle column, and table entry(maxlines
* 2 + subscript) to the right column.

HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

I've done that, usually with more than one program.  If you only do a
page at a time (starting over with a new A on page 2), you can do a
table easily enough.  Redefine table (row, column) to (column, row).

#### ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

- **From:** "John R. Piggott" <Piggott@spc-systems.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378CC0DE.A6891E85@spc-systems.com>`
- **References:** `<378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

Are you running on a platform that supports the enhanced COBOL Report Writer
option?  If so, that should do what you want nicely.  Just GENERATE the
left-hand side and use SET COLUMN and SET LINE to do the middle and
right-hand big columns.  RW has the option to buffer the whole page till
you're ready to let
it print.

Another way is to read ahead one big column of lines and use the REPEATED 3
TIMES clause on your report group description, e.g.

(in Report Section)
  01  BIG-COLUMN  REPEATED 3 TIMES EVERY 40 COLS.
    03  LINE + 1   OCCURS 60 TIMES  DEPENDING ON W-COUNT  VARYING R-LNO.
      03  COL 1    PIC X(20)   SOURCE W-TABLE (R-LNO).

...
(in Proc Div)
     (read up to 60 records with count in W-COUNT)
     GENERATE BIG-COLUMN

I used an OCCURS DEPENDING ON to handles the unused lines at the end of the
last big column.  Unused big columns on the last page are automatically left
blank by the REPEATED clause.  I've missed out the Page Heading etc. from
the above scheme.

These features are described on www.spc-systems.com/refce/cobrwr.htm.


"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> I want to print a list of names alphabetically, 3 columns in landscape.
> (8.5 x 11). The easiest way is to print :-
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com> <378CC0DE.A6891E85@spc-systems.com>`

```
John R. Piggott wrote:
> 
> Jimmy,
> 
> Are you running on a platform that supports the enhanced COBOL Report Writer
> option?  If so, that should do what you want nicely... <snip>

Thanks John,

See reply to Michael. Never got around to pursuing R/W - From memory,
RM/COBOL V2 didn't have R/W, then used Microsoft COBOL - don't think
that had it. It was available in Microfocus DOS Version, then dropped in
Visoc and resurrected in NetExpress.  Currently using NetExpress.

With all this 'off-again', 'on-again' just didn't consider R/W. Read a
good detailed book on it years ago and I felt it was a very powerful
tool. Taking R/W in its current format - I suspect it's a bit of a
bummer to use with PCs where you want to select different printer-types,
landscape/portrait, print size, density etc.... (And probably its
"Sectionalising" doesn't fit well with OO, which I'm using).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Alphabetical Lists

- **From:** "John R. Piggott" <Piggott@spc-systems.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378DC2BB.E4BC387@spc-systems.com>`
- **References:** `<378BC9B3.E6F12C45@home.com> <378CC0DE.A6891E85@spc-systems.com> <378CCA5D.E7A744F8@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,
You'll be glad to know RW is required in the current draft Standard.  There's an
ATTRIBUTE clause in it that should handle those "special effects".  Our add-on RW
allows you to select the printer in the SELECT statement by coding e.g. TYPE IS
HPLASERJET.  STYLE PORTRAIT or STYLE LANDSCAPE at the RD level, STYLE 12PT, STYLE
10PT at the line level, and STYLE font-name at any level are available.  RW should
mix ok with OO, at least the standard says so.  John.

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> John R. Piggott wrote:
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: MF COBOL-Error Codes — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL-Error Codes

- **From:** "Steven Jones" <Steven11@tesco.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mesnv$6go$1@barcode.tesco.net>`

```
I am a COBOL student and have not been taught how to build in IF statements
that deal with File open modes etc

Do I run the program and then copy the error code in to the IF statement
e.g. IF Print-File = 00 Then

Can anybody point me in the right direction

Thanks

Steve J
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL-Error Codes

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378b379a.25492246@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7mesnv$6go$1@barcode.tesco.net>`

```
On Tue, 13 Jul 1999 09:08:46 +0100, "Steven Jones"
<Steven11@tesco.net> wrote:

>I am a COBOL student and have not been taught how to build in IF statements
>that deal with File open modes etc
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's one way, but you should enclose the tested item in quotes:  

ie:  If Print-File-Status = "00"
.....

One way I prefer is a set of 88 levels on the file status statement.

01  File-Status  Pic XX.
      88  File-Success Value "00" "02" "05".
      88  End-0f-File    Value "10" "14".

etc etc...
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7mesnv$6go$1@barcode.tesco.net> <378b379a.25492246@news1.ibm.net>`

```

> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> etc etc...

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Calculations — 101 msgs

### Calculations

- **From:** "Arkitektur Brabrand, Danske Data A/S" <arkb@ddb.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bece01$79a072c0$4d9af9c3@internet>`

```
(this message is best read using COURIER)
I'm currently running through some course material I have been writing, and
I
have trieda few statements to see if I got the result I expected.
Just for the record I'm using IBM VS COBOL II Release 4.0 09/15/92  on
OS/390
with allrelevant CSD's incl. the LE/390 stuff.

On the multiply statement I tried the following program:

 id division.
 program-id. iub00ci0.
 environment division.
 configuration section.
 special-names.
    decimal-point is comma.
* I'm danish thus comma as decimalpoint
 data division.
 working-storage section.

  01 show.
     05 var-01    pic +999999.
     05 var-02    pic +999999.
     05 var-03    pic +999999.

  01 calc.
     05 var-01    pic s999999 comp value 2.
     05 var-02    pic s999999 comp value 2.
     05 var-03    pic s999999 comp value 2.

 procedure division.
     multiply var-02 of calc by var-01 of calc
                                var-02 of calc
                                var-03 of calc

     move corresponding calc to show

     display 'var-01' var-01 of show
     display 'var-02' var-02 of show
     display 'var-03' var-03 of show
     goback.

I would expect that after the run to have seen:
 var-01  4
 var-02  4
 var-03  8
as the manual says:
                                     +---------------+
                                     v               !
      >>MULTIPLY-+- identifier-1 -+- BY --- identifier-2
-+----------------->
            !                !
            +- literal-1 ----+


 In format 1, the value of identifier-1 or literal-1 is multiplied by
the value of
 identifier-2; the product is then placed in identifier-2.

 For each successive occurence of identifier-2, the multiplication takes
place in the
 left-to-right order in which identifier-2 is specified.

but what I got was

 var-01  4
 var-02  4
 var-03  4

The options used was:
      ADV
      APOST
    NOAWO
      BUFSIZE(16384)
    NOCMPR2
    NOCOMPILE(S)
      DATA(31)
    NODBCS
    NODECK
    NODUMP
    NODYNAM
    NOEXIT
      FASTSRT
    NOFDUMP
      FLAG(I)
    NOFLAGMIG
    NOFLAGSAA
    NOFLAGSTD
      LANGUAGE(EN)
      LIB
      LINECOUNT(60)
    NOLIST
      MAP
    NONAME
    NONUMBER
     NUMPROC(NOPFD)
     OBJECT
     OFFSET
   NOOPTIMIZE
     OUTDD(SYSOUT)
     RENT
     RESIDENT
     SEQUENCE
     SIZE(MAX)
     SOURCE
     SPACE(1)
     SSRANGE
   NOTERM
     TEST
     TRUNC(BIN)
   NOVBREF
   NOWORD
     XREF(FULL)
     ZWB

Is it my reading of english and is this the expected way of behavior or
does other Cobol versions / vendors have other implementations????
```

#### ↳ Re: Calculations

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378CAA22.67B274C1@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<01bece01$79a072c0$4d9af9c3@internet>`

```
Arkitektur Brabrand, Danske Data A/S wrote:

> (this message is best read using COURIER)
> I'm currently running through some course material I have been writing, and
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
> does other Cobol versions / vendors have other implementations????

The reason for the answer is that on the 2nd multiply of var-02 of calc by
var-02 of calc the answer (4) was deposited in var-02 of calc therefore making
the result of the third multiply 4 X 2 = 8.  The result of the second
calculation changed your multiplier!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Calculations

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378ce519.3463110@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<01bece01$79a072c0$4d9af9c3@internet>`

```
Cobol uses temporary storage areas for intermediate results that are
to be stored or used in later calculations.  Thus your statement


    multiply var-02 of calc by var-01 of calc
                               var-02 of calc
                               var-03 of calc

is compiled into language like:

    MOVE var-02 of calc to temp
    MULTIPLY temp by var-01 of calc
    MULTIPLY temp by var-02 of calc
    MULTIPLY temp by var-03 of calc

I think this is done because the way the statement is coded, you are
really saying "Take the contents of var-02 an mulitply it into var-01,
var-02, var-03".

The results you were originally expecting (IE 4 4 8) would be
equivilent to 3 Seperate multiply statements:
   MULTIPLY var-02 by var-01
   MULTIPLY var-02 by var-02
   MULTIPLY var-02 by var-03
This would do the 4 4 8 result.  

Hope this makes some sense,

Paul

    
    
--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```
