<a id="t-00e478044b"></a>
## Thread: BtYacc 3.0 released — 1 msgs

### BtYacc 3.0 released

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378E46CB.9FC9426E@siber.com>`

```
New 3.0 version of BtYacc is released.

You can download it for free from 
http://www.siber.com/btyacc/ or
http://www.siber.org/btyacc.

The README file that explains what BtYacc is and
how version 3.0 differs from prior versions is enclosed.

We used BtYacc to write a working Cobol parser
that is now used in CobolTransfomer toolkit.

	     BTYACC -- backtracking yacc
	     ===========================

BTYACC was created by Chris Dodd using ideas from many
places and lots of code from the Berkeley Yacc
distribution, which is a public domain yacc clone put
together by the good folks at Berkeley.  This code is
distributed with NO WARRANTEE and is public domain. 
It is certain to contain bugs, which you should 
report to: chrisd@collins.com.

Vadim Maslov of Siber Systems <vadik@siber.com> 
considerably modified BTYACC to make it suitable 
for production environment.

Several people have suggested bug fixes that 
were incorporated into BtYacc.

See the README.BYACC files for more about 
Berkeley Yacc and other sources of info.

http://www.siber.com/btyacc/ is the current home of BtYacc.
It is provided courtesy of Siber Systems http://www.siber.com/.


		Version 3.0 changes
		-------------------
		  by Vadim Maslov

Changes mostly occurred in btyaccpa.ske file that 
contains the parsing shift/reduce/backtrack algorithm.

Version 3.0 innovations focus on:
- text position computation and propagation,
- industrial-strength error processing and recovery.


** Added mechanism for computing and propagating 
text position of tokens and non-terminals.

Compilers often need to build AST trees such that every node 
in a tree can relate to the parsed program source it came from.
The following applications are very likely to need this:
- debuggers that show actual source of the debugged program,
- source-to-source translators that want 
  unchanged parts of the tree to generate the unchanged code.

The new YYPOSN mechanism added in this version of BtYacc
helps you in automating the text position computation
and in assigning the computed text positions to the AST.
This mechanism is successfully used in commercial 
parsers and source-to-source translators.

In standard Yaccs every token and every non-terminal
has an YYSTYPE semantic value attached to it.
In this new version every token and every non-terminal
also has an YYPOSN text position attached to it.
YYPOSN is a user-defined type that can be anything and
that has a meaning of text position attached to
token or non-terminal.

In addition to semantic value stack BtYacc now maintains
text position stack. Behavior of the text position stack
is similar to the behavior of the semantic value stack.

If using text position mechanism, 
you need to define the following:

YYPOSN	Preprocessor variable that contains C/C++ type of 
	the text position attached to 
	every token and non-terminal.

yyposn  Global variable of type YYPOSN.
        The lexer must assign text position of 
	the returned token to yyposn, just like it assigns 
	semantic value of the returned token to yylval.

YYREDUCEPOSNFUNC
	Preprocessor variable that points to unction that 
	is called after the grammar rule reduction
	to reduce text positions located on the stack.
       
        This function is called by BtYacc to reduce text 
	positions. The function is called immediately after 
	the regular rule reduction occurs.
       
	The function has the following prototype:
	void ReducePosn(YYPOSN  &ret,
			YYPOSN  *terms, 
			YYSTYPE *term_vals,
			int      term_no, 
			int      stk_pos, 
			int      yychar,
			YYPOSN  &yyposn,
			UserType extra);

        The function arguments are:
        - ret         
	  Reference to the text position returned by 
          the rule. The function must write the computed 
          text position returned by the rule to ret.
          This is analogue of the $$ semantic value.

        - term_posns  
          Array of the right-hand side rule components 
	  YYPOSN text positions.  These are analogues of 
	  $1, $2, ..., $N in the text position world.

        - term_vals
	  Array of the right-hand side (RHS) rule components 
	  YYSTYPE values. These are the $1,...,$N themselves.

        - term_no
          Number of the components in RHS of the reduced rule.
          Equal to size of arrays term_posns and term_vals.
          Also equal to N in $1,...,$N in the reduced rule.

        - stk_pos 
          YYSTYPE/YYPOSN stack position before the reduction.

        - yychar
          Lookahead token that immediately follows 
 	  the reduced RHS components.

        - yyposn
          YYPOSN of the token that immediately follows 
	  the reduced RHS components.

        - extra
          User-defined extra argument passed to ReducePosn.
       
        Typically this function extracts text positions from 
	the right-hand side rule components and either 
	assigns them to the returned $$ structure/tree or
        if no $$ value is returned, puts them into 
	the ret text position from where
        it will be picked up by the later reduced rules.
       
YYREDUCEPOSNFUNCARG 
	Extra user-defined argument passed to 
	the ReducePosn function. This argument can use 
	any variables defined in btyaccpa.ske.


** Added code to btyaccpa.ske that automatically cleans up
semantic semantic values and text positions of tokens 
and non-terminals that are discarded and deleted as 
a result of error processing.

In the previous versions the discarded token and non-terminal
semantic values were not cleaned that caused quite severe
leaks.  The only way to fix it was to add garbage collection
to YYSTYPE class.

Now BtYacc skeleton calls delete functions for semantic
values and positions of the discarded tokens and
non-terminals.

You need to define the following functions that BtYacc 
calls when it needs to delete semantic value or text position.

YYDELETEVAL
	User-defined function that is called by BtYacc
	to delete semantic value of the token or non-terminal.
	
	The user-defined function must have the prototype:
	void DeleteYYval(YYSTYPE v, int type);
	v    is semantic value to delete,
	type is one of the following:
	0 	discarding token
	1       discarding state
	2       cleaning up stack when aborting

YYDELETEPOSN 
	User-defined function that is called by BtYacc
	to delete text position of the token or non-terminal.

	The user-defined function must have the prototype:
	void DeleteYYposn(YYPOSN p, int type);
	v    is semantic value to delete,
	type is one of the following:
	0 	discarding token
	1       discarding state
	2       cleaning up stack when aborting


** User can define "detailed" syntax error processing
function that reports an *exact* position of 
the token that caused the error.

If you define preprocessor variable YYERROR_DETAILED in 
your grammar then you need define the following 
error processing function:

void yyerror_detailed(char    *text, 
		      int      errt, 
		      YYSTYPE &errt_value,
		      YYPOSN  &errt_posn);

It receives the following arguments:
text		Error message.
errt		Code of the token that caused the error.
errt_value	Value of the token that caused the error.
errt_posn	Text position of token that caused error.


** Dropped compatibility with C.

Compatibility with C became increasingly difficult 
to maintain as new features were added to btyaccpa.ske.
So we dropped it. If anybody wants to make the new version
compatible with C, we would gladly accept the changes.

Meanwhile we expect that you use C++ to write grammar 
actions and everything else in grammar files.
Since C is (in a sense) subset of C++, your C-based 
grammar may work if you use C++ compiler to compile it.

		Version 3.0 bugs fixed
		----------------------

Matthias Meixner <meixner@mes.th-darmstadt.de> fixed a bug:
BtYacc does not correctly handle typenames, if one typename 
is a prefix of another one and if this type is used after 
the longer one. In this case BTYacc produces invalid code.


		Version 2.1 changes
		-------------------
		  by Vadim Maslov

** Added preprocessor statements to BtYacc that are similar 
in function and behavior to C/C++ preprocessor statements.

These statements are used to:

- Introduce modularity into a grammar by breaking it
  into several *.y files and assembling different
  grammars from the *.y modules using %include and %ifdef.

- Have several versions of the same grammar
  by using %ifdef and $endif.

- To include automatically generated grammar fragment.
  For instance, we use %include to include
  automatically generated list of tokens.

Preprocessor statements are:

%define <var-name> 
	Define preprocessor variable named <var-name>.

%ifdef <var-name>
	If preprocessor variable named <var-name> 
	is defined by %define, then process the text from
	this %ifdef to the closing %endif.

%endif
	Closing bracket for %ifdef preprocessor statement.
	Only one nesting level of %ifdef-%endif is allowed.

%include <file-name>
	Process contents of the file named <file-name>.
	If <file-name> is a relative name, it is looked up
        in a directory in which btyacc was started.
	Only one nesting level of %include is allowed.


		Version 2.0 changes
		-------------------
		  by Vadim Maslov


** Changed 16-bit short numbers to 32-bit int numbers in
grammar tables, so that huge grammar tables (tables that
are larger than 32768 elements) resulting from huge
grammars (Cobol grammar, for instance) can work correctly.
You need to have 32-bit integer to index table bigger than
32768 elements, 16-bit integer is not enough.

The original BtYacc just generated non-working tables
larger than 32768 elements without even notifying about
the table overflow.


** Make error recovery work correctly when error happens
while processing nested conflicts. Original BtYacc could
infinitely cycle in certain situations that involved error
recovery while in nested conflict.

More detailed explanation: when we have nested conflicts
(conflict that happens while trial-processing another
conflict), it leads btyacc into NP-complete searching of
conflict tree. The ultimate goal is YYVALID operator that
selects a particular branch of that tree as a valid one.

If no YYVALID is found on the tree, then error recovery
takes over.  The problem with this is that error recovery
is started in the same state context that exists on the
last surveyed branch of the conflict tree.  Sometimes this
last branch may be of zero length and it results in
recovering to exactly the same state as existed before
entering the conflict. BtYacc cycles then.

We solved this problem by memorizing the longest path in
the conflict tree while browsing it. If we ever get into
error recovery, we restore state that existed on the
longest path.  Effectively we say: if we have an error,
let us move forward as far as we possibly could while we
were browsing the conflict tree.


** Introduce YYVALID_NESTED operation in addition to
simply YYVALID.  When we have a nested conflict (conflict
while processing in trial mode for another conflict), we
want to relate YYVALID to a particular level of conflict
being in trial.

Since we mostly anticipate only 2-level nested conflicts
YYVALID_NESTED tells the parser to satisfy only the
internal conflict.  Therefore, in 1-level conflict
situation YYVALID_NESTED acts like a regular YYVALID, but
in 2-level conflict it is a no-op and the other YYVALID
for outer conflict will be searched for.


** Improved handling of situation where /tmp directory is
missing.  Original btyacc just died quietly when /tmp
directory was missing.  We added code that states the
problem explicitly. While on UNIX /tmp directory is always
present, it may be missing on WIN32 systems, therefore
diagnosing this situation is important.


	Version 1.0 changes: BackTracking 
	=================================
		by Chris Dodd

BTYACC is a modified version of yacc that supports
automatic backtracking and semantic disambiguation to
parse ambiguous grammars, as well as syntactic sugar for
inherited attributes (which tend to introduce conflicts).
Whenever a btyacc generated parser runs into a
shift-reduce or reduce-reduce error in the parse table, it
remembers the current parse point (yacc stack and input
stream state), and goes into trial parse mode.  It then
continues parsing, ignoring most rule actions.  If it runs
into an error (either through the parse table or through
an action calling YYERROR), it backtracks to the most
recent conflict point and tries a different alternative.
If it finds a successful parse (reaches the end of the
input or an action calls YYVALID), it backtracks to the
point where it first entered trial parse mode, and
continues with a full parse (executing all actions),
following the path of the successful trial.

Actions in btyacc come in two flavors -- {}-actions, which
are only executed when not in trial mode, and []-actions
which are executed regardless of mode.  There are also
inherited attributes, which look like arguments (they are
enclosed in "()") and act like []-actions.

What this buys you:

* No more lexer feedback hack.  In yacc grammars for C, a
standard hack, know as the "lexer feedback hack" is used
to find typedef names.  The lexer uses semantic
information to decide if any given identifier is a
typedef-name or not and returns a special token.  With
btyacc, you no longer need to do this; the lexer should
just always return an identifier.  The btyacc grammar then
needs a rule of the form:

typename: ID [ if (!IsTypeName(LookupId($1))) YYERROR; ]

While the hack works adequately well for parsing C, it
becomes a nightmare when you try to parse something like
C++, where treating an ID as a typedef becomes heavily
dependent on context.

* Easy disambiguation via simple ordering.  Btyacc runs
its trials via the rule "try shifting first, then try
reducing by the order that the conflicting rules appear in
the input file".  This means you can deal with semantic a
disambiguation rule like:
    [1] If it looks like a declaration it is, otherwise
    [2] If it looks like an expression it is, otherwise
    [3] it is a syntax error
	[Ellis&Stroustrup, Annotated C++ Reference Manual, p93]

To deal with this, you need only put all the rules for
declarations before the rules for expressions in the
grammar file.

* No extra cost if you do not use it.  Backtracking is
only triggered when the parse hits a shift/reduce or
reduce/reduce conflict in the table.  If you have no
conflicts in your grammar, there is no extra cost, other
than some extra code which will never be invoked.

* C++ and ANSI C compatible parsers.  The parsers produced
by btyacc can be compiled with C++ correctly.  If you
"#define" YYSTYPE to be some C++ type with constructor and
destructor, everything will work fine.  My favorite is
"#define YYSTYPE SmartPointer", where SmartPointer is a
smart pointer type that does garbage collection on the
pointed to objects.

BTYACC was originally written to make it easy to write a
C++ parser (my goal was to be able to use the grammar out
of the back of the ARM with as few modifications as
possible).  Anyone who has ever looked at Jim Roskind
public domain C++ yacc grammar, or the yacc-based grammar
used in g++ knows how difficult this is.  BTYACC is very
useful for parsing any ambiguous grammar, particularly
ones that come from trying to merge two (or more) complete
grammars.

Limitations of the backtracking: Currently, the generated
parser does NO pruning of alternate parsing paths.  To
avoid an exponential explosion of possible paths (and
parsing time), you need to manually tell the parser when
it can throw away saved paths using YYVALID.  In practice,
this turns out to be fairly easy to do.  A C++ parser (for
example) can just put a [YYVALID;] after every complete
declaration and statement rule, corresponding to pruning
the backtracking state after seeing a ';' or '}' -- there
will never be a situation in which it is useful to
backtrack past either of these.

Inherited attributes in btyacc:

Inherited attributes look a lot like function arguments to
non-terminals, which is what they end up being in a
recursive descent parser, but NOT how they are implemented
in btyacc.  Basically they are just syntactic sugar for
embedded semantic actions and $0, $-1, ... in normal yacc.
btyacc gives you two big advantages besides just the
syntax:
    1. it does type checking on the inherited attributes, 
       so you do not have to specify $<type>0 and makes sure 
       you give the correct number of arguments (inherited 
       attributes) to every use of a non-terminal.
    2. It "collapses" identical actions from that are produced 
       from inherited attributes.  This eliminates many 
       potential reduce-reduce conflicts arising from 
       the inherited attributes.

You use inherited attributes by declaring the types of the
attributes in the preamble with a type declaration and
declaring names of the attributes on the lhs of the yacc
rule.  You can of course have more than one rule with the
same lhs, and you can even give them different names in
each, but the type and number must be the same.

Here is a small example:
           /* lhs takes 2 inherited attributes */
%type <t1> lhs(<t1>, <t2>) 
	   stuff(<t1>, <t2>)
%%
lhs($i1, $i2) : { $$ = $i1 }
	      | lhs($i1, $i2) stuff($1,$i2) { $$ = $2; }

This is roughly equivalent to the following yacc code:
lhs :
      { $$ = $<t1>-1; }
    | lhs [ $<t1>$ = $-1; ] [ $<t2>$ = $<t2>0; ] stuff
      { $$ = $4; }
    ;

See the file "test/t2.y" for a longer and more complete
example.  At the current time, the start symbol cannot
have any arguments.

Variant parsers:

Btyacc supports the -S flag to use a different parser
skeleton, changing the way that the parser is called and
used.  The skeleton "push.skel" is included to produce a
"passive" parser that you feed tokens to (rather than
having the parser call a separate yylex routine).  With
push.skel, yyparse is defined as follows:

int yyparse(int token, YYSTYPE yylval)

You should call yyparse repeatedly with successive tokens
of input.  It returns 0 if more input is needed, 1 for a
successful parse, and -1 for an unrecoverable parse error.


	Miscellaneous Features in ver. 1.0
	----------------------------------
		by Chris Dodd

     The -r option has been implemented.  The -r option tells
Yacc to put the read-only tables in y.tab.c and the code and
variables in y.code.c.  Keith Bostic asked for this option so
that :yyfix could be eliminated.

     The -l and -t options have been implemented.  The -l
option tells Yacc not to include #line directives in the code
it produces.  The -t option causes debugging code to be
included in the compiled parser.

     The code for error recovery has been changed to
implement the same algorithm as AT&T Yacc.  There will still
be differences in the way error recovery works because AT&T
Yacc uses more default reductions than Berkeley Yacc.

     The environment variable TMPDIR determines the directory
where temporary files will be created.  If TMPDIR is defined,
temporary files will be created in the directory whose
pathname is the value of TMPDIR.  By default, temporary files
are created in /tmp.

     The keywords are now case-insensitive.  For example,
%nonassoc, %NONASSOC, %NonAssoc, and %nOnAsSoC are 
all equivalent.

     Commas and semicolons that are not part of C code are
treated as commentary.

     Line-end comments, as in BCPL, are permitted.  Line-end
comments begin with // and end at the next end-of-line.
Line-end comments are permitted in C code; they are converted
to C comments on output.

     The form of y.output files has been changed to look more
like those produced by AT&T Yacc.

     A new kind of declaration has been added.  
The form of the declaration is

	  %ident string

where string is a sequence of characters beginning with a
double quote and ending with either a double quote or the
next end-of-line, whichever comes first.  The declaration
will cause a #ident directive to be written near the start 
of the output file.

     If a parser has been compiled with debugging code, that
code can be enabled by setting an environment variable.  
If the environment variable YYDEBUG is set to 0, debugging
output is suppressed.  If it is set to 1, debugging output 
is written to standard output.


		Building BtYacc
		---------------
	by Chris Dodd and Vadim Maslov

We used GCC and GNU make to compile BtYacc both on UNIX and
WIN32 paltforms.  You are welcome to try different
combinations of makes and compilers.  Most likely it will
work, but it may require Makefile changes.

There is no config script.
Just type "make" and it should compile.

AWK. If you want to change file btyaccpa.ske (backtracking 
parser skeleton), you will need awk to compile it into 
skeleton.c file. We used GNU AWK (gawk) version 3.0.

It is known that using older versions of gawk
may create problems in compilation, because older awks 
have problems with backslashes at the end of a line.

For MSDOS, there a "makefile.dos" that should do the trick.
Note: makefile.dos was not tested for a long time.

The result of compilation should be a single executable called
"btyacc" which you can install anywhere you like; 
it does not require any other files in the distribution to run.


	       Legal Stuff
	       -----------
	by Chris Dodd and Vadim Maslov

In English: BtYacc is freeware. BtYacc is distributed with 
no warranty whatsoever. The author and any other contributors 
take no responsibility for any and all consequences of its use.

In Legalese: LIMITATION OF LIABILITY. NEITHER SIBER SYSTEMS
NOR ANY OF ITS LICENSORS NOR ANY BTYACC CONTRIBUTOR SHALL BE
LIABLE FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL
DAMAGES, OR DAMAGES FOR LOSS OF PROFITS, REVENUE, DATA OR
DATA USE, CAUSED BY BTYACC AND INCURRED BY CUSTOMER OR ANY
THIRD PARTY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION IN CONTRACT OR TORT, EVEN
IF SIBER SYSTEMS HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH
DAMAGES.
```

Asking us which is the better compiler could only cause a war
amongst all the different COBOL users within this group.
It'd be handy to know what environment you wish to develop on and
what type of applications you wish to develop.

I have been with three employees, one in Africa and two here
in Canada, all used the same COBOL, and its always been Acucobol.
You can find out more about them at either of the following links.
www.cobol.com
www.acucorp.com
www.acucobol.com

Regards,

Robert Annandale
Vancouver, BC

I do not have a web site, and have yet to learn how to
create one to "use" my allocated space. However, I feel what you are talking
about is desirable even if all it does is compare features, methods of doing
the same thing beyond the standard, etc. From prior experience it is my view
that this could be done by either the Bureau of Standards (all of our tax
money supports BOS, or by someone like Consumer's Report.

Vendors would not "enjoy" participation in such an effort, but might learn
something, and the result might be an increase in the level of common
language and interchangeability (reduction of conversion requirements.)
Maybe JOJ could get MS to do this as a condition of settling the current
litigation. Or maybe some organization like the DPMA or UN could sponsor
such an activity.

Last time I know of such an effort, the lawyers for the various vendors
involved asked the person involved to stop.  If that person were alive
today, I believe he might forget that he had done anything like this. It was
basically a head to head comparison of the features, and language support of
each vendor at the time.

Warren Simmons
```

Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> ploy guys take a bow.

Ken,

With no intent, I've put Bill between a rock and a hard place. I accept
his reasons for not participating. But as you say, there is a need. For
only about 2 months I've being using the web regularly now that I have a
direct connection. Others agree with me, 80% of what is on the web is
superfluous crap - plus God help you if you accidentally key in the
words "girl" or "boy"! (Don't get me wrong; I think sex is much more
interesting than jogging or hiking).

The web is too loosey-goosey with its key words. Had two grandchildren
with projects; the girl "Mexican traditional clothing" - got 500 hits
using WebFerret and it returned a list which was 80% U.S. retailers
selling Mexican style clothing. The boy, a specific query on a
particular dinosaur - Wow ! All sorts of info from US museums, the
Tyrrell Paleontolgy Museum here in Alberta, lots of pictures, drawings,
stories and pictures he could print-out and paint. 

If I key in COBOL I don't want to see references to C++, Pascal et al -
which is what happens. Dare I say it - are our COBOL standards
committees a bit self-serving, (like Juan Samaranch and his cronies -
took Salt Lake City to bring that to the fore). Are COBOL  Standards
heavily weighted in favour of both hardware/compiler manufacturers - may
not be in their interest to have a Master Site - but perhaps this is
something they should have provided/organized  some years ago. This
internet stuff places a very different emphasis on communication.  As
consumers of their products, perhaps we should remind them that "The
customer is always right".

Your observation on Fujitsu is correct - but don't forget at $499 US -
Microfocus tried it first with Visoc(Visual Object COBOL).

PS. How are you situated Down Under. Do you have a good Australian
'National Computing Centre' ? Years since I experienced it but NCC based
in Manchester did a damn fine job when VAT was introduced in the UK.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-17T00:00:00

On Mon, 19 Jul 1999 07:07:56 -0600, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>> One suggestion for you.  Try http://www.google.com for your search
>> engine.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Maybe it's designed for different type of queries than I make.

Remember it's an automatic Boolean AND search.  That might make a
difference.  Also, put the most important word (to you) first.

For example, if looking for Dump trucks made by Tonka in the 1960's, I
would enter "Tonka Dump Truck Vintage" 

and see what I get... just for grins..

I get a BUNCH of E-bay listings where I bight buy one of these. Same
words in another search engine, oh say, Infoseek - 

I get everything from heavy equipment to Burger king toys (don't tell
me those are vintage).
```

On Sat, 17 Jul 1999 04:47:35 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>Your observation on Fujitsu is correct - but don't forget at $499 US -
>Microfocus tried it first with Visoc(Visual Object COBOL).


First they gave away a beta version free - for OS/2.   Then the
switched it over to Windows, then they abandoned it.

At this time, Dialog system has been introduced.  We called MicroFocus
SEVERAL times to find out - which of these apparantly opposing
technologies were their future direction.  They claimed V O COBOL, but
soon changed to the other direction.  I don't think the $499.00 price
had anything to do with it's demise.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mqjeu$d0e@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net...
> On Sat, 17 Jul 1999 04:47:35 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

*NOT* a Micro Focus response - but

I think that the price had EVERYTHING to do with the demise of VisOC.  The
technology of NetExpress is *truly* a follow-on to the technology of VisOC.
(In fact, even the technology in the 32-bit Mainframe Express *compiler* is
a follow-on to that of VisOC.)

On the other hand, CA (Reallia), IBM, and Micro Focus all were offering
under $1,000 offerings at "roughly" the same time.  Every one of these found
that there was NOT sufficient market-demand for such products to make a
profit - or even to keep maintenance/marketing going. Interestingly enough,
even Fujitsu gave V3 of their product away - while V3 was still "current" -
but have *not* continued giving away their "current" V4.x product (although
their price for their current compiler is WELL below the price of other
vendors current products).

The bottom-line (IMHO) is that *most* of the COBOL vendors have found that
the "individual" developer market is not worth their effort - instead all of
them are concentrating on the "corporate" customers.

I am curious, I know that I have read things in this NG about people working
at sites that use MF, IBM, CA, AcuCOBOL, etc.   Is there ANYONE who reads
this NG who works for a "company" with more than 5 COBOL developers where
Fujitsu is the *CORPORATE* COBOL product of choice?  If (as I suspect) the
world is divided among "corporate" COBOL users and "individual" COBOL users,
that really does answer the original question/issue about the "demise" of
Micro Focus VisOC - as a "product" (not as a technology).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3791698F.93AFCA96@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net> <7mqjeu$d0e@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in message
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com

Thane and Bill,

I disagree with the suggestion that the demise was due to the price, or
that they actually abandoned the product. As you pointed out Bill,
NetExpress is the same product as Visoc. It includes goodies for
Internet, re-introduction of Dialog System, fresh shrink-wrap and price
x 3.

Thane - the OO/GUI code I wrote for Visoc works in NetExpress without
any changes - just re-compile. Want to know why it failed - LACK OF
EXAMPLES. Their product was way ahead of the rest of the pack, but
people couldn't comprehend OO or using the OO/GUI combination. All the
folks that took beta versions just didn't buy. I moved direct from DOS
and had the double-whammy, OO and Windowing. On two occasions I nearly
gave up.

Here's an interesting quote, without comment :-

> VisOC failed as a commercial product but was a huge success as a
> technology demonstration. Did you ever benchmark the VisOC compiler 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Then it wouldn't have been such a marketing fiasco.

Here is the real irony - the IDE for Visoc/NetExpress is 90%+ written
using OO/GUI technology, and the Dialog System is backed up by the same
OO/GUI. Having used all the gizmos they created to run the IDE - they
didn't and still don't get the message that they should provide examples
of how to use them. 

Popup Menus - You can look at the on-line help which tells you what to
do but no example. I used their IDE search engine to reference their OO
source files - there is a perfectly good example of using Popups in one
of the sources - but no reference to it in the on-line help.

What concerns me more is Bill's statement that it just isn't viable,
(for anybody), to produce an OO/GUI COBOL for PCs - the money isn't
there. 

Yet another quote :-

> - Gardner Group reports that by 2001, only 40% of development
> will be for Windows GUI interfaces (of any kind). 70% (I think
> that was the number) of all development will move to browser
> front-ends. The basic theme was Windows development will begin to
> disappear.

Thane, that kinda makes all that effort you and I are putting into
Fujitsu and NetExpress respectively, a bit of a waste of time, doesn't
it. The compiler-makers aren't too thrilled about the PC market, throw
away those screen-toolkits and use something else - why even bother with
COBOL for PCs - is this the death knell for COBOL ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3791CC59.BC1C4E52@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net> <7mqjeu$d0e@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <3791698F.93AFCA96@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> > - Gardner Group reports that by 2001, only 40% of development
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I do not agree with the browser stuff.

advantages:  Write once and run on any computer on the premises.

Disadvantages: Smart screens require Java script (which is not
portable) or all options just appear and validate on the back end
(CICS and MVS welcome back).

XML is supposed to help this along but I think that this may be
overrated and you rely on the fact that XML works on that particular
browser (not valid across the intranet where we get hits for netscape
V2.0)

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37927e5f.73173901@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net> <7mqjeu$d0e@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <3791698F.93AFCA96@home.com>`

```
On Sun, 18 Jul 1999 05:36:47 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:


>> - Gardner Group reports that by 2001, only 40% of development
>> will be for Windows GUI interfaces (of any kind). 70% (I think
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Oh, I see it, but I have a few thoughts.

1.  I am watching another group at work struggle with the Browser
interface.  On some machines they need IE 4.0 for it to work, on
others IE 5.0, sometimes it just doesn't work at all.  And these
people  DO know what they are doing, having created the most accepted,
widely distributed POS product in our industry. The browser interface
is just LAME it has a long way to go.

2.  I keep hoping thin client will win this game.  Get all the bells
and whistles you want and still have an internet application.

3.  I have no doubt that if XML (for example) became the standard for
the Browser interface, my interface vendor would have a way to take my
existing COBOL and move to XML with no code changes.

4.  Who says COBOL and a browswer interface are incompatible (Even of
the browswer interface is NOT a good idea).

5.  --Anectodal only-- People HATE the browser interface to an
APPLICATION - it's fine for presenting information, but for DOING
WORK, with lots of data entry, screen movement, etc - it is AWEFUL.
The only GOOD thing about it - and this may be why it "Makes it" - is
that the "client hardware" doesn't matter.  Even though today (for my
companies efforts at least) it still has to be Windows and IE.

6.  PC graphics gaming cards are going big.  We will have PCs for a
long time.  People like to "own" their applications and run them
THEMSELVES - this ownership (same attitude as cars in CA) will keep
the PC alive and applications on it alive - without the browser.
Imagine MS Word with a Browser interface.  

7.  To re-iterate - COBOL has a place in ALL these arenas.  We are not
wasting our time!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37929e40.4030724@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net> <7mqjeu$d0e@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <3791698F.93AFCA96@home.com> <37927e5f.73173901@news1.ibm.net>`

```
redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>On Sun, 18 Jul 1999 05:36:47 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
>wrote:
>
>
[snip]

>1.  I am watching another group at work struggle with the Browser
>interface.  On some machines they need IE 4.0 for it to work, on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is just LAME it has a long way to go.

I agree that Browser interfaces are not all that some industry pundits
claim that they are.  I also believe that 5 years from now the user
interface will be dramtically different and those who have "hardwired"
their user interface code will be relegated to the sorry task of
having to rewrite it all over again... 

>2.  I keep hoping thin client will win this game.  Get all the bells
>and whistles you want and still have an internet application.

I do too, but the masses will dictate which techniques are in greatest
demand.  Right now a thin client makes sense on many business to
business applications (high transaction volume; frequent access).  On
the other hand, it is absolutely amazing to me that many managers will
continue to insist on a web browser interface even though the purpose
of the application may be high volume data entry with daily access.

Frequently the reason that a browser client is chosen is because the
manager believes that because the masses are using browser clients, it
must be right.  They forget that a large percentage of the masses use
a browser interface to access site infrequently or with minimal data
entry requirements.

>3.  I have no doubt that if XML (for example) became the standard for
>the Browser interface, my interface vendor would have a way to take my
>existing COBOL and move to XML with no code changes.

...my lips are sealed!!  ;-)  I don;t want to be accused of selling
vaporware!

>4.  Who says COBOL and a browswer interface are incompatible (Even of
>the browswer interface is NOT a good idea).

You are 100% correct...they are indeed not incompatible.  It is simply
a matter of creating a good design for a universal "user interface
middleware" product which provide a portable target interface.  It has
been done with character mode and GUI screens, why not with browsers?
There may indeed be some limitations as with text to GUI interfaces
..it's tough to display bitmaps on a VT100 screen ;-)

but I'm sure that it is possible to come quite close...

>5.  --Anectodal only-- People HATE the browser interface to an
>APPLICATION - it's fine for presenting information, but for DOING
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>companies efforts at least) it still has to be Windows and IE.

Most people do complain about the slow, interpretive nature of
browsers.  I hear people say that when the communications are improved
to offer higher transfer speed, all our problems will be solved....but
I believe that when communications speed improve, the market will find
ways to eat up all the improvements made in the rate of data transfer.

>6.  PC graphics gaming cards are going big.  We will have PCs for a
>long time.  People like to "own" their applications and run them
>THEMSELVES - this ownership (same attitude as cars in CA) will keep
>the PC alive and applications on it alive - without the browser.
>Imagine MS Word with a Browser interface.  

Blechh!

>7.  To re-iterate - COBOL has a place in ALL these arenas.  We are not
>wasting our time!

Agreed!  If we re-wrote cirical business applications everytime the
latest and greatest programming langauge is developed, we would be
constantly re-writing our applications!  This is far too expensive for
the questionable benefit of re-writing them.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ZUk3.729$OP.2566@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net> <7mqjeu$d0e@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <3791698F.93AFCA96@home.com> <37927e5f.73173901@news1.ibm.net> <37929e40.4030724@news.enter.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
> redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> >7.  To re-iterate - COBOL has a place in ALL these arenas.  We are not
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the questionable benefit of re-writing them.

agreed, but there is often a very big business benefit.

example: an insurance company offers quotes to customers via the telephone.
customer rep takes details from caller, keys them in, a few transactions
with mainframe COBOL and a quote is relayed back to the caller. now put an
HTML front-end onto those same transactions. access is immediate and direct
to millions of potential customers and your costs are dramtically reduced.
```

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)

```
In article <5ZUk3.729$OP.2566@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>, gaz@home.com 
says...
> agreed, but there is often a very big business benefit.
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gazaloo

These are the types of apps I think the browser interface is perfect, 
opening data up to the masses. Our web app saves around 3600 calls a 
month, adding 100 to 150 users each month.

I'm just not sold on delivering an entire mission-critical application 
with such a primitive interface. Sure you can spruce it up with 
JavaScript, activex, etc but then its back to platform-specific. If I'm 
writing for Windows, why not just write a native GUI interface and 
forget the bloat of the browser?

Just my thoughts, I'm interested in hearing other sides of the story.

ray
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.11fdae0058322c8698968a@news>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net> <7mqjeu$d0e@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <3791698F.93AFCA96@home.com> <37927e5f.73173901@news1.ibm.net>`

```
In article <37927e5f.73173901@news1.ibm.net>, redsky@ibm.net says...
> On Sun, 18 Jul 1999 05:36:47 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> disappear.

Just to put this in the context of our company. Our main project is a 
Dialog GUI project which runs all of our core-business. There are four 
small web projects. One web enables a 'tiny' portions of our core-
business. The others are related to but not part of the main business. I 
guess Gardner would call that a "4 to 1 margin" ("lies, damn lies and 
statistics" comes to mind). The web apps combined don't add up (in lines 
or complexity) to the smallest module in the core system. Truth is, only 
one of the web apps is even necessary for the core business and it is 
mainly for account inquiry and one entry form to update demographics.

> >
> 
> The browser interface is just LAME it has a long way to go.
I agree -- today. Windows v1 was also pretty lame. I'm sure this will 
mature in time but the whole concept may change. Persistent connections 
in the new HTTP protocol may change the way we code CGIs two years from 
now.
> 
> 2.  I keep hoping thin client will win this game.  Get all the bells
> and whistles you want and still have an internet application.
This is our next thrust for our main project (the current client is 
pretty fat).

> 
> 5.  ... is
> that the "client hardware" doesn't matter.  Even though today (for my
> companies efforts at least) it still has to be Windows and IE.
We surveyed 125 clients. Only one has a non-pc platform (one user with 
both Mac and PC on her desk). The 'independent' argument just doesn't 
mean too much to us when all the clients have the same platform. By 
contrast, they have every vendor's browser from the newest to the 
version of IE that came with the original Win95 CD. The only common 
ground is HTML 3 which makes for a primitive interface by comparison.

> 7.  To re-iterate - COBOL has a place in ALL these arenas.  We are not
> wasting our time!
I Second that motion!
```

Jimmy, Calgary AB

Here's what I remember.

I went to the "Free" site to download the "open Beta".  It was 16 bit,
or 32 bit if running OS/2.  I liked it.  Showed it to the boss.  He
said, Yeah but it's not Windows - but the site said Win32 would be
coming soon.  So I waited a few weeks, went back, and it was Win32 -
free for 30 days then $400 or so dollars.  Wow I said our prayers are
answered - but we had started down the Dialog system path - so my boss
called his MF sales rep and she said that DS would be dead and VSIOC
would be "the product with a future" - she had it backward.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: About cobol compilers

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5BUk3.525$OP.2000@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7mlq82$93f$1@aquila.tiscalinet.it> <378f9ed1$0$32398@fountain.mindlink.net> <378FAFEF.EC0FB7D0@home.com> <7mochm$im9@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <378FC67C.15F676BA@zip.com.au> <37900C74.75641372@home.com> <37909d8c.379322916@news1.ibm.net> <Ko8k3.1773$4J2.31618@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <379280a7.73757233@news1.ibm.net> <ZZQk3.3753$4J2.48743@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <3793eb87.9012317@news1.ibm.net> <379401bf.14701262@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane, i do understand your confusion! these various releases were
"concurrent" (it's difficult to introduce new stuff cleanly). i can try and
clear it up as best i can, but be warned it may generate more! i'll stick
with the thread as long as you have steam! ;-)

Object COBOL for OS/2 and NT was built on "classic" technology. this is the
same core compiler technology that existed in 16-bit form on DOS, Windows
and OS/2 and in 32-bit form on UNIX, OS/2 and Win32.

VisOC was the first product release that was built on "genesis" technology
(next generation -- primarily done to address some architectural issues with
existing "classic" tech and as a means to enable new bleeding-edge
enhancements to COBOL such as multi-threading, >32-bit etc). the new
'Express line of compiler solutions from MERANT Micro Focus (Net, Mainframe
& Server) have all evolved from this technology base.

in many ways, VisOC was initially aimed at providing an MSVC++ like tool for
COBOL programmers, hence GUI was supported in a C++ like manner (either thru
Win32 and/or class libraries in combination with a dialog editor, but NOT
Dialog System). today, all of these methods are supported, plus the
traditional Dialog System (which has been enhanced to support ActiveX) and
Form Designer (an COBOL-centric HTML based painter).

hope this helps more than it confuses!
```

to send 500 identically worded insulting "reviews",
they will STICK.

a conflict of interest (whether fiscal or mental) out.

<a id="t-0da3b42c08"></a>
## Thread: WANG VS/COBOL 85 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### WANG VS/COBOL 85

- **From:** Mark Tymieniecki <markt@mccabe.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378F056B.7D61CC1F@mccabe.co.uk>`

```
Hi folks,

Is anyone out there running the WANG VS/COBOL 85 emulation environment
on UNIX/AIX/SOLARIS. I have just done some tool development work on this
and am wondering there are more sites out there with similar migration
problems.
```

#### ↳ Re: WANG VS/COBOL 85

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A6C3B27CEEAA9078.2643E522701EDDAC.3D1BD8CEE7F552E4@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<378F056B.7D61CC1F@mccabe.co.uk>`

```
Mark Tymieniecki wrote:
> 
> Hi folks,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> problems.

Mark,
We've been running the VUPort product from San Soft, Inc. for about five
years.  It is extremely Wang-like in every way, including the command
processor, print/procedure queues, nearly all Wang utilities, etc.  In a
nutshell, VUPort is a pre-processor for Micro Focus COBOL and a runtime
system to provide the Wang environment.  It runs on all major flavors of
Unix.  See http://www.sansoft.com for more details (they're re-working
the site so it has a few "gaps").

If you're migrating yourself I highly recommend it.
```

#### ↳ Re: WANG VS/COBOL 85

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mrg01$js@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<378F056B.7D61CC1F@mccabe.co.uk>`

```

LANGUAGES: C, COBOL, C++, EASYTRIEVE, JCL SHELL, VISUAL BASIC

DBMS: ORACLE, PL/SQL

INTERNET: HTML

OTHER SKILLS: SPF, SYNSORT, AWK, ACCESS, UTILITY SCRIPTS,
TELECOMMUNICATIONS DATA NETWORKING SERVICES, TELECOMMUNICATIONS
BUSINESS KNOWLEDGE.

 Reply and send Resumes to...: mailto:jdimov@ibm.net

When replying,  In the Subject Line of your email, PLEASE refer to the
Job Number listed above.

<a id="t-1fe4ef6784"></a>
## Thread: IF-statement — 2 msgs

### Re: IF-statement

- **From:** "Paul Keirnan" <paul.keirnan@det.nsw.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378eb6e3@htdev1.isd.tafensw.edu.au>`
- **References:** `<7lfkt7$q1j$1@endevour.netg.se> <377BE098.51CC@compaq.com> <377CBF37.D1AF156A@NOSPAMhome.com> <377d352a.8529028@netnews> <#VOZzkRx#GA.306@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <377E06FA.666993E6@zip.com.au> <eH5kTyAy#GA.264@cpmsnbbsa02> <37836C57.D4C2C9BF@att.net>`

```
One of the reasons for doing away with all periods except the last
one in a paragraph, is that they are small (not highly visible).

I believe that this extends to the period used at the end of the
paragraph as well, especially when using a free-format, variable-
line-length COBOL.

My preference therefore is that the last line of a paragraph is
always:
               CONTINUE.

This helps to guard against accidental deletion of the line.

Regards,
Paul



William Lynch wrote in message <37836C57.D4C2C9BF@att.net>...
>"Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: IF-statement

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<378F3618.120B171@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7lfkt7$q1j$1@endevour.netg.se> <377BE098.51CC@compaq.com> <377CBF37.D1AF156A@NOSPAMhome.com> <377d352a.8529028@netnews> <#VOZzkRx#GA.306@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com> <377E06FA.666993E6@zip.com.au> <eH5kTyAy#GA.264@cpmsnbbsa02> <37836C57.D4C2C9BF@att.net> <378eb6e3@htdev1.isd.tafensw.edu.au>`

```
Paul Keirnan wrote:
> 
> One of the reasons for doing away with all periods except the last
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> This helps to guard against accidental deletion of the line.

That makes sense for your style.  But I'm not worried about errors which
the compiler will catch.  The only reason you have the period seems to
be to get a clean compile.  If someone accidentally deletes your period,
no harm is done, the compiler tells you about it and you fix it.  But
this LOOKS cleaner so I like it.  Or would, if I were convinced to go
with the periodless style.
```

- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37916EE8.DA9B1DB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <7mqa20$c9q$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37915526.21B0C334@home.com> <7mrobe$969$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> No, James, I'm very out of date. I've been retired since Nov. '81, and only
> recently began to learn the current state of the art.  I'm very happy about
> all of the progress that has been made, and look for you youngsters to do
> something useful to aid the future generations. <VBG>

What a very nice man you are - "youngsters" - I immediately went out in
the garden and did one of those Gene Kelly jumps in the air and clicked
my heels - mind you I nearly broke my bloody neck! But, you've got me
worried; your advice please, should I phone Ottawa and ask the Feds if
they want me to return those old age pension payments they've been
sending me ?

On a serious note. Although in systems since '63, I didn't get into
programming until about the same time you 'left'. Most comments to this
have been quips - Either they think I'm certifiable or they just don't
get it. (Age in your case allows you to judge and look forward, not
backwards). I hate the thought, but I think this BEAUTIFUL language is
headed down the tubes.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qljk3.21317$Tl3.138872@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <7mqa20$c9q$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37915526.21B0C334@home.com> <7mrobe$969$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37916EE8.DA9B1DB@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>... I hate the thought, but I think this BEAUTIFUL language is
>headed down the tubes.

I also hate the thought, but I am seeing things that disturb me.  One
local government I know of has people in upper management who have
decided that COBOL and mainframes are a waste of time.  One of these
same people said about 10 months ago "Y2K is a hoax."  So the word is
"Throw out the mainframe and COBOL, and rewrite everything on PC based
client server using proprietary tools and languages, and do it as fast
as humanly possible."  The MIS staff and budget are going to at least
quintuple, system reliability and up-time are going to take a serious
hit, but they really don't care.  They do not want to hear about recent
surveys showing only 16% of such attempts succeeding, industry wide.

When people start making such decisions for all the wrong reasons, it
does not bode well for COBOL, or the future of business in general.
Business decisions are supposed to be made based on careful analysis
of cost/benefit, not 'whim du jour'.  If this were an isolated thing,
it wouldn't bother me so much.  But I am seeing this attitude spread
in other organizations I know.  If this kind of 'thinking' continues
to spread, it won't matter what COBOL's advantages are, because the
bottom line is now subordinate to being P.C.  If this is the way we
are going to conduct business in the new millennium, all I can say is
"God help us."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c1nk3.3409$dA5.55871@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <7mqa20$c9q$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37915526.21B0C334@home.com> <7mrobe$969$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37916EE8.DA9B1DB@home.com> <qljk3.21317$Tl3.138872@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>
>Business decisions are supposed to be made based on careful analysis
>of cost/benefit, not 'whim du jour'.

If I may qualify that statement a tad: most business decisions *are* made on
that basis.

Unfortunately, too many managers only assess costs and benefits over too
short a timeframe. (Like, the end of the current quarter). A major software
rewrite's benefits may not be fully realized until a much greater time
period has elapsed

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

Michael Mattias wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>made on that basis.

Then God help us. :-)

>Unfortunately, too many managers only assess costs and benefits over
>too short a timeframe. (Like, the end of the current quarter). A major
>software rewrite's benefits may not be fully realized until a much
>greater time period has elapsed

I would feel better if these folks had done *any* sort of cost/benefit
analysis, but no.  They simply decided that PC based C/S was *the only*
way to go, because they think it's 'sexier', and that's the end of it.
They refused to even meet with knowledgeable in-house staff to hear any
alternate ideas.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3792086D.C6098A1F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <7mqa20$c9q$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37915526.21B0C334@home.com> <7mrobe$969$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37916EE8.DA9B1DB@home.com> <qljk3.21317$Tl3.138872@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote:
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

Perfectly true what you say Judson. To re-emphasize your point, about
bad decisions, let me give three examples from my commercial experience.
All are from my UK days :-

(a) Unigate a dairy company - 'merger' of two separates, United Dairies
and Cow & Gate. United had the bucks - and they didn't like the fact
that Cow & Gate evaporated milk was a more successful product than their
"Winnie Wilts(hire)" evaporated with a cartoon of a grinning cow. So
let's introduce a new product "Regal" and an advertising campaign. One
of the bean-counters did some numbers and worked out it would take six
years to recover the advertising costs. Anybody from the UK reading this
? - I challenge you to go into a supermarket and find a can of Regal
evaporated milk.

(b) Same company. Back in '60s Green Giant didn't have its own
distribution set-up in UK. The corporate secretary told me he got called
in from his garden one Saturday morning. It was the Sales Manager (VP
Sales). "Hi John, I'm at Heathrow airport, flight leaves in half an hour
for Switzerland. I'm going to discuss a deal with an American company,
Green Giant, about us distributing their product. What sort of
accounting/legal questions should I ask them ?"

(c) We worked across the road from the Somerset County Council. One of
their transportation engineers bought time on our ICL mainframe to run
his FORTRAN programs. Reason - their DPM didn't like the ICL salesman he
had to deal with - so switched to a Honeywell.

Nothing changes.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3792791e.71828404@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <7mqa20$c9q$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37915526.21B0C334@home.com> <7mrobe$969$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <37916EE8.DA9B1DB@home.com> <qljk3.21317$Tl3.138872@news3.mia>`

```
On Sun, 18 Jul 1999 07:00:50 -0500, "Judson McClendon" 
>it wouldn't bother me so much.  But I am seeing this attitude spread
>in other organizations I know.  If this kind of 'thinking' continues
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Our mainframe has been in "danger" for a while.   And it might soon
bite the dust due to the myopic idea that C/S is a success.

We are betting the farm on a central server (but certainly NOT a
mainframe) database architecture with "Web Browser" interface.

I laugh in my cube all the time.  I can whip out a COBOL ap that
works, is fast, reliable and complete, faster than most can decide
which HTML element will give the correct look to the application. We
are moving backward - but I have confidence it will stop.

Why?  It COSTS TOO MUCH.  Problem is that it also costs to "Go back".


I find we are understaffed.  But I still can't see that this "new"
technology leads to faster, cleaner, better products.

Me?  I'm just going to keep plugging away and not worry about it.
```

the facts'. Born 9.9.31.

I can code exactly what I want, providing somebody buys it - but I would
really like to think it remains COBOL or COBOOL (Common Business Object
Oriented Language). I'll probably persist until I either go ga ga or
take the Great Elevator, upwards I hope. I will be the envy of you all,
I'll be sat on a cotton cloud chewing the fat with Grace Hopper. 

No, don't even ask. I'm not going to come down and whisper in your ear
that such a place really does exist. Like me, you are going to have to
figure that out for yourselves. Keep you guessing - and it will make you
nice people, just in case the Big Elevator also goes downwards.

Concluding on a serious note. It was extremely exciting when computers
were first introduced - came from a generation when only a select few
made it to university, so data processing presented a golden opportunity
for the remainder who were 'thinkers' or had an ability to write machine
code or Assembler. I object to all the new languages, they are just like
the in-vogue 'teach kids a new way to do math'. All the clever stuff in
C++, Pascal, Java etc. could have been added to COBOL a long time ago. 
If you've been following me, I mentioned I used Microsoft COBOL. Now why
do you think Mr. Microsoft dropped COBOL and gracefully handed over the
baton to Microfocus ? We may not be happy with Bill's stranglehold on
PCs, but you have to give him his due - a smart marketer and visionary.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

I am not sure that Cobol *is* the language to write high volume commercial
software in.  However, there is a major difference between high volume
commercial software and large customized systems, or even semi-customized
systems.

BTW, you mentioned a ZIP file in your OO posting.  Such things get stripped
from NG's by my ISP, could I trouble you to mail me a copy? I would like to
try to get an OOP "hello world" program to work under Fujitsu.

do

was
>> going to keep up to your posts and work twelve hour days as well. It now

…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

```
Ok, I will start.

    identification division.
    program-id. "Cobol".
    procedure division.
    main-line.
         perform analyse-request-line.
        perform pass-one-scan.
        perform pass-two-scan.
        if listing-flag is equal to "y"
            perform listing-pass.
        goback.

    analyse-request-line.
        continue.
    pass-one-scan.
        continue.
    pass-two-scan.
        continue.
    listing-pass.
        continue.


Your turn.

> <Barticus@att.spam.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

This had me going for a little while.  I was wondering when I had
responded to my own post, because I didn't think I had.  I hadn't.

> > African elephant or Indian elephant?
> > 
> See Donald. My beloved wife is extremely impressed by him - she's the
> whiz on Jeopardy.

All I did was stall by asking a question.  Of course it was the right
question, would should impress a Jeopardy fan like your beloved.  

A.  Number of times Randall has tried out for Jeopardy without getting on.
Q.  What is 11?

BTW, I have deliberately spelled "Jeopardy" exactly the way you did.  See
if your wife can correct us.

> World Almanac 1999 - African 660 days, Asian 645 days.

After stalling, I went searching the net for the answer, and couldn't find
it.  For a laugh, go to http://www.ask.com and ask Jeeves any question
containing the words "gestation period".  Sometimes inference engines can
use logic that is a little too fuzzy.

Every site I found gave the traditional answer of 22 onths, except one
that said 21 for the African elephant (Asian wasn't mentioned).  Actually,
I already thought it was the African elephant's gestation that was a
little shorter.  It makes sense that the larger animal has the longer
gestation (the smallest animal with a gestation period over a year is the
Camel) but there are lots of exceptions.  The gestation period of a bear
is about 2 months.  (That 2 month figure is off the top of my head.
Please don't enter into the bear-breeding business without further
research.)

> -- ooOO00oo--??--??--
> R B |\  Randall Bart the Wisest
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> l    |/ That's like asking if anyone has read Magna Carta.

LOL.  And yes, I have read the Magna Carta.  A Modern English translation;
I don't read Latin or Middle English.
```

Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> > > African elephant or Indian elephant?
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I don't know how to phrase this delicately, so I'll put it word for
word; my beloved's reply, "Bloody idiot. I suppose he spells it
Jepperdy!". I can only comment, that as I type this I'm looking at the
CD Version and there is the so suave Alxex posing next to the word
J-E-O-P-A-R-D-Y. Are you surprised it took you 11 miserable attempts.

> .....the traditional answer of 22 onths, except one.....

I know it's being picky but she also commented, "Doesn't he use a
spellchecker ?"

Yes, did go take a look-see at Ask Jeeves - the mind boggles ! My
beloved thought you should be brought up-to-date on the topic of
gestation. She assumes you mean Bactrian Camel - 406. Ass and Grant's
Zebra 365, and you were way out of wack on the bears, Black 219, Grizzly
225 and Polar 240. (PS. We saw two grizzlies, in natural habitat,  last
year for first time - magnificent).

Thought this one up about a year ago based on its coincidences.

A: (a) Movie # 1 - She starred with actor A who also produced/directed.
   (b) Movie # 2 - don't worry about the movie name, I can't
       remember and it was a non-event anyway. She starred with Actor
       B. He used stage name, his own already in use by another actor. 
       She was also married to Actor B for a short while. 
   (c) Movie # 3 - she starred with Actor B as her lover and Actor C
       as her father
   (d) Movie # 4 - Actor D produced/directed(?). Actors A and C
       were also in movie. 
   (e) TV - she starred with Actor D in a TV version of a stage play
       already turned into a movie.

Q. Who is she ?

You probably need to be a film buff - if it's too difficult - I'll
give clues, upon request. Anybody can take a shot at this, but please
supply method of deduction with correct answer. Will only provide clues
when requested by Randall.  

Jimmy, Calgary AB

but you can read the books at
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYLR201/CCONTENTS. 
A list of books is at
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/FINDBOOK?filter=COBOL&REFRESH=Refresh&Collection=0

The standards committee have removed the REPORT WRITER and SCREEN
sections.  Now I think that was a mistake, driven by the mainframe
people. 

Speaking of standards, are any or all of the PC vendors (uFocus,
et. al.) working on the committee, and is another version in the
works?  We've been "COBOL 85" for quite a while now...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL and Standards (was Re: Environment stability

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n2u2t$l24@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <3790C304.688FFF03@techie.com> <7mqk04$dbg@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <37910E64.B37AD42@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
   <much snippage>
>
> Bill,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I am a really BAD person to be answering this - because I have some "issues"
with the current Standards groups.  On the other hand, I can at least point
you to some starting places.

For J4 (the ANSI-ish COBOL group), look at:
  http://www.microfocus.com/Standards/

For WG4 (the ISO-ish COBOL group), look at:
  http://anubis.dkuug.dk/jtc1/sc22/wg4/

For NCITS, the parent body to J4, look at:
  http://www.ncits.org/

For SC22, the parent body to WG4, look at:
  http://anubis.dkuug.dk/JTC1/SC22/

A few other "bits of information" related to your question (or at least how
I read it):

1) The next COBOL Standard is being developed as an ISO Standard, not as an
ANSI one.  The assumption (hope? expectation) is that once it is adopted
thru the ISO procedures, that ANSI will also adopt it - with minimal or no
modifications.  (This is the reverse of previous Standards which were first
ANSI Standards and then adopted by ISO.)

2) I think that your reference to "database" was probably to the CCC JOD
(COBOL CODASYL Committee - Journal of Development).  At one time, J4 (then
X3J4) did no (or almost no) "development" on the COBOL language.  Instead
this separate entity (CCC) did development and X3J4 took "snap-shots" of the
JOD and "adopted" it to make a new ANSI Standard.  (This is highly
simplified - but gives a clue of the OLD procedures.)  The database
structure was a part of the JOD but has never progressed to the ANSI (or
ISO) Standard.

3) Different companies have joined and then left different ones of these
committees (CCC and X3J4 - also some "consortia" such as X/Open and MIA).
My impression (VERY personal - not official) is that a number of companies
have "written" off COBOL and do not feel it is worth their
time/effort/resources to participate in the COBOL development and
maintenance procedures.   ISO (as stated in another message) has delegates
from COUNTRIES and *not* from companies.

4) FYI - another entity of interest is FIPS which issued a US government
"COBOL Standard".  Because of the "money involved" (he said cynically) the
FIPS Standard was often quite significant.  For example, it REQUIRED the
Intrinsic Function Module - while the ANSI Standard had it optional - and
few (if any?) vendors provide "HIGH Subset" COBOL compilers *without* the
Intrinsic Function Module.  There was also another group (related to FIPS -
but not quite the same) which USED to offer "certification testing" of COBOL
compilers.  It was *relatively* easy to pass these tests (and still have
many "bugs") but I think that group is no longer doing COBOL certifications
(but I could be wrong on this - it has "come and gone" a number of times in
the last few years).

5) Membership in the J4 committee is VOLUNTARY and so when people write
about "kicking people out" or "who decides who can join" these are really
irrelevant questions.  Similarly, both the ANSI and ISO rules and procedures
for creating and adopting a new Standard apply to *everything* for which
Standards are made - for example the COBOL Standard goes thru (mostly?) the
same steps and procedures and screw-drivers, plumbing interfaces, C++, and
SQL.   Therefore, if you want to suggest that J4 do something (officially)
think about how it fits into the ANSI (or ISO for WG4) "world".

I hope this helps (a bit) and I would be happy to see others add to my
(relatively) brief explanation.
```

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> 2) I think that your reference to "database" was probably to the CCC JOD
> (COBOL CODASYL Committee - Journal of Development).

There was also an X3 committee which worked on a cross-language database
standard in the late 70s and early 80s.  They were trying to make a
database which cold be accessed simultaneously from multiple ANSI standard
languages.  This couldn't be done without standardizing cross-language
data types, which no language agreed to do, so the project collapsed.

There was a later committee just to develop cross language data standards.
It may have been a spin-off of the database committee.  Each language
group was interested in changing the other languages to match their own
data types, and no one was interested in supporting anything new.  That
collapsed, as well.

> There was also another group (related to FIPS -
> but not quite the same) which USED to offer "certification testing" of COBOL
> compilers.  It was *relatively* easy to pass these tests (and still have
> many "bugs")

They sent you all the program sources you had to compile and run.  In
theory, you could write a "compiler" which recognized each of those
program sources and spat out the correct output.  There were some
compilers which passed certification with a release which was completely
unusable, i e, which failed to compile most ANSI programs.  You couldn't
sell computers to the Feds until your compiler passed certification.  It
let you sell computers to the government, while you got the compiler
working.

In the Burroughs COBOL-74 compilers for Medium Systems (V Series) and
Large Systems (A Series) the early release of Indexed files was intended
only to pass ANSI certification.  Every customer who used it was warned by
their Burroughs rep not to.  But soem customers stubbornly used it anyway,
and kept reporting bugs to Burroughs until it was fixed.  Neither
implementation was ever efficient.  Burroughs had a strict poilicy against
taking performance issues as bugs.  There were very rare exceptions, like
when a feature was suddenly running at one tenth its former speed, and
even then you had to fight your way thru the bureaucracy.
```

## Thread: Compiling a COBOL program both as Batch and Online — 5 msgs

### Compiling a COBOL program both as Batch and Online

- **From:** "Raghu" <raghubabu@mindspring.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<7mq97u$vv7$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi,

I would like to compile  Cobol programs so that two
load modules (one is batch and other one is Online used
by CICS) are created.

I can modify the JCL to create two load modules. But
are there any specific procedures I need to be aware of.
Because, If I'm calling another program inside my program
online version expects two parms : DFHEIBLK and
DFHCOMMAREA.  I can't have two copies of same program
one for batch and one for online, I would like to achieve
this with one copy of source.
Any suggestions,
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiling a COBOL program both as Batch and Online

- **From:** "Steve Beattie" <steve@sbeattie.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7msl0e$vh8$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7mq97u$vv7$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I've no doubt someone will correct me if I'm wrong, but why don't you just
use a CALL statement in your CICS program (as long as you're using VS Cobol
II) to invoke the subroutine.  The main program will remain in main storage.
Remember to use a GOBACK or EXIT PROGRAM in the subroutine.

Hope this helps, Steve.

#### ↳ Re: Compiling a COBOL program both as Batch and Online

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3791DC69.A6DC5FB0@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<7mq97u$vv7$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Options:

1.  Use a front end to the batch program to call the online
program with the two extra parameters.  (Of course, the online
program is smart enough not to try and use them in the batch
environment!)

2.  Use an entry point into the program for the batch invocation. 
This is required if you need the PARM field from the EXEC card as
that comes in the first parameter in batch.

3. If you don't need the PARM field, then you can use the same
entry point.

4.  If you truly do not need any CICS support in the program when
it runs online, compile it twice.  Once with the CICS preprocessor
and one without.  All the preprocessor will do is make one version
receive the two extra CICS parameters in the online version. 

5.  If this is a subroutine, then you don't HAVE to call with the
two extra parameters unless you need them, i.e. are going to
request CICS services.  We have many subroutines (e.g. table
lookups) which are called without the extra two parameters in the
online environment.  Remember that the CICS preprocessor will
tweak you entry points where you receive parameters, but it does
not do anything to the CALLs where you pass parameters.

6.  You can use the SET statement to interchange pointers passed
to you.    There is a pseudo register (special name) for each 01
level in the LINKAGE SECTION.  You can assign the ADDRESS OF one
01 level to another.  I've used this to adjust things at the front
of a program to correct for the different parameters passed in
batch and CICS.

Raghu wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```

<a id="t-772b5b35fc"></a>
## Thread: Web access to compiler information — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Web access to compiler information

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mp3ms$qoh@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
As a follow-up to another thread on "which compiler is best,"  I did want to
make it clear that I am *more* than happy to add web-links to the homepage
of any vendor that anyone (vendor or otherwise) can point me to - in the
COBOL FAQ.

Currently I do this for those that I have - but MOSTLY these are UNIX/PC
based compilers - and not the "proprietary" (mainframe and mid-size)
compiler vendors.  If anyone has a COBOL vendor that they would like me to
add a web-site for, please feel free to send it to me by private email - or
via the NG. Similarly my section on both "tools" and "education" are pretty
weak - but I just never seem to get around to updating those (nor do I see
doing this as a very high priority among comp.lang.cobol NG readers).  Let
me know if your "vendor of choice's" homepage is missing from the current
on-line FAQ.

The thing that I (personally) don't want to add to the FAQ is either
feature/price comparisons *or* user (happy or unhappy) reviews.  Again, I
understand the desire for these - but they are outside what I would like to
get in the middle of.  (I do usually use vendor-provided "summaries" of
their products - without comment when describing the various offerings.
Sometimes I "hold my nose" while doing so, but do feel it is the fairest
approach for *my* FAQ to take at this time.)
```

---
