- **Message-ID:** `<C8D540A5B5A6E754.5080EF4D8306F56C.C48BA9F2B875EF46@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com> <r0%k3.2575$_f5.26236@news3.mia> <37949F1E.602548B9@home.com> <i83l3.3060$_f5.30095@news3.mia> <3794D221.15FF9397@home.com> <TNkl3.4715$Kk.39268@news2.mia> <379654EC.FD57917B@home.com> <mCGl3.2309$xN4.21583@news3.mia> <37997EBB.9F638EC7@zip.com.au> <7ncemp$n8g$1@news.hitter.net> <379C6CAF.97BB9A4E@NOSPAMhome.com> <379C9AE3.E9EA0995@home.com> <379CA768.67F1B445@NOSPAMhome.com> <7nildo$dl0@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 26 Jul 1999 16:56:12 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>> James J. Gavan wrote:
>>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>PICTURE that the programmers specifies.

I'm wondering if the previous discussion is relevant to a problem I
recently encountered in a CICS Cobol (OS/390 MVS) program.  

I needed to set the length field of a temp storage READQ statement.
CICS requires this field to be a half-word (PIC 9(04) COMP).  The
length of the queue in decimal was 14,400 or 3840 in hex.  In
Working-Storage I defined a field thus:

05  WS-QUEUE-LENGTH     PIC 9(04)  COMP  VALUE 14400.

Now if I remember correctly I got a compiler error on the field.  I
worked around it by defining the following:

05  WS-QUEUE-LENGTH-DBL   PIC S9(08)  COMP  VALUE 14400.
05  WS-QUEUE-LENGTH-FIELDS  REDEFINES  WS-QUEUE-LENGTH-DBL.
      10   FILLER                           PIC S9(04)  COMP.
      10  WS-QUEUE-LENGTH-HALF  PIC S9(08)  COMP.

I then used WS-QUEUE-LENGTH-HALF as my length field in the CICS
command to read the temp storage queue.  

It seems rather awkward that even though 14400 would in hex fit nicely
into a PIC 9(04) COMP field, the compiler wasn't smart enough to
figure that out.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Some days your the dog.  Some days your the hydrant.


 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3796639D.E3E58E40@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <UVJk3.971$ng2.5648@news2.mia> <37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com> <r0%k3.2575$_f5.26236@news3.mia>`

```
One tiny detail about COBOL internal subroutines.  Some, but not
*all* can be compiled externally.  The difference is if variables
are exposed in the calling program (GLOBAL clause) or not.  The
compile of the inner program fails when compiled standalone
because the definitions in the outer program are not available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n2o56$5l3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <UVJk3.971$ng2.5648@news2.mia> <37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com>...

>OK.  So when do you want this better isolation badly enough to get drop
>the simplicity of PERFORM?  When the program isn't debugged?


I've seen monolithic COBOL programs with 500,000 source lines and COBOL's
traditional "planar" data storage.  It would indeed make such a program
easier both to debug and to maintain if the data were  "cospatial" with the
code, and is considered immaterial (or even nonexistent) if it's not
required to be "persistent" between procedure calls.

Also, on some machines, the amount of information that can be maintained
current has some limit, based perhaps on some memory allocation scheme.
Having data spaces associated automatically with subsections of the program
by the system software (including the compiler) drastically reduces these
resource requirements.

ALGOL, and its descendant Pascal, and even to some degree PL/1, demonstrated
that this approach to program design has much to recommend it, particularly
for very complex programs.

Consider, for example, a hypothetical COBOL compiler itself written in
COBOL.  Is there any particular necessity for the compiler variables
associated with PICTURE clause parsing to be "hanging around" while
generating code for a COMPUTE statement?  Would it not make debugging or
maintenance of such a hypothetical COBOL compiler easier if the maintenance
programmer didn't have to wade through all (or even any of)  the data
related to the last PICTURE clause that was parsed in order to find his
COMPUTE code generation problem?

Such are the mechanisms provided by "nested programs" introduced with the
1985 standard for COBOL, in response to the requests of those who had been
exposed to "block-structured" languages and found the benefits of such an
*automatic* association of data with code appropriate to the COBOL
environment.

        -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37972C34.86429137@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <UVJk3.971$ng2.5648@news2.mia> <37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n2o56$5l3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3794F514.18707BAB@home.com> <3795CD41.E65E9399@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > > Consider, for example, a hypothetical COBOL compiler itself
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> objects interface with everybody else's objects.

I think the point of having a open compiler is that new technology can
be developed without waiting for the compiler vendors to prove it is
possible.  This will effectively force the vendors to follow the real
users (us).

The point of having it in cobol source is that anyone that uses the
compiler will be able to modify the compiler to do what they want.

I believe that the simplest way (in the short term) is to have a cobol
precompiler (of sorts) that takes in 'GNU' Cobol and outputs to
whatever dialect your compiler supports.  Eventually this could create
actual run time code but that is a long way off IMHO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37977739.4AA803E8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <UVJk3.971$ng2.5648@news2.mia> <37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n2o56$5l3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3794F514.18707BAB@home.com> <3795CD41.E65E9399@NOSPAMhome.com> <37972C34.86429137@zip.com.au> <37974FEB.53BF7CDF@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> > > Why?  Who would buy this or put in a COBOL virtual machine?   COBOL is
> > > a compiled language anyway.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'compiled' if you wish).

Well, Rexx is interpreted, Pascal uses P-code, and Java uses a more
advanced kind of p-code.  The JVM understands Java Bytes and translates
them into machine language at run time.  A compiled language translates
its commands into machine language at compile time.  I know terms get
confusing as the technologies merge and separate, but this old
terminology is what I was meaning.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3794F418.33644953@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <UVJk3.971$ng2.5648@news2.mia> <37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n2o56$5l3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
One use that we have of nested programs at my current client is the
design of our callable date routine such that the source code for the
program is in a copybook in the copy library.  The "source library"
member is just a COPY datertn.  The normally batch use is as a
separately compiled and linked program.  The use in CICS is as a nested
program where the last two statements of the program are "COPY datertn."
followed by "END-PROGRAM calling-program-name".  If for performance
reasons we wanted to change a batch program to have this routine
embedded in it would be to add those two statements to the program and
recompile.  The difference in overhead between the intra-module call and
the dynamic call on IBM MVS had a fairly large per executed call
component (as opposed to the overhead for the initial establishment of
linkage).    

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <37937B17.2E9B2D6A@NOSPAMhome.com>...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> snip
Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services.
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** "Carlo Hogeveen" <hyphen@xs4all.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n027o$dic$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

It is called structured programming!

Having been Cobol 74 programmers for so long, we have become
blind to the concept of procedures with local variables.

Instead we bicker about whether to perform sections or paragraphs :-)

Sections and paragraphs are procedures without parameters or local variables.

Subroutines are procedures with both parameters and local variables,
and we "know" that subroutines are only useful when called by more
than one program, because subroutines are external, so we design them
that way, and it becomes a selffulfilling truth.

Well, we have Cobol 85 (and extensions) now with INTERNAL subroutines,
and there is a new truth in town.

Alas :-) Cobol 85 is backwards compatible with Cobol 74, so we can program
the way we used to, and what 's the use of these internal subroutines anyway?

In my case it is both a mental and a design problem.

First the mental problem.

In any other programming language I know, I  learned to use procedures
with local variables from the start and I wouldn't dream of putting all variables
in one place: my teachers would have (verbally) hurt me!

And yet, when I start to design a structured Cobol program
I keep coming up with Cobol 74 chunks: variableless procedures.

The way I try to break through this wall, is by first imagining how I would
attack the problem in structured language X, and then translate it in
my head to Cobol 85 procedures.

And now for the design problem.

What if we don't know any structured languages, how would we
find good candidates for Cobol 85 's internal subroutines?

My first rule: look at the data!

Is there data we can attach it's own program-specific procedure to?
I think of this as internal I/O: chunks that communicate with each other.
(Yeah, nicely heading towards OO, that can't hurt.).

Is there a record that must be handled in a for this program particular way?
Interface: record in, nothing out. Side effect: processing.
Local variables for dissecting and otherwise processing the record!

Is there a field that must be calculated using a for this program specific formula?
Interface: a few fields in, a result out. Local variables for intermediate results!

Is there a report to be printed?
Interface: fields for one detail-line in, nothing out. Side effect: report is printed.
Local variables for editing the detail-line to be printed and possibly for
printing page headers and accumulating and printing totals.

Page headers and totals are possible candidates for their own
procedures too.

All of the above are examples of possible program-specific subroutines.
Once a record is read, no two programs might handle it the same way.
No two programs will print the same report.
No two programs will handle the same screen.
A formula might be specific to a program or to an external subroutine.

And in general, of course, just like with external subroutines, we keep
in mind to search for procedures with high cohesion and low coupling.

Having said all this, of course sections and paragraphs won't both
disappear: they will still outnumber internal subroutines.


There can be a totally different reason to use internal subroutines
instead of external subroutines: speed!

A call to an internal subroutine is much faster that a call to an external subroutine.

One of our Cobol guru's extended the Cobol preprocessor to replace the
call to our most heavily used external subroutine, by building in and
calling that subroutine internally, and got a significant speed increase.


Carlo





Howard Brazee wrote in message <37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com>...
I'm a mainframe programmer who has never worked in a location which has
more than one COBOL program in one source member.

When we have called programs, it is because several programs will be
doing the calling.

I suppose the called program can be in a copy library, but I also
haven't worked where compile time calls were standard.

The one place I can see a use for this is if only one program does the
call, and a perform won't work because the called program and calling
program use different subschemata.  (having fun with that word -
schemata is a dictionary plural for schema!!)

What am I overlooking?  What are the shops I have worked in overlooked?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37939CFA.2E7EAC5D@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n027o$dic$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`

```
> And now for the design problem.
> 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> in mind to search for procedures with high cohesion and low coupling.

Thank-you.
 
> Having said all this, of course sections and paragraphs won't both
> disappear: they will still outnumber internal subroutines.
> 
> There can be a totally different reason to use internal subroutines
> instead of external subroutines: speed!

Yes, but I would expect that paragraphs have the same advantage over
internal subroutines.
 

I think the biggest difficulties in moving to internal sub-programs are:

1.  More coding = more work.
2.  The advantages haven't shown to be big enough to be worth while
doing things differently in the minds of people making standards or
expecting others do do maintenance.  Is this what the maintenance
programmers see as KISS?  It will be once it is widely accepted in your
shop, but not before.
3.  Documentation procedures were designed for paragraphs which don't
need documenting.
4.  Debugging procedures often work poorly with called programs, giving
errors at the wrong location.
5.  Nobody wants to be the first to test their pre-compiler when they
have a deadline to complete a program. 
6.  When you try to justify experimenting with this, people will ask
about speed effects.

On the other hand, once a few sub-programs have been written and
included, our desire to cut, paste, and modify will mean that the NEXT
report program will come much more quickly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379662EF.1E3945E3@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n027o$dic$1@news1.xs4all.nl> <37939CFA.2E7EAC5D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I only use COBOL because I have to.  (My present job is 75% COBOL,
25% ASM, so I have to...)  In the last two shops I've started
using internal subroutines.  And I got quite a battle.  Those who
have never programmed outside COBOL on one side, those who know
another language (Basic, C, Fortran, PL/I) on the other.

We examined the IBM VS COBOL compiler and the COBOL/390 (aka MVS
COBOL) compilers.  Looking at the generated code, you could not
see a calling sequence for an internal routine that was any worse
than a PERFORM.  In many cases, the compiler decided to "inline"
the subroutine to there was zero performance penalty.
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n0bt8$rh8$3@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The main reason that I have seen it used is for multi-threaded code,
particularly where the threads are event driven.  If you have, for example,
three windows all working together, it makes a lot of sense to write it as
three individual programs within one startup program.

#### ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** "Ed Milne" <emilne@ford.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n1vpv$o3p1@eccws1.dearborn.ford.com>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The easiest justification for using an internal program is when you have to
do the same process on multiple fields.  Instead of moving data back and
forth between common fields for processing, you can use multiple CALL
statements USING the different fields.

Ed Milne

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>I'm a mainframe programmer who has never worked in a location which has
>more than one COBOL program in one source member.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>What am I overlooking?  What are the shops I have worked in overlooked?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37972DAE.578BC6EF@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n1vpv$o3p1@eccws1.dearborn.ford.com> <37962417.61685ECF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ed Milne wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> internal programs)

current example:

Move screen-effective-date  to ws-validate-date
perform p100-validate-date
move ws-validate-return     to screen-effective-date

module example:

call 'validdt'  using screen-effective-date
                      screen-effective-date

cost nil: 'call' to 'perform' and it removes the move statements.
-  negative run time.
-  Once you are used to doing this it is actually clearer (less code)

Jarred's note implies that the cobol compiler may eliminate the
perform entirely making the call actually faster (if the source of
'validdt' is inside the same program.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple programs in one source

- **From:** Patrick <pwm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932827205.25922@www.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<37932F22.A4751958@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Programs can be "nested" since 1985. A nested program could
be a copybook. The advantage of a separate program over a
section or paragraph that is performed is "abstraction." A
separate program is not limited to processing only the data
defined within the program, it is capable of processing any
data passed to it by the calling program, whatever program
that might be.

Someone Important
XYZ Corporation

Dear XYZ Hiring Executive,

Let's slash and burn the prancing and dancing and cut right to the action
item on the table.  I'm ready to roll up my sleeves and put my can-do
attitude to use, moving forward with the XYZ Team (XYZT) to get closure
on XYZ's conversion from Corporation to Winner.  If you book me as an
asset to the XYZT, my Hands On/No Fuss philosophy will help throw XYZT
problems over the fence.  Often cited and criticized as a "Motivationalist",
by my less-than-can-do coworkers, I take pride in my ability to stay
busified
even in times of what the motivationally disabled refer to as "down time".
In my book, the only thing "down" is their attitudes.  Don't get me wrong,
I'm a team player.  Sometimes I'm the teamest player there is.  However,
when the ball is in my court I take it and run with it.  Deadbeats be
damned.
If my teammates aren't willing to run with me, its time to outsource or
downsize them to a more deserving task, like emptying the circular files.
I believe that the deserving employee is the serving employee.

I am self conscious about "selling myself" to corporations, because by
nature I am a modest person.  However, I can safely say that my skills far
exceed the requirements for any position in XYZ.  With a lifetime of
Personnel skills under my belt, I'm a hypertextual interface between worker
and management, between pigeon and fox.  My employers, colleagues, and
childhood friends will attest to my tenacious "go getter" attitude when it
comes to climbing, shining, or moving people out of the way on the corporate
ladder.  I am a furiously adept Multitasker with years of experience in
diversification and tackling a variety of workable items.  I have a long
history with computers and can relate to the laid back feel of the programs
that the XYZ team develops.  I want to participate in transforming the
Internet into the Interestingnet. To say the least, my technical computer
skills
are MicroMacro.  Overall, my ability to Achieve will surpass your
expectations.

Despite my aggressive and sometimes obsessive approach to employment, I
like to kick back and enjoy time off, almost as much as I relish hard work.
I love to Farfenugen myself out of the rat-race each weekend with the Yakima
racks loaded and a stoked feeling in me that only the anticipation of
X-treme
sports can bring.  Whenever I'm not rock climbing, kayaking, snowboarding,
mountain biking, white-water rafting, roller blading, bungie jumping, or
training for marathons I like to attend wilderness survival camps, men's
drum
groups, swing-dance competitions, and Jazz shows at coffee shops.  At home,
I enjoy brewing fine microbrews, watching independent films, and practicing
the guitar with my band The RetroRockets.  Currently, the RetroRockets are
negotiating with a major record label and video producer, further attesting
to my ability to succeed in and out of the workplace.

Without lowering myself to obloquies about the modern American work ethic,
I must say that right now things in corporate America stink.  Its time to
sacrifice the passing-fad individualistic attitude of the past and move
forward
into a bright new future of corporate leadership.  Take me on board and see
the
true potential of a diligent can-do employee with the know-how to succeed.

enclosures: no enclosed resume, my attitude speaks for itself

Sincerely,

## Thread: Environment stability — 37 msgs

### Re: Environment stability

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> NetExpress.

I have seen systems which are no longer maintainable according to the
companies using them.  Some of them aren't Y2K compatible, others won't
work with Windows 95 or NT, etc.   A friend of mine gets all of the
business he wants by specializing in dBASE & Clipper maintenance.
 
> There are incompatibilities with upgrades. The biggest nightmare for
> compiler developers must be this building-in of backwards compatibility.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'old' versions of COBOL continue to be supported.

Start charging for support, and then support these as long as the
support pays for itself.  Lots of shops converted their ANSI 68 code
only for Y2K.
 
But the cycle for supporting PC programmers for "obsolete" compilers is
much shorter.


> Perhaps, instead of looking at the "throw-away" concept, as a group, we
> should be looking for "forwards-compatibiliity" of COBOL compilers.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> variants on mainframes.

Java is an attempt to do this, as is Windows.  But neither has
stabilized so that 5 year old calls are still the way to do things.
 

> (a) File handling : Using COBOL syntax, you write a data file and if you
> send it to me on a diskette I can't read it on my PC using COBOL, and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> MAKE-DIRECTORY(FOLDER) ?

I have long wanted better ISAM and hierarchical database commands (which
can be used elsewhere).  What does CHANGE-DIRECTORY mean in MVS?  Maybe
the concept of COBOL on a VAX is a better idea - the FD can refer to the
name, or DCL can redirect the name to a different name.  I want to run a
generic COBOL program with run-time determination of the file name,
directory name, etc.  Maybe we need a standard batch language (IBM tried
to do this with REXX).  Speaking of Rexx, when it tries to do
environment specific commands, all of a sudden it stops being clear.
 
> (b) Printing : Why re-invent the wheel each time somebody wants to WRITE
> from COBOL. Couldn't there be a COBOL standard to link to print-drivers
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> pushing it. The latter could be covered by third-party toolkits.

Since graphics are wanted, and run-time printer commands are wanted,
maybe COBOL should be designed with an interface to talk with a run-time
printer/display program.  One user  may print landscape, another wants
two-sided with space for hole punches, another wants green headings, and
another wants his report to page on his monitor.  I suppose output could
be designed around the concept of spread sheets, with spots for
calculations (page totals) which are done on print time.

Start with the assumption that the COBOL program should not be required
to know the page length or width.
 
> (c) Screen Handling : (Don't know if this has any relevance to
> mainframe). SCREEN SECTION - Was(is) a fairly flexible COBOL tool -
> within DOS I was able to emulate 'Windows' menu-bars and pop-ups.
> Perhaps the Screen Section should be the base module for a COBOL
> specific solution to GUIs regardless of platform.

Depends on your mainframe.   A common interface here would be desired
too.   Most mainframe screens which I have used are pre-defined screens.
 
> (d) Sorting - check that on-going thread - the problems with sorting on
> mainframes. Sorting in COBOL - should be one language, one solution.

I have never seen any variations in my COBOL sort logic.
 
> (e) I'll throw it in here, because it's relevant at this point, but I
> cover it in more detail later. Object Orientation - I have a real
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> things straight is :-

Even without OO, languages such as C have an infinite number of
commands.  If you learn one library, someone can create another
library.  Or change an existing library.
 
> "Open COBOL - A Definition
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Linux - will that give us Corel Windows as well as Microsoft Windows.

Interesting

> > The claims made by OO advocates are unconvincing to me.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to show us how it would work".

Of course.  My Masters was on OO, and I know how much of a religion this
is.  

I don't think that's quite what I meant to imply though.  It was more of
the claim that objects can be adjusted to our new environment so that
the code won't be obsolete.  I don't trust a debugged object to stay
debugged in a complex environment, nor do I trust a changed object to
work everywhere it's invoked in a complex environment.
 
> Hope I'm not trying to teach my grandmother to suck eggs - but I'm
> assuming others are asking for this information. Well I'll try and keep
> this as short as I can and I've attached a zip file showing how I use OO
> for file handling.

I'm looking forward to studying what I downloaded.  I haven't done any
OO Cobol, (in the last 10 years my only COBOL has been mainframe in
environments where there IS not OO COBOL).
```

#### ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>> (b) Printing : Why re-invent the wheel each time somebody wants to WRITE
>> from COBOL. Couldn't there be a COBOL standard to link to print-drivers
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>calculations (page totals) which are done on print time.

The problem with your suggestion gentlemen, is that not all platforms has a
standardized printer driver interface like we know it from Windows, OS/2.
Postscript is an alternative approach, it does however have its downsides as
well.

I am desperately trying to get a hold of the new draft to read about the
Report Section enhancments, I have been told there are some suggestions in
the direction of providing font support. Without having read the draft, I
have a hard time understand how this should be possible to do.

Now, understand me correct, anything can be done, as long as one is not
considering portability. To target one single issue from the platforms I am
experienced with. Dos and Windows. While the latter has a driver scenario
accomplishing the task of configuring a printer to use font A or B, the
first one does not. What would the Cobol developer company then do? Write th
eir own driver for that platform? For how many printers? It is indeed a
doable task, however it will be huge and incredible expensive. Another
downside with this is that if this really happened, how much is portability
worth, if the various Cobol vendors approach have small but important
differences within. Let say something as simple as margins. One vendor would
for instance choose to leave the margins as is, leaving to the Cobol
developer to choose these, another would by default set margins as low as
possible. The third vendor may choose to reset margins to printer defaults.
What if the chosen font is not available for the selected
printer/environment.

As I said, I have yet not read the draft, by some obscure reason I get
access denied when I go to the web address that Mr Klein has been so kind to
share with us. Once I have got a hold of it and read it, I will give you my
2 cents. But I must admit that I have my doubt.

My view is that these kind of things should be left as a third party
product. Like Formprint (not that I have tried it, but the concept is
sound).

>Start with the assumption that the COBOL program should not be required
>to know the page length or width.

Agree, that should be left to the print engine.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:

> >Since graphics are wanted, and run-time printer commands are wanted,
> >maybe COBOL should be designed with an interface to talk with a run-time
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> well.

I don't want a printer interface so much as a report interface.  Maybe
define columns, headers, and footers in a report writer or Easyrieve
style, but leave out page sizes, fonts, etc.  Possibly include some
pseudo variables, but maybe not.

The report program knows how to format the headers, totals, footers, etc
for the page size, medium, and even typeface desired by the user.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3793CC92.B80B5051@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
How about an interface to XML?

XML is a HTML which can do type setting if needed.  Let the Report
Writer section generate lines and breaks.  Let it also generate
tags to go with the headings and details.  Provide a standard set
of tag names defined to work in a line printer environment.  Let
the compiler implementor deliver those tags, integrated into some
serial output scheme going directly to a dumb printer.

The report writer section would have all the stuff to count lines,
etc. as it does now.  Likewise, it does grouping and breaks.  Now
the extensions need to support two modes.

First, the compiler (or run time) needs the find out some basic
measures like how much vertical space the heading and detail line
will take, how big the page is, and them it can do all the
calculations for vertical layout.  Same for horizontal.  This
information can be derived from the name of the tags associated
with each part of the report.  For example, a heading line can
have one of H1 H2 H3 for the tag.  H1 can be defined by the
standard as 250% size of basic line, H2 200%, etc.  Another set of
tags for the basic line can say 10 point, 12 point, 16 cpi, etc. 
Again, the standard defines the a set of tags and dimensions for
each.

The second mode is type set mode when the tags are imbedded in the
data stream with the print characters.  Run time also needs to
know that it should suppress some breaks (say page breaks) because
the type setter will determine where they fall.  The type setter
will see tags around the heading lines, the data lines, etc. 
These tags will be chosen by the programmer written in extensions
"TAGS are <ASD> <ASD>"  to report writer syntax.  Understand that
typeset mode will require 1) coordination between the programmer
and type setter over the names/definitions of the tags, and 1) a
separate typeset program outside the compiler.  This program
should be XML compliant (to a XML subset defined in the COBOL
standard.  I add this last part because XML is so darn flexible
that the COBOL program won't be able to use every XML feature due
to COBOL report write definition limitations)   

For Typeset mode and an enhanced printer, the compiler generated
output delivers tags as well as the data in a stream which can
them be feed to a print/browse program.  In a windows environment,
this would be IE5 (or the underlying pieces of it).  In a MVS
environment, this could be AFP API calls and formdefs/pagedefs.

For basic mode, I would expect some environment related support in
particular compilers.  For example, an IBM MVS environment
compiler would probably provide generated code/runtime code that
would call the AFP APIs based on the tags.  A PC oriented compiler
product would probably provide I/O routines that use the tags to
generate some PCL (HP printer control language) so the program
produced a data stream that can go straight to the printer.  

The most primitive compiler i/o would need to use some common
white space characters (LF, CR, HT, BS) to produce something like
an old TTY would print, or carriage control and TRC like the IBM
3800 uses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37947871.465377E4@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <3793CC92.B80B5051@techie.com>`

```
Jared Thomas wrote:
> 
> How about an interface to XML?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> serial output scheme going directly to a dumb printer.

This has lots of possibilities.  HTML and now XML are ubiquitous with
tools virtually everybody can play with.  We think of the front end as
database tools, but COBOL extraction is sometimes more appropriate.  But
report formats certainly don't need to be controlled the way the data
extraction is.  I like it.
```

>I hope not.  If XML is the output for the COBOL, then the tool using XML
>will adapt the page size to the user's needs and to selected forms.  I'm

OK as I understand XML (which is limited based upon what I have read).

You can output tags (the data with names associated to it) and then
integrate this with a format (like your invoice).  Since the two are
actually separated you can dynamically modify your format, including
dropping fields that are not interesting to you on this particular
field.  You may have view 1 for one user and view 2 for a second user.

Sounds great, have not really read through the details just the
disguised marketing blurbs

Ken

I do have a believe in this approach, we just have to be aware the
limitations. Another issue is the interpreter of this stuff. If one were to
use a browser, that means one will have to print to a file and thereafter
launch the browser with print instruction. That implies no control
whatsoever of the execution of the print itself.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n273a$a71$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>I don't want a printer interface so much as a report interface.  Maybe
>define columns, headers, and footers in a report writer or Easyrieve
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>for the page size, medium, and even typeface desired by the user.

This is all fine until you start mentioning:
    page sizes
    fonts
    typeface

To control these attributes of a printer you will need a command language.
That is what a printer interface does. If not using a printer interface, you
will have to maintain your own, an awfully tedious and expensive task.

Consider a Postscript printer, an IBM Matrix printer, a Canon ink printer
and a HP Laser III. Not to forget that out there is a number of 7 bit
printers still going around.

You speak of the tasks as they are simple, well I can tell you that to set
the proper font, typeface and page size of a HP Laser III you will need to
send a large bunch of escape sequences if not using a common printer driver
interface. For the printers named above, you would have to multiply this by
4. Knowing that the world is crowded with proprietary printer instruction
set makes me feel sorry for those guys to implement that stuff.

No way, managing the printer device settings like page size, typeface, font,
graphics belongs to the OS and should never be dealed with by the
application itself, that is a step back if not two.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3794A90B.1B6793CF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n273a$a71$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Those should be handled by the browser or Crystal Reports, or whatever
program the user has on his PC.  Not by the COBOL program which is
designed to be more stable than the current way the user wants to view
the data.

Nobody needs to know escape sequences in this scenario.  Whomever wrote
the interface program (say Crystal Reports or Netscape) needed to know
them.  The user uses standard Windows commands to pick his WYSIWYG
display and prints from there.

The COBOL application should neither know nor care what type of
printer/monitor is being used to display the report.  It should provide
some standard (XML?) formatted data which  can be read by the user's
tool of choice and printed with the user's needs in mind.

That tool also needs to have a repository of forms which can be
maintained across the enterprise.  If they want to print a W2 or a
Packing slip, they need to use that form.  Or they can pick a standard
report form or design the report they want.  We don't care, our job was
to provide the data.

Cheesle wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com>...
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n4l0f$9bv$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n273a$a71$1@news.cerf.net> <3794A90B.1B6793CF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <3794A90B.1B6793CF@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Those should be handled by the browser or Crystal Reports, or whatever
>program the user has on his PC.  Not by the COBOL program which is
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>to provide the data.

The problem with a solution depending on the use of Crystal Reports or a
browser is that they are not communicating. That's what I like with
Formprint. You load a report on the demand from the user, and fill it with
data directly from the Cobol application, you do not go via disk files and
continue to run without having any idea of what actually happened. Besides,
nor CR or a browser solution gives you field by field control. Also, why XML
in such a case? The XML is only good for the browser, given a solution that
identifies the field you would get less overhead.

Pseudo code for how I think it should be:
    Call LoadDLL
    Call LoadForm form_name
    Call PreparePage
    Perform until finished
        Call PutData "MyFieldIdentifier" "ThisIsMyData"
    Call ShowData
    If not Successful
        RollBack
    Call UnloadDLL

All logic related to the form should be stored in the form description file
and used by the form application with the essential difference from Crystal
Reports and Browser that there is a real time dialog field by field between
the print engine and the Cobol application. Simply said, I would equal the:
    Call PutData field_identifier data_item
with:
    WRITE printer_record FROM data_item

In this case the form engine should find the data_item in the form
identified by printer_record's sub items and do what the form description
says about it.

The print engine should be equal to the Report Writer in the sense of taking
care of all thinkable attributes.

A Crystal Reports, browser solution can not do this.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL Report Writer and XML (was Environment stability)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37965D71.D5F29411@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n273a$a71$1@news.cerf.net> <3794A90B.1B6793CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n4l0f$9bv$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
This subject takes some research on my part.  I know what I want
to do, but I can't produce correct code off the top of my head for
Report Writer and XML.  So I going to do these posts in parts.

Part 1 -- Theory of Documents

A document has pieces.  Each piece is delimited and named (i.e.
tagged).

Using HTML like syntax (crossed with IBM DCF aka Script son of
Un*x roff), here is an example.

<DOC NAME=asdf>
<TITLE>Document Title</TITLE>
<H1>Outermost heading</H1>
<P>A paragraph</P>
<LIST BULLETED>
<LI>Item 1
<LI>Item 2
<LIST NUMBERED>
<LI>Subitem 2a
</LIST>
</LIST>
</DOC>

For a report, you might see
 
<REPORT>
<H1>Report Head
<H2>Group Head
<LINES>
detail line 1
detail line 2
<T2>
Group totals line 1
Group totals line 2
<LINES>
detail line 3
<T2>
Group totals line 1
Group totals line 2
<T1>
Report totals
</REPORT>


Now when you print on a page, if you know the page dimensions as
well as the dimensions of each line, then you are in.  With
standard height lines, you count lines just like report writer has
been doing since day 0.

The next advance is when you know the dimensions, say in points or
% of line heights or something else you can turn into inches.  The
layout is similar to counting lines, but you have factions of a
line (i.e. inches or cm).  But there is not a big problem figuring
if a group will fit on the remain page, or if a detail line will
be widowed or orphaned.

You can also replace the <tags> with escape sequences for a
printer, if you know something about the hardware.  I'd expect a
COBOL compiler for common use on a platform to know a little bit
about HP PCL printers, IBM 3800 printers, AFP printers, TTYs, what
ever matches the platform where the compiler is going to be
sold/used a lot.

But what if you don't know escape sequences?  Enter XML. 

Then you turn the job over to an external program.  I'll use a WWW
browser as an example.

If the tags with the correct HTML ones (<HTML> <HEAD> </HEAD>
<BODY> <CENTER> <TABLE> <SMALL> <P> </BODY> and a host of others)
then the output could be sent to a file, and that file loaded into
the browser.  The browser PRINT button could give hard copy.

Note that the browser doesn't have to insert page breaks.  WWW
browsers don't when displaying on the screen, and can when
printing.  When an external program is processing the COBOL
program output, some cooperation is needed so that report writer
does not insert page breaks if the typesetter does not want them
(e.g. screen display) or will decide itself where to put them. 
For example, it can format a report writer group to see how big it
is, then decide based on the current paper in use if it will fit
on this page or the next.  

The chief objection to WWW Browsers is that they lay out a page as
the choose to be viewed on a screen in a variable sized window. 
Most people would like to print to a fixed size piece of paper and
put print lines exactly where they want (fill in a form).

To overcome this, XML is being deployed.  GML (IBM DCF/Bookmaster)
HTML, XML, and others are defined by a very complex standard
called SGML.  SGML in all it's glory and complexity is used in
some type setting applications as well as others (indexing,
viewing, audio readers).  Being too complex to implement with good
performance and easy, SGML has been used to define XML.  XML is
itself a way to define tags, and how they are to be processed. 
That processing can include pixel resolution, type styles, style
sheets.  Almost anything you can ask a type setting program to do
can be marked up in XML.

Filling out forms is trivial IF you have a set of tags defined
which understand where things are on the form.  For last printing
subsystem I did, I caused the programmers to tag everything
including return addresses and zip codes.  The printing system
counted "to" addresses to see how many copies, printed BAR codes
for mailing from the ZIP codes, and even sorted by ZIP code for
the cheaper mailing rates.  Programmers first ask "why can't I
just tag an address as a bunch of lines (with line breaks)".  I
explained that the tag was not formatting directions (don't flow
these lines) but usage information (this is mailing ZIP info).

So now we face the problem of everyone and his sibling defining
tags and their implementation.  That's where the COBOL report
writer standard will have to exert some controls like "The report
writer expects the tag definition to include/not include these
actions".

For example, a tag that report writer will insert to delimit a
group could be required to maintain vertical alignment of the
enclosed lines so that a font with variable width characters can
be used, and duplicate information on lines within a group can be
blanked out on lines after the first.  (There is better way to
handle this problem -- see tables).

Next I want to conjure some report writer syntax and maybe a set
of tags with known dimensions in another post.  Feel free to type
yourself!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Report Writer and XML (was Environment stability)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n7g3m$8l3$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n273a$a71$1@news.cerf.net> <3794A90B.1B6793CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n4l0f$9bv$1@news.cerf.net> <37965D71.D5F29411@techie.com>`

```
Jared Thomas wrote in message <37965D71.D5F29411@techie.com>...
>Now when you print on a page, if you know the page dimensions as
>well as the dimensions of each line, then you are in.

This is the interesting part, from where do you get that? May be I am wrong
but I understand your approach as you think of using a web browser to do the
print, how would you let the user select device? Through the browser? May
one assume such a function available through a API?
Once the device is selected, would you also be able to obtain the page
primitives? From where? The browser?

>The next advance is when you know the dimensions, say in points or
>% of line heights or something else you can turn into inches.  The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>be widowed or orphaned.

I guess you will also have to know the font size of the device in order to
calculate this stuff. Are you into any presumptions regarding fonts?

>COBOL compiler for common use on a platform to know a little bit
>about HP PCL printers, IBM 3800 printers, AFP printers, TTYs, what
>ever matches the platform where the compiler is going to be
>sold/used a lot.

First, I would not expect the compiler to know it, possibly the runtime. On
the other hand, I disagree with you here. It is not the task of nor a
compiler or a runtime to know a printer.

>But what if you don't know escape sequences?  Enter XML.

I can't see what good XML does here, it is device independent, hence, how
can it possible format anything to a preprinted form?

If you make this and it targets my doubts, I am really impressed!

>Filling out forms is trivial IF you have a set of tags defined
>which understand where things are on the form.

I doubt.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL Report Writerand XML

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379660F4.3C1CB14F@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n273a$a71$1@news.cerf.net> <3794A90B.1B6793CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n4l0f$9bv$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
1.  We are using a browser like IE5 in this discussion because a)
It exists now, a) It supports XML, and b) People are familiar with
WWW browsers.  The actual program interfaced to Report Writer
could be anything...

2.  There is actually an interactive form for browsers.  In fact,
many.  The oldest (I think) is CGI with HTML Post.  The markup
defines fields that the user can fill in, and buttons to click to
send (post) the data.

The result is that the server which supplied the markup gets a
message back from the browser with the posted data and runs an
application to process it and create more HTML.

ASP Active Server Pages are another way.  M$ Front Page extensions
and ActiveX controls are another.  Netscape and IE ship with
scripting languages.  Many people are their ahead of us.

3.  For true interaction, I see a conflict with the SCREEN
Section.  One set of requirements is an interactive GUI, and I say
Go For It.  Just remember the other requirement for interaction
with Report Writer.  Their probably out to be a common thread in
the Language Designer's mind about both...

The last time I noticed the interactive screen problem was for
CICS on the mainframe.  97% of the CICS work is outside COBOL,
i.e. in the CICS preprocessor for COBOL and the CICS BMP support
for screen definitions.  To some extent, I hear you calling for an
integrated solution for the CICS problem which could be applied to
a WWW browser or other PC interactive interface as well as CICS.

XML would work as a scripting thing for a browser type program
which could interact with COBOL Report Writer.


PS: Add a tag line please, Sr. Cheesle, so we can get an e-mail
address for you.

Cheesle wrote:
{snip}

> All logic related to the form should be stored in the form description file
> and used by the form application with the essential difference from Crystal
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37997BE3.62E7788C@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n273a$a71$1@news.cerf.net> <3794A90B.1B6793CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <7n4l0f$9bv$1@news.cerf.net> <37989e41.12657476@news.enter.net>`

```

XML is a great interchange between disparate systems (or will be when
people actually figure it out).

To see a discussion regarding XML for purely rendering reasons then
see 

HTTP://photo.net/wtr/thebook

Click on the HTML in 21 minutes and look for his XML diatribe.

While your there read the book, I breezed in there recently and this
is the best discussion on building an intelligent web site that I have
seen.

Regarding the need to know the page size.  You should not period.  Yes
the concept sounds good but mostly you want to know this to allow
grouping lines together on a single page and this could be handled by
the fundamental layout tool (internal or external).

Where it is interesting is when you want a true form and then you
already know the page size anyway.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3794F3C8.A317EAE2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee and Cheesle wrote :-

<much snippage>

On topic of printing, problems, difficulties, suggestions and how it
would fit into R/W COBOL-2000. I will confine myself to printing but the
same applies to other COBOL modules.

Gentlemen,

My quotes below are from somebody who is interested but would prefer to
be anonymous.

Read your pros and cons and suggestions with interest. Mine is a systems
background, so I tend to view things from there.

	Phase 1 - Let's all pick a pot of paint, any colour except
	white and a very broad brush. Discuss the merits/demerits of
	can we create a 'beastie' that can fly. Daub as much paint as
	you like on the canvas. We come to the conclusion it might fly
	- then somebody takes a can of white paint, and over the
	existing mess, frames in our final thoughts. Now we can move on. 
	If we agree it wont fly, then just burn the canvas.

	Phase 2 - Fresh canvas, paint, but smaller brushes. Now we
	get down to discussing what the wings, fuselage, rudder of this
	'beastie' will look like. Prototypes designed, tested and we
	find it really will fly.

	Phase 3 - Now using very fine brushes - let's get down to the 
	nitty-gritty and code programs.

I have to use my premise about OPEN COBOL and Object Orientation as
being the best approach through the three phases.

> Grace once talked about one compiler and different input routines for > different application needs. At that time it was important NOT to gore > someone else's ox.  Instead we helped.......

At the current time McroFocus has the following classes - Base 46, GUI
120, OLE 48 OLE Automation 6. Assume if we take the whole COBOL clan
from mainframe thru Unix to PCs, and come up with an horrendous total of
5,000 classes. No, I don't and you don't need to store that total number
on our machines - just those class-groups applicable to our environment.
But each class-group may have cross-overs to utility-class-groups
applicable to all flavours of COBOL. And as Ken Fosey rightly added, in
a separate message, we need a Standard Template

In the context of printing - MicroFocus provide the following
compiler/platform specific PC_PRINT routines :-

        Print a file, Close a printer channel, Send a printer command to 
	a printer, Free bitmap from memory, Get printer information,
	Load bitmap into memory, Open a printer channel, Set printer 	
	color, Set printer font, Write text to a printer, Write bitmap
	to a printer

They make no claim that this is all-singing/all-dancing, just covers the
basics. With perhaps the exception of bitmaps, isn't this what we all
need. As Bill Klein has already indicated, much of this is in COBOL-2000
for R/W.

Graphics, (both printed and screen) - I think - would be best left to
toolkit-providers, writing graphics-classes, because this is a very
specilaised area - and I'm mathematically-challenged ! Although :-

> O.K. maybe my attempts at comparisons are far fetched.  Maybe not.
> I remember telling an ACM session on Graphics that this was something > COBOL could use.  They laughed. With the help of Prozac so do I.



 
I have Red Hat Linux, but haven't loaded it. (I am real leery about
partioning my disk for two opertating systems). They are not well
categorised from the manual but there are hundreds of utilities/toolkits
to do what is necessary for windowing, printing etc. If I was to look, I
expect the tookits would rate from -1 to +10.

As a broad concept, you trigger COBOL on your machine, using a JCL
command, command-line or file parameter, telling  COBOL Base Class which
operating system you are functioning in. This sets flags to be able to
invoke, from the great mass, those class-groups which are applicable to
your O/S.



As Cheesle pointed out - print-drivers for PCs are one thng, but for
other machines ?
> 
> Cheesle wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> for the page size, medium, and even typeface desired by the user.

I've done a quick count on NetExpress and these are the approx number of
classes in each Group :-

	Base		 46
	GUI		120
	OLE		 48
	OLE Automation	  6
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n4m3a$9mb$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <3794F3C8.A317EAE2@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <3794F3C8.A317EAE2@home.com>...
>My quotes below are from somebody who is interested but would prefer to
>be anonymous.

That's alright.

>Graphics, (both printed and screen) - I think - would be best left to
>toolkit-providers, writing graphics-classes, because this is a very
>specilaised area - and I'm mathematically-challenged ! Although :-

I heartfully agree on this one, although my sometimes poor English limits me
from expressing my views correctly, this is what I have been trying to say.

Extending the Cobol syntax to deal with this stuff would be a mess,
particularly because not all platforms do have a common printer interface.

I may add that the origin of my entusiasm in this debate was a discussion
between Mr. Klein and another fellow about the font statement in the new
draft. I was commenting on that one because I could not see how the various
Cobol companies would be able to deliver that on each and every platform
without a simple interface to deal with. For Windows it is ok, but for other
platforms? No way. Actually it may be a problem with Windows as well. It is
my opinion that a font statement is too hardware related to be portable,
hence it should not be a part of the Cobol draft.

When this is said, I also have to admit that I belong to those (may be I am
the only one) that have never seen the big deal with Report Section. What it
does is easily accomplished through ordinary programming, and what it does
not do is a whole lot. Ok, ok, I realize I am dancing in dangerous waters
now, it was not my intention to start a war, however I stand by my view.

I have often wanted report capability in Cobol, but I understand that my
wishes/needs extends far beyond the capability of the Report Section and in
that sense it would only complicate the picture even more. So, homemade is
better. Formprint by Flexus is a good solution, however not perfect in my
view. There is a new contributor coming soon, with features that is giving
it all to the Cobol developer, except the tedious part of designing the
form. In my view it is way better than any report engine I have seen yet.
Well, I have already said more than I should, but this is the approach I
believe in for Cobol, leave the report engines as an outside utility, not
integrated, that would ruin portability and simplicity.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379607E8.3214FC43@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <3794F3C8.A317EAE2@home.com> <7n4m3a$9mb$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
> When this is said, I also have to admit that I belong to those (may be I am
> the only one) that have never seen the big deal with Report Section. What it
> does is easily accomplished through ordinary programming, and what it does
> not do is a whole lot. Ok, ok, I realize I am dancing in dangerous waters
> now, it was not my intention to start a war, however I stand by my view.
 that would ruin portability and simplicity.
> 
> Cheesle

Report Writer advocates wonder why they are so much in the minority. 
One reason is that  the improvements which Report Writer offers aren't
great enough to change programming practices (see PL/1).  And ALL COBOL
programmers need to have the skills to write reports without report
writer, but many do not know report writer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n7nk7$58i@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7n54bd$e13$1@news.cerf.net> <19990722004616.05850.00001048@ng-ce1.aol.com> <AsFl3.2185$xN4.20419@news3.mia>`

```
I am not going to "show you the code" - but if you have a multi-page report
with headers and footers - including the page number in one of them and
multi-level groups (with sub-totals), then report writer really CAN do this
in a FEW lines (with minimal testing or opportunities for errors) while
"standard" (i.e. non-RW) code requires lots of page-break logic, group
logic, sub-total variables, calculations, etc.  If you really want to see an
example, look back a few months to the question on multi-level report
processing (in DejaNews) can compare the "normal" code with the RW solution.
Of particular interest was that one of our (regular and usually accurate)
posters made a logic error in the accumulation processing - which RW
couldn't allow (unless the compiler had a bug).
```

> Would you care to give us an example where 'thousands' of lines of

I've seen cases where thousands of lines of code could be replaced by a
few dozen lines of code, without adding any new tools.

The reason RW is so little used is that ANSI (and more important FIPS) mad
it optional.  IBM marketing took this as an opportunity to charge extra
for it.  Shops that weren't using it, or were using it very little, or
were cheap, didn't pay for To:

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37972A8F.452157A4@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net> <379366FD.568AF8CF@NOSPAMhome.com> <3794F3C8.A317EAE2@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> 
> Graphics, (both printed and screen) - I think - would be best left to
> toolkit-providers, writing graphics-classes, because this is a very
> specilaised area - and I'm mathematically-challenged ! Although :-

The graphics images should be able to be called into a document via a
defined interface.  For example:  AFP allows you to load a PSEG or
HTML allows you to imbed a graphics file.  The underlying format
engine must be able to draw that and use it.  It is simply the best
way to do forms and such.

In a complex world why don't we use a chain of programs in the classic
Unix method cobol output's HTML or Tex or script and is formatted by
the next program.  The language elements must be portable but the
method of implementation does not.  A classic example of this is
indexed files, no two vendors do it the same way but the code is
portable from compiler to compiler.
 

PS:  James,

To reply to the OO templates thread, me too.  (I could not in all
conscience do this separately).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Environment stability

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379A431B.881C7C03@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com> <37932DDF.41797291@NOSPAMhome.com> <7mvk3a$d8j$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Re : Messages between Howard Brazee and Cheesle regarding printing, and
my goofed message where I accidentally sent the incomplete draft.

Sorry gentlmen,

I'm not going to pursue Open COBOL/OO here at this stage. No, that isn't
a cop-out. It had been my intention to hang my hat on "printing" as a
topic, but your messages have well and truly illustrated that it
wouldn't bode well to use printing as the basis for expressing an idea.

Nothing of what you wrote touches on Phase 1 and some, I would suggest,
occurs in my Phase 2, the majority is the nitty-gritty of Phase 3.

It was only yesterday that I hit on a simpler topic to develop my
argument and I will be able to illustrate it better with code as it is
applicable to all flavours of COBOL - given they all had the 'basics' of
OO.

There are plenty of folks out there prepared to come fortward for the
prosecution. As attorney for the defence, I need time to marshal my
thoughts on paper and gather together some witnesses for the defence.
Could take me another 2 - 3 months.

Nevertheless, was interested in the points you both were making about
printing.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
