###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Game of life - in COBOL?

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZMnl3.660$4o4.21691@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7n21bs$bme$1@endevour.netg.se> <37954a2b.4370081@news1.ibm.net> <3795F73B.CB776B47@dhfs.state.wi.us>`

```
Suzanne Higgins wrote in message <3795F73B.CB776B47@dhfs.state.wi.us>...
>not to be really obtuse or show my depraved childhood to the world, but
>what is the game of life??

I'm not sure it meets the definition of "game", but in a two dimensional
array, elements are either alive or dead (or "X" or space  OR 1 or 0
depending on how you want to represent it).  In going from one generation to
the next, a live cell (i.e. array element) stays alive if surrounded (one
"square" in any of 8 possible directons) by 2 or 3 live cells, a dead cell
becomes alive if surrounded by exactly 3 live cells, and all other
combinations will kill a cell.  "Out of bounds" cells are treated as dead.
In a very simple example (called traffic lights) if on a chessboard only the
cells b1, b2, and b3 are alive, the next generation will see a2 and c2 come
alive while b1 and b3 (only one live neighbor each) will die.  The cell b2
stays alive.  Successive generations will cause the live cells to go from a
row of three vertical to three horizontal or vice versa.  I recall seeing
this in a Scientific American article sometimes in the late 1960's.
>
>> >Is there anyone who has produced one of those Game of Life (John Conway)
in
>> >COBOL. Because I would really like to see one, I have found them in
JAVA,
>> >C++ and PASCAL but so far none in COBOL. Thankful for help.


www.hiring.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Game of life - in COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Eurl3.679$4o4.22550@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7n21bs$bme$1@endevour.netg.se> <37954a2b.4370081@news1.ibm.net> <3795F73B.CB776B47@dhfs.state.wi.us> <ZMnl3.660$4o4.21691@axe.netdoor.com> <37962B53.C67E3AC0@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <37962B53.C67E3AC0@home.com>...
>Warren Porter wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I'm not sure it meets the definition of "game", but in a two dimensional
array ......
>
>Like Suzanne I don't want to appear obtuse (thick), but is what you are
>describing a fancy name, (or implementation), for Tic-Tac-Doe or Noughts
>and Crosses. If it is Tic-Tac, the original enquirer should get a copy
>of the Microfocus program TicTac.cbl.

It is normally "Played" on a much larger grid, something like 20x20, perhaps
larger.  It is nothing like Tic-Tac-Toe, Pente, GO, or chess.  After
entering the initial data, the "game" consists of pressing a key to force a
display of the next generation.  A different key would take one out of the
program.  There is no "opponent" as such, the user just sits back and
watches the blinkin' lights as patterns grow, die, or reach something
stable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Game of life - in COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "tmj" <tmj@no.spam.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n5fqt$e3i3@musky.state.wi.us>`
- **References:** `<7n21bs$bme$1@endevour.netg.se> <37954a2b.4370081@news1.ibm.net> <3795F73B.CB776B47@dhfs.state.wi.us> <ZMnl3.660$4o4.21691@axe.netdoor.com> <37962B53.C67E3AC0@home.com>`

```
It's not Noughts.

It is more of a simulation than a game.  A "player" sets up an initial state
and watches the computer play it out.

"Life" makes a prominate appearance in "Glory Season" by David Brin.
http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/0553567675/o/qid=932593638/sr=2-1/002
-4641772-7878852

"Life" games were a major gambling venue as well a a method for sending
secret messages.
```

```
replicating machines and what "life" reall is.  The "Rules" can vary,
but we used to play in with graph paper, pencils and erasers in high
school. 

I just looked for my source - I don't have it.  Maybe I did it in
Basic on the Timex Sinclair.  I can't remember, but that's Life.
```

## Thread: Environment stability — 4 msgs

### Re: Environment stability

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37946B6D.138517B0@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37863A82.E91ED08D@NOSPAMhome.com> <37902327.CC6F6211@home.com>`

```
James,

You certainly have hit the nail on the head with the long note (6
printed pages impressive).

1.  Sorting:  Internal sorts are fine in MVS.  We generally choose to
separate them because they are easier to unit test and separating
large programs into smaller ones makes them easier to write.  If
portability were more important then I would consider internal sorts.

2.  Method names.  Cobol will need to create it's own Standard
Template Library like HP did for C++ as public domain.  The same
problem was occurring for C++ but after STL it quickly drew into
line.  The multiple visual interface libraries is a huge problem still
in C++, this would be our chance to one up them.  (Bob I don't expect
to much support for the visual library ;->)

Judson,

I agree with your comments about the browser interface.  I knew there
was something wrong with the picture but could not accurately describe
the problem, thanks.

... general ...

I have been following a thread about MVC (Model View and Controller)
on comp.object.  This solution has some flaws and I am trying to fully
understand the issues but we must nut this one out.

Basically MVC is how you put the business logic with a screen and
attach that to an input device.  I am getting head aches from the
descriptions I am getting.  One tutorial was one paragraph and
simplistic the other described how to do it in smalltalk without
actually describing what it all means.

Who is volunteering for the common STL for cobol?
Ken
```

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> James,
> 
> You certainly have hit the nail on the head with the long note (6
> printed pages impressive).

Apologies for being so long-winded, but having spent many frustrating
years trying to get decisions out of bean-counters, I found the only way
was to be very detailed and make it quite clear they couldn't use an
ambiguity as a let-out from making a decision.

It also results from 12 years in the RAF. Military theory/documentation
is excellent. In practice it doesn't always work, (Anzacs - Gallipoli,
Canadians - Dieppe, US - 101 Airborne at Ste. Marie d'Eglise on D-Day,
British - Arnhem). With Pentagon involvement on the original standards -
you'll appreciate how the military preciseness of COBOL appealed to me
as the language of choice. 

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS : I'm putting this through the 'English' spellchecker for Netscape.
If I put it through the American version it suggests WRAF as a
replacement for RAF - I can assure you I am not AC/DC !
```

<a id="t-4bf3281043"></a>
## Thread: Does AWO option in IBM COBOL still matter? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Does AWO option in IBM COBOL still matter?

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3794F6F0.ED76F62E@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
The COBOL II and COBOL for MVS and VM programmer's guides both claim
that use of the AWO (apply write only) option will optimize the size of
output blocks for variable length non-VSAM files.  Since this was an
underneath the covers way of applying the APPLY WRITE-ONLY clause (only
WRITE x FROM y used never WRITE x) to a variable length record file,
this would make a difference.  A WRITE x statement used code to build
the record in the output file buffer so a block had to be written any
time the maximum size record could not be added to the block.

The reason for the question is that I have the feeling that IBM COBOL no
longer builds the output record in the output file buffer but in effect
alway uses PUT MOVE rather than PUT LOCATE.  Could someone on this
newsgroup confirm or disprove my theory.  This is in response to a
question from a fellow worker.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
```

#### ↳ Re: Does AWO option in IBM COBOL still matter?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n2uc8$l6o@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3794F6F0.ED76F62E@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
I can't specifically address your PUT MOVE vs PUT LOCATE issue - but I do
know that the "newer" COBOLs are *supposed* to work whether the DCB is above
or below the line.  Hence it wouldn't surprise me if all records are "built"
somewhere other than in the buffer.

My guess is that this is *NOT* a "documented" interface - so my "usually
reliable sources" won't be able to give me (you) an official answer.  But I
can ask "just in case".
```

## Thread: Migrating MF Indexed files to SQL Warehouse — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Migrating MF Indexed files to SQL Warehouse

- **From:** "Jason Bailey" <jbailey5@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n2hgu$kv2$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Our insurance company is at a cross-roads where we are about to start
underwriting a large amount of new policies per month.  Our current system
was written in the 80's and we have a need to facilitate large data files.

Because I worry about indexed files become too bloated (and performance
degradation) I am looking at migrating the files to SQL Server 7.  This
would not only help with the performance issue, it would also allow us to
dynamically create reports from custom-made queries.

My question is:  Has anyone had experience with migrating from MF files to
SQL?  We'll be using AcuCobol 4.2 with Acu4GL for MS SQL Server.

Jason Bailey
Lead Programmer/Project Lead
```

<a id="t-8c4664e59f"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Report Writer and XML — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### COBOL Report Writer and XML

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37953B63.C50962F8@techie.com>`

```
This subject takes some research on my part.  I know what I want
to do, but I can't produce correct code off the top of my head for
Report Writer and XML.  So I going to do these posts in parts.

Part 1 -- Theory of Documents

A document has pieces.  Each piece is delimited and named (i.e.
tagged).

Using HTML like syntax (crossed with IBM DCF aka Script son of
Un*x roff), here is an example.

<DOC NAME=asdf>
<TITLE>Document Title</TITLE>
<H1>Outermost heading</H1>
<P>A paragraph</P>
<LIST BULLETED>
<LI>Item 1
<LI>Item 2
<LIST NUMBERED>
<LI>Subitem 2a
</LIST>
</LIST>
</DOC>

For a report, you might see
 
<REPORT>
<H1>Report Head
<H2>Group Head
<LINES>
detail line 1
detail line 2
<T2>
Group totals line 1
Group totals line 2
<LINES>
detail line 3
<T2>
Group totals line 1
Group totals line 2
<T1>
Report totals
</REPORT>


Now when you print on a page, if you know the page dimensions as
well as the dimensions of each line, then you are in.  With
standard height lines, you count lines just like report writer has
been doing since day 0.

The next advance is when you know the dimensions, say in points or
% of line heights or something else you can turn into inches.  The
layout is similar to counting lines, but you have factions of a
line (i.e. inches or cm).  But there is not a big problem figuring
if a group will fit on the remain page, or if a detail line will
be widowed or orphaned.

You can also replace the <tags> with escape sequences for a
printer, if you know something about the hardware.  I'd expect a
COBOL compiler for common use on a platform to know a little bit
about HP PCL printers, IBM 3800 printers, AFP printers, TTYs, what
ever matches the platform where the compiler is going to be
sold/used a lot.

But what if you don't know escape sequences?  Enter XML. 

Then you turn the job over to an external program.  I'll use a WWW
browser as an example.

If the tags with the correct HTML ones (<HTML> <HEAD> </HEAD>
<BODY> <CENTER> <TABLE> <SMALL> <P> </BODY> and a host of others)
then the output could be sent to a file, and that file loaded into
the browser.  The browser PRINT button could give hard copy.

Note that the browser doesn't have to insert page breaks.  WWW
browsers don't when displaying on the screen, and can when
printing.  When an external program is processing the COBOL
program output, some cooperation is needed so that report writer
does not insert page breaks if the typesetter does not want them
(e.g. screen display) or will decide itself where to put them. 
For example, it can format a report writer group to see how big it
is, then decide based on the current paper in use if it will fit
on this page or the next.  

The chief objection to WWW Browsers is that they lay out a page as
the choose to be viewed on a screen in a variable sized window. 
Most people would like to print to a fixed size piece of paper and
put print lines exactly where they want (fill in a form).

To overcome this, XML is being deployed.  GML (IBM DCF/Bookmaster)
HTML, XML, and others are defined by a very complex standard
called SGML.  SGML in all it's glory and complexity is used in
some type setting applications as well as others (indexing,
viewing, audio readers).  Being too complex to implement with good
performance and easy, SGML has been used to define XML.  XML is
itself a way to define tags, and how they are to be processed. 
That processing can include pixel resolution, type styles, style
sheets.  Almost anything you can ask a type setting program to do
can be marked up in XML.

Filling out forms is trivial IF you have a set of tags defined
which understand where things are on the form.  For last printing
subsystem I did, I caused the programmers to tag everything
including return addresses and zip codes.  The printing system
counted "to" addresses to see how many copies, printed BAR codes
for mailing from the ZIP codes, and even sorted by ZIP code for
the cheaper mailing rates.  Programmers first ask "why can't I
just tag an address as a bunch of lines (with line breaks)".  I
explained that the tag was not formatting directions (don't flow
these lines) but usage information (this is mailing ZIP info).

So now we face the problem of everyone and his sibling defining
tags and their implementation.  That's where the COBOL report
writer standard will have to exert some controls like "The report
writer expects the tag definition to include/not include these
actions".

For example, a tag that report writer will insert to delimit a
group could be required to maintain vertical alignment of the
enclosed lines so that a font with variable width characters can
be used, and duplicate information on lines within a group can be
blanked out on lines after the first.  (There is better way to
handle this problem -- see tables).

Next I want to conjure some report writer syntax and maybe a set
of tags with known dimensions in another post.  Feel free to type
yourself!
```

## Thread: Kinda off topic --> PC Shipping software — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Kinda off topic --> PC Shipping software

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37961bab.6965648@news.enteract.com>`

```
Hello,

I am asking for your advice/recomendations for a PC Shipping system
for processing shipments for UPS, FedEx, RPS etc etc.

A company presently has a UPS supplied PC that handles their UPS
packages (Weighs and prints labels).

They want to move to a generic shipping system that will handle
packages for all carriers.  I have been looking at one called CPS from
HarveySoft.  It seams ok, but I know there must be more out there, and
am wondering what peoples experiences with them are.

ALso, are there any that run on unix?

Any good web sites dealing with reviews of these systems?

Thanks for any help,

Paul
--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: Kinda off topic --> PC Shipping software

- **From:** rghcomputg@aol.com (Rghcomputg)
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990723143547.26086.00000594@ng-fp1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37961bab.6965648@news.enteract.com>`

```
Your choice might weigh more heavily upon the buyers pockets and the
anticipated shipping volume. Harveysoft is a lower priced product and they are
not nationwide (support concerns). Clippership is more costlier and they boast
of dealerships throughout the nation. Both are robust in features that support
integration for multiple shippers.

Reed Harvey
```

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00

```

Jonathan Roberts <zimboon @ hotmail . com> wrote in message
news:3796190e$0$26808@fountain.mindlink.net...
> You don't.
> Firstly you make sure the employee file input program
> has a REQUIRED on the gender field.
> The best way to solve a problem is to eliminate the possibility of it
> happening.

Agreed. How did bad data get past the data entry program? That said,
and since the company is so dim as to have allowed the problem to
appear, I suggest the project be expanded into comparing the
employee's name against a list - several thousand should do it - of
probable/certain sex-related names.

IF FIRST-NAME = "ABLE' MOVE 'M' TO EMPLOYEE-SEX.
IF FIRST-NAME = 'BARRY' MOVE "M" TO EMPLOYEE-SEX.
etc. for a couple-thousand lines. Only if the test fails, should
the program print a discrepancy list.

Next, the program should prepare for the future. Since the mere
keeping of records by sex will possibly someday be viewed as
evidence of discrimination, the program should have some way
of: a) erasing it's own code used to perform the test, and b) scrambling
the sex-designator in the master file.

Now we have a project worthy of an outside consultant's time.

P.S.
Humans don't have GENDER. Electrical plugs and German nouns
have gender. Humans have sex.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol program -- HELP

- **From:** "F.Pelser" <fpelser@concentric.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3797518A.FBDE7D55@concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<AIll3.2997$J5.31994@c01read02-admin.service.talkway.com> <3796190e$0$26808@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Sorry, but I have to disagree with you. The original question is actually
quite simple to solve. It all comes down to the very first basic function of
information processing and that is "editing". In most systems developed
using Cobol, the front-end processing programs are usually doing the editing
so that data flows correctly downstream.
In this case, the file structure would be:    (Field sizes are for examples
only). Cobol II code.

01 employee-record-input.
    05   emp-name      pic x(30) .
    05   emp-gender   pic x(01).
    05   emp-salarly    pic s9(06)v99 packed-decimal.

01 employee-record-rpt.
    05 emp-nm-rpt    pic x(30).
    05 emp-gen-rpt   pic x(01).
    05 emp-sal-rpt    pic z,zzz,zzz.99-.
    05 emp-sal-stars  redefines emp-sal-rpt pic x(13).

Procedure Division.

     If  emp-name = spaces
         move all '*' to emp-nm-rpt
    else
         move emp-name to emp-nm-rpt
    End-if

    If emp-gender = 'm' or 'f''
       move emp-gender to emp-gen-rpt
    else
        move all '*'  to emp-nm-rpt
     end-if

    If  emp-salary numeric
       move emp-salary to emp-sal-rpt
   else
      move all '*' to emp-sal-stars
    end-if

-- (Write the employee report record.)

There, I helped with somebody's homework

FP




Jonathan Roberts wrote:

> You don't.
> Firstly you make sure the employee file input program
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
F.Pelser wrote:
>

> quite simple to solve. It all comes down to the very first basic function of
> information processing and that is "editing". In most systems developed
> using Cobol, the front-end processing programs are usually doing the editing

Good for you - you've done his homework - the instructor will be
pleased.

However, your validation checks don't cover it all. We all know about
'Eternal Loops' but what about 'User Generated Eternal Loops". Let me
illustrate when it was ADP and Noah was still loading the Ark. We were
brand new to the game, some of our bright kids were using machine code
and Assembler and we were designing.

It was a system to invoice dairy products. We were feeling our way, and
this was also a brand new game for the people in Sales Ledger/Accounts
Receivable. We designed a Customer input record, with of course an
account number. CustomerX had the option of buying himself, buying
through a Grocers' Buying Association, (that's two accounts), or if it
was a Co-operative could buy as Cardiff Co-op or South Wales Co-op or UK
Co-operative Society (now three accounts) and so on.... All these
groupings were so that retailers could get the current best deal on
prices.

We realized that the A/R people might screw-up when setting-up accounts,
so we designed the card :-

	A/c 1234	Joe Blow	New A/c 6789

They could 'correct' the system by adding that 6789 - which the program
used as the new reference point.

Being real high-tech we had an RCA Magnetic Card File (index system
before IBM disks - 3 Billion chars ?), which whisked 'plastic mag cards'
in a figure-of-eight around the read-write-head then sucked them back
into the appropriate magazine. 

Not unusual on the old clunkers for a process to take 2 hours for one
program doing look-ups. The Ops Manager was used to this. Though he did
find it curious when it took 3, then 4, then 5 hours...... He pulled the
plug.

One little sweetheart in A/R had coded two cards as follows :-

	A/c 1234	Joe Blow 1	New A/c 6789	
	A/c 6789	Joe Blow 2	New A/c 1234

You can't catch 'em all.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Data validation needs to be a part of every program (just a personal thing).
It would be nice if you could guarantee all of your incomming data was good,
but you can't. These validation routines will help. Check any of your
reference manuals for data validation techniques.

F.Pelser <fpelser@concentric.net> wrote in message
news:3797518A.FBDE7D55@concentric.net...

of
> information processing and that is "editing". In most systems developed
> using Cobol, the front-end processing programs are usually doing the
editing
> so that data flows correctly downstream.
> In this case, the file structure would be:    (Field sizes are for
examples
> only). Cobol II code.
>
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol program -- HELP

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379A670E.CA7F7CFA@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<AIll3.2997$J5.31994@c01read02-admin.service.talkway.com> <3796190e$0$26808@fountain.mindlink.net> <3797518A.FBDE7D55@concentric.net> <mZmm3.1108$dU.6206@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
David Kane wrote:
> 
> Data validation needs to be a part of every program (just a personal
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Generalizations are generally bad.

Input to a system should be validated.  For example an interface
between the general ledger and foreign exchange should check the input
but between input program and posting program there should not be.

To much code is invalidated if a structure changes,  validation logic
is in too many places and it slows down processing.

How much validation should you apply, as much as is reasonable.  Use
your personal judgement but if you get it wrong you can bet on a huge
amount of time cleaning up the mess.

Ken
```

### [Fwd: Re: Using EXEC CICS... as Copybook]

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>

```
I am not disagreeing with your answer but I believe that it is absurd
that we still have to do this in 1999. The COBOL compiler should be set
up to call an appropriate translator for every EXEC statement in either
main source or copy book.  If that won't work, the preprocessor should
be set up to be able to parse required copybooks (including correctly
handling COPY REPLACING and nested copy) and pass the translated source
with the translated copybooks to the compiler.  In either case we should
not have to go through unnatural gyrations to do the simple thing.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
Gunnar Opheim wrote:
> snip
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Using EXEC CICS... as Copybook]

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3795BCA2.1C241F83@zip.com.au>`

```
Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> I am not disagreeing with your answer but I believe that it is absurd
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Gunnar.

Look at some of your tools you have available.  One of them might be
able to expand the copybook before the CICS translator.  For example
if you use CICS and DB2 use EXEC SQL INCLUDE and do your DB2
precompiler first.  The CICS translator will then see the CICS code in
situe.

If you use endevor I think there is a tool that will expand the COPY
statements for you.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Using EXEC CICS... as Copybook]

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3796DCE0.7E63F9D9@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<37958EAC.63D06F26@nbnet.nb.ca> <3795BCA2.1C241F83@zip.com.au>`

```
Based on a posting in bit.listserv.ibm-main, I looked up the TSO utility
program named ISRLEMX.  After fussing around with the manual, I was able
to run it in a plain old OS/390 batch job.  It's a utility program that
read a COBOL program and expand the copybooks.

I wanted something like it for a different reason.  I haven't tested it
to see if it handles nested copy or copy replacing, but it will search
up to 8 copybook libraries in the order you specify.  Besides COBOL, it
can also be configured to handle Assembler or PL/1.

If anyone is interested I can post a sample of the JCL.
```

```
On Thu, 22 Jul 1999 03:57:04 -0500, Arnold Trembley
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [Fwd: Re: Using EXEC CICS... as Copybook]

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37980522.3DA86491@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<37958EAC.63D06F26@nbnet.nb.ca> <3795BCA2.1C241F83@zip.com.au> <3796DCE0.7E63F9D9@worldnet.att.net> <379800f5.101941961@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 22 Jul 1999 03:57:04 -0500, Arnold Trembley
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please.

Fortunately, I printed off a copy at work today.  Here's what ISRLEMX
would look like:

//****************************************************
//*     EXPAND COBOL COPYBOOKS
//****************************************************
//TRN     EXEC PGM=ISRLEMX,COND=(12,LE),
//        PARM=('COB,progname,B,N,E,5, ,00,ENU,4,7',
//             '1,/,SYSDA)
//*
//*  INSERT A STEPLIB DD HERE IF ISRLEMX IS NOT IN YOUR SYSTEM LIBRARY
//*
//ISRLCODE DD DSN=MY.COBOL.SOURCE.LIB,DISP=SHR
//         DD DSN=TEST.COPYBOOK.LIB1,DISP=SHR
//         DD DSN=TEST.COPYBOOK.LIB2,DISP=SHR
//         DD DSN=TEST.COPYBOOK.LIB3,DISP=SHR
//         DD DSN=PROD.COPYBOOK.LIB,DISP=SHR
//ISRLEXPD DD UNIT=SYSDA,DISP=(NEW,PASS),
//            SPACE=(CYL,(2,2)),DSN=&&TEMP1
//ISRLMSG  DD SYSOUT=*

You might find it easier to put ISLRLEMX into a proc, so you can pass
your 'progname' in as a substitution parameter.  Don't forget the 'E' as
the fifth subparameter!  If you need more than five libraries in your
ISRLCODE search chain, you can go up to nine by putting a '9' after the
'E' in the parameter list.  The 'ENU' just means English.  If you have
questions about the rest of the parameters, e-mail me, or look it up in
the manual (I forgot which one it was, but I think it was ISPF on book
mangler).

The PDS library containing your COBOL source program has to be the first
PDS under the ISRLCODE DD.  All the rest are your copybook libraries. 
You can override them to change the search order as needed.

ISRLEXPD can be directed to any file you want, of course, but you may
just leave it as a temporary so you can pass it into another language
translater, such as the CICS preprocessor, the compiler itself, or in my
case the standards compliance checker.

I've never gotten any messages out of ISRLMSG.  I suppose it's just
there in case of severe errors.  ISRLEMX will insert comment lines
immediately before and after any included copybooks.  We're at OS/390
Release 2.5, but I believe ISRLEMX has been around for a few
releases.      

Good luck!
```

## Thread: help needed with windows api — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help needed with windows api

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n4eu4$qon$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 Once again I am experiencing the joys of inter-language programming
and the Windows API.
I have a program that searches the registry, and now am extending that
to set values
in the registry.  Based on the original pgm, I am successfully
connecting to the registry
of a remote server and navigating down the tree to the key I want.
Then I try to use
the RegEnumKeyExA function to determine the names of the subkeys at
that level.  That
call is giving a return code of 234, which, from the skimpy
documentation I have, simply
indicates "Error. More information available."  I�m pretty certain that
the data type
of one of my parameters is wrong.  If anyone has any experience with
this and would like
to take a look, here�s the relevant W.S. and Procedure.

environment division.
configuration section.
special-names.
call-convention 74 is apientry
call-convention 2  is winapi
.

working-storage section.

01  REG-Rc                      pic s9(9) comp-5.
01  REG-KeyHandle               pic  9(9) comp-5.
01  REG-dwIndex                 pic s9(9) comp-5.
01  REG-szData                  pic x(256).
01  REG-iDataLen                pic s9(9) comp-5.
01  REG-lpcbName                pic s9(9) comp-5.
01  REG-lpcbClass               pic s9(9) comp-5.
01  REG-lpName                  pic x(256).
01  REG-lpClass                 pic x(256).
01  REG-lpftLastWriteTime       pic x(256).

procedure division winapi.
call APIENTRY "RegEnumKeyExA" using
by value     REG-KeyHandle
by value     REG-dwIndex
by reference REG-lpName
by reference REG-lpcbName
by value     0 size 4
by reference REG-lpClass
by reference REG-lpcbClass
by reference REG-lpftLastWriteTime
returning    REG-Rc
end-call

At the entry to the call, the arguments have the following values:
REG-KeyHandle = value returned by the last RegOpenKeyExA
REG-dwIndex = +0000000000
REG-lpName = all x�00�
REG-lpcbName = +0000000000
REG-lpClass = all x�00�
REG-lpcbClass = +0000000000
REG-lpftLastWriteTime = all x�00�
REG-Rc = +0000000000

After returning from the call, all values are the same except REG-Rc,
which contains +0000000234

The documentation on this call is at
http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/sdkdoc/winbase/regapi_4854.htm

Thanks for any assistance.
```

- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

#### ↳ Re: help needed with windows api

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qvkl3.2167$um6.17916@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7n4eu4$qon$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
A 234 means your buffer is too small, which may be embarrassing depending on
the social situation in which you get a 234. Your calling parameters look ok
to a casual glance, although the naming is a bit confusing (I tend to
translate the 'lp' part to 'by reference' and drop it from the name of the
variable). I think all you need to do is set the length of the receiving
buffers.

    MOVE FUNCTION(LENGTH) REG-lpName to REG-lpcbName
    MOVE FUNCTION(LENGTH) REG-lpClass to REG-lpcbClass



> Once again I am experiencing the joys of inter-language programming
>and the Windows API.
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help needed with windows api

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n56mo$5fa$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7n4eu4$qon$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qvkl3.2167$um6.17916@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I try to avoid social situations where the size of my buffer would come
into question <G> but giving the API that info solved my problem.
Thanks.

I had assumed (and we all know what *that* spells) that those length
fields were RETURNED FROM the call, rather than SUPPLIED TO it.  But
now that it comes up, I vaguely remember having the same problem on
some other calls when I first started using the Windows API.

The use of the Hungarian notation on the data names was straight from
the documentation on the API function.  You'll have to take that up
with Bill's boys.

Thanks again for the assistance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help needed with windows api

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_sql3.2527$um6.22246@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7n4eu4$qon$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qvkl3.2167$um6.17916@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <7n56mo$5fa$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com> wrote in message
news:7n56mo$5fa$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
<<SNIP>>
>
> The use of the Hungarian notation on the data names was straight from
> the documentation on the API function.  You'll have to take that up
> with Bill's boys.
>

The use of hungarian notation is all well and good, but lpName is translated
as long pointer to Name. If you want to be pedantic (I have no idea what
'pedantic' means but it sounds impressive) then you should declare a pointer
in working-storage called lpName and do a set lpName to address of Name.
However, with COBOL, the 'by reference' clause does this for you.

In effect, by using

    by reference lpName

you are saying 'a pointer to a pointer to a Name' which is one level of
indirection too many.

My opinion, humble or otherwise.



<<SNIP>>
```

## Thread: AS/400 program — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### AS/400 program

- **From:** "Michael Thompson" <thompsonm1@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n4o5r$89g$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
I'd first like to say I'm not too familiar with the Cobol language. What I'd
like to know is if it's possible to send a command to a printer to change
fonts. We have a AS/400 program that we would like to use a barcode in. Our
software programmers tell us we can't do it without an IPDS compatible
system. The printer manual (a 4840 model) says to change the font of the
printer, but gives example only in BASIC code. Is it right to assume that
it's not possible to convert that BASIC code into something that can be
understood by Cobol? Thanks for any info.

Mike
```

```
I'd
>like to know is if it's possible to send a command to a printer to change
>fonts. We have a AS/400 program that we would like to use a barcode in. Our
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>understood by Cobol? Thanks for any info.

I don't know AS400, but you should not have any problem translating that
particular stuff into Cobol code. Question is if the AS400 would let you
send it to the printer without altering it.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-11d61af22e"></a>
## Thread: Year 2000?? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Year 2000??

- **From:** joe4288@usa.net
- **Date:** 1999-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n537c$e3m$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

   _            -----_
  //           {|||} 0]_____
  ||          {|||}   \-----]
   \\        {|||}    _\____    Computer: (noun)
    \\      {|||}    /  ----      A person who tabulates numbers;
     \\--------     /             accountant; actuary; bookkeeper.
     /             |
     |   DogByte   |            -- Merriam Webster Dictionary (1946) 
     \| ----------- \
       |           ||
       ||           ||
       ==           ==

There was once a COBOL programmer in the mid to late 1990s. For the
sake of this story, we'll call him Jack. After years of being taken 
for granted and treated as a technological dinosaur by all the UNIX
programmers and Client/Server programmers and website developers, 
Jack was finally getting some respect. He'd become a private consultant
specializing in Year 2000 conversions. He was working short-term
assignments for prestige companies, traveling all over the world on
different assignments. He was working 70 and 80 and even 90 hour weeks,
but it was worth it.

Several years of this relentless, mind-numbing work had taken its toll
on Jack. He had problems sleeping and began having anxiety dreams
about the Year 2000. It had reached a point where even the thought of
the year 2000 made him nearly violent. He must have suffered some sort
of breakdown, because all he could think about was how he could avoid
the year 2000 and all that came with it.

Jack decided to contact a company that specialized in cryogenics.
He made a deal to have himself frozen until March 15th, 2000. This
was very expensive process and totally automated. He was thrilled.
The next thing he would know is he'd wake up in the year 2000; after
the New Year celebrations and computer debacles; after the leap day.
Nothing else to worry about except getting on with his life.

He was put into his cryogenic receptacle, the technicians set the
revive date, he was given injections to slow his heartbeat to a bare
minimum, and that was that.

The next thing that Jack saw was an enormous and very modern room
filled with excited people. They were all shouting "I can't believe
it!" and "It's a miracle" and "He's alive!". There were cameras
(unlike any he'd ever seen) and equipment that looked like it came
out of a science fiction movie.

Someone who was obviously a spokesperson for the group stepped
forward. Jack couldn't contain his enthusiasm. "It is over?" he
asked. "Is 2000 already here? Are all the millennial parties and
promotions and crises all over and done with?"

The spokesman explained that there had been a problem with the
programming of the timer on Jack's cryogenic receptacle, it hadn't
been year 2000 compliant. It was actually eight thousand years later,
not the year 2000. But the spokesman told Jack that he shouldn't get
excited; someone important wanted to speak to him.

Suddenly a wall-sized projection screen displayed the image of a man
that looked very much like Bill Gates. This man was Prime Minister of
Earth. He told Jack not to be upset. That this was a wonderful time
to be alive. That there was world peace and no more starvation. That
the space program had been reinstated and there were colonies on the
moon and on Mars. That technology had advanced to such a degree that
everyone had virtual reality interfaces which allowed them to contact
anyone else on the planet, or to watch any entertainment, or to hear
any music recorded anywhere.

"That sounds terrific," said Jack. "But I'm curious. Why is
everybody so interested in me?"

"Well," said the Prime Minister. "The year 10000 is just around the
corner, and it says in your files that you know COBOL."
```

## Thread: Porting to Windows NT 4.0 — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Porting to Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** Markus Triska <triska@netway.at>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37978BF0.46BDC694@netway.at>`

```
Hello, everybody!

            I want to port some sources from DOS AcuCOBOL 3.2 to
WINDOWS-AcuCOBOL 4.0.
So, my questions: I know that there could be problems with function
keys.
    Does anybody know something about that issue? Are there any other
things that will matter?

                            Thank you in advance,

                                                        Markus Triska.
```

#### ↳ Re: Porting to Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n8n9i$kah$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37978BF0.46BDC694@netway.at>`

```
Markus Triska wrote in message <37978BF0.46BDC694@netway.at>...
>Hello, everybody!
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>keys.

Well, in general F10,  is a special key under Windows, that is scheduled to
be ok with their 4.3

Is it anything particular you are thinking of?

>    Does anybody know something about that issue? Are there any other
>things that will matter?

I guess it is more a question of change of environment, when I did the move
some years ago, we did not alter much. we had not used a configuration file
before, we added one and here are the entries we introduced:

screen          size=25,80 window=999,999
white-fill      1
color-model     5
3d-lines        1
background-intensity 1
translate-to-ansi    1
quit-mode       11

The most important one if you are non-english is the translate-to-ansi
variable, which lets you keep your data in ascii, while the screen / printer
io goes in ansi. Screen, white-fill, color-model, 3d-lines,
background-intensity, quit-mode are windows specific stuff.

And that's basically it, there may be a few quirks that I don't remember,
but those are probably not important.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Porting to Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** Markus Triska <triska@netway.at>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379AC8F6.25FE0938@netway.at>`
- **References:** `<37978BF0.46BDC694@netway.at> <7n8n9i$kah$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
>

 First, thank you very much for your answer.
The tip concerning the configuration-file is very important to me

> Well, in general F10,  is a special key under Windows, that is scheduled to

>
> Is it anything particular you are thinking of?

I thought that the F10-key was DISABLED to programs running under MS-Windows.
Is there a way to handle them in AcuCOBOL 4.0? (possibly with embedded C/C++ ?)

Are there any other keys (-or key-combinations) reserved?

>
>
> And that's basically it, there may be a few quirks that I don't remember,
> but those are probably not important.

Oh, could you PLEASE tell me? Everything concerning this problem is important
to me !

Thank you in advance,
                                                        Markus Triska.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting to Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nh0bm$g7o$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37978BF0.46BDC694@netway.at> <7n8n9i$kah$1@news.cerf.net> <379AC8F6.25FE0938@netway.at>`

```
Markus Triska wrote in message <379AC8F6.25FE0938@netway.at>...
> First, thank you very much for your answer.

You are welcome, I once was in the same situation myself.

>I thought that the F10-key was DISABLED to programs running under
MS-Windows.

Generally yes, however, you may access it, if needed.

>Is there a way to handle them in AcuCOBOL 4.0? (possibly with embedded
C/C++ ?)

In 4.3 you have a possibility to control F10.

>Are there any other keys (-or key-combinations) reserved?

Ah, I must admit I really can't remember, guess you may ask their support
for that one.

>> And that's basically it, there may be a few quirks that I don't remember,
>> but those are probably not important.
>
>Oh, could you PLEASE tell me? Everything concerning this problem is
important
>to me !

Well, you see, I can't really come up with any others. So, I think what you
got in the configuration file actually is it. Oh yeah, basically, you cannot
resize your windows freely, there is however a workaround. If you display
the initial window graphical with a specified font/size you will get through
it.

Are you going to convert to ANSI charset or continue with OEM?

My suggestion to you is to start running with it. It should be runable just
out of the box, and then you will experience the differences on the fly,
other than those I told you of it should only be minor adjustments, nothing
bad. It is strictly depending on your existing implementation and the habits
your endusers have.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting to Windows NT 4.0

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nicgr$2q1$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37978BF0.46BDC694@netway.at> <7n8n9i$kah$1@news.cerf.net> <379AC8F6.25FE0938@netway.at> <7nh0bm$g7o$1@news.cerf.net> <379CA72B.46AD5C42@netway.at>`

```
Markus Triska wrote in message <379CA72B.46AD5C42@netway.at>...
>By now, I've found out that also the ALT + Function-key - combinations are
>disabled for programs.

That is the general setup, you may however alter this programmatically,
like:

           SET     ENVIRONMENT      "KEYSTROKE" TO
                                    "Exception=1  ^[".

Which terminates an accept if ESCAPE key was pressed. To obtain the same
behaviour with for instance Alt F1, you would:

           SET     ENVIRONMENT      "KEYSTROKE" TO
                                    "Exception=101  U1".

Which terminates an accept when ALT F1 is pressed and sets escape key to
101.

>When will version 4.3 be released?

I have no idea, they told about it at a conference I was attending.

>Who told you the new features the 4.3 will have?

As I said, it was at one of their conferences.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Porting to Windows NT 4.0

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Markus Triska" <triska@netway.at>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nnqg8$mb4$1@news.netway.at>`
- **References:** `<37978BF0.46BDC694@netway.at> <7n8n9i$kah$1@news.cerf.net> <379AC8F6.25FE0938@netway.at> <7nh0bm$g7o$1@news.cerf.net> <379CA72B.46AD5C42@netway.at> <7nicgr$2q1$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

Which keys (AND key-combinations!) are in fact 100%, totally and forever
disabled (read: not accessible by any configuration or programming-
sequence) for a Windows NT-program compiled with AcuCobol Version 4.0 and
4.1 ?

Thank you again, for your answers as well as in advance,

                                        Markus Triska.
```

```
My boss will see this, but he knows whats up.

You post a rate, I send you my resume.  GUARENTEED.

I've attempted the deal with several recruiters.   Only one has taken
me up on it.  

The PRICE of my resume is YOUR posting a rate to the group.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IMMEDIATE OPENING

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akent-2307991935570001@o9.dialup.seanet.com>`
- **References:** `<IJQl3.727$Gu2.21512@iad-read.news.verio.net> <19990723003021.13317.00000434@ng-fr1.aol.com> <3798033e.102527051@news1.ibm.net>`

```
It is a waste of time to "tell" a recruiter on this newsgroup to post
rates.  What you need to do is to EMail the individual and let the
recruiter know then that once the rate (or rate range) is revealed, then a
resume will besent.

I have noticed that the "moderator" of this newsgroup has consistently
posted messages to the recruiter to post rates.  But, if you follow this
closely, the same ads appear again, again with no rate.

I also agree with what others have written: Never respond with a resume to
a recruiter who does not post the rate.  That is just good business sense.

In article <3798033e.102527051@news1.ibm.net>, redsky@ibm.net (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

> On 23 Jul 1999 04:30:21 GMT, profrmk@aol.com (ProfRMK) wrote:
> 
> >Thanks for your note. Rates are relative to the consultant experience.
> >
> >If you have the required skills for this project please send me a copy
of your
> >resume.
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> The PRICE of my resume is YOUR posting a rate to the group.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IMMEDIATE OPENING

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

```
Arthur, I nearly always post and mail.  I did in this instance also.
No reply.  I did in another more recent, and after a heated discussion
(not on my part) I was told I was not wanted and there was talk of
bridges for kindling.  Anyway a few days later I recieved a polite
request for my resume, which I countered with my deal AGAIN - tell me
the rate, I'll send you the resume.  Didn't take me up on it.  

But in our most recent case the recruiter was participating in the
discussion on the group - so he/she should have received my mail AND
read my post.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IMMEDIATE OPENING

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QbYl3.770$Gu2.24057@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<IJQl3.727$Gu2.21512@iad-read.news.verio.net> <19990723003021.13317.00000434@ng-fr1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19990723003021.13317.00000434@ng-fr1.aol.com>,
ProfRMK <profrmk@aol.com> wrote:
>Thanks for your note. Rates are relative to the consultant experience.

Then please post a range associated with experiences.

>
>If you have the required skills for this project please send me a copy of your
>resume.

I do not send resumes to anyone who does not post a rate, or range of
rates, associated with the position(s) offered; I have concluded that they
are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to determine rates.

If you want to see my papers then you show the associated numbers,
allowing me to determine if we have the possibility for a match.  You are,
of course, most welcome to take your business elsewhere; if you choose
that route then I hope you are very happy with the people you find who are
willing to do business with you under those conditions...

...but *none* of them will be me.

DD
```

<a id="t-7f17301d46"></a>
## Thread: Problems animating subroutines — 3 msgs

### Problems animating subroutines

- **From:** Reijo Koivisto <reijok@hotpop.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37971D44.7AF18AA6@hotpop.com>`

```
We have received some cobol programs for witch we should do Y2000
checks. These programs use routines of a library (also made with cobol)
and call some operating system API:s. The problem comes with debugging
(or animating as they call it at Micro Focus). The animator in Micro
Focus Object Cobol 4.0 seems to always want to have a source code for
the routine available. With the library routines we have that but for
the operating system code obviously not. Program id in the library
routines is not the same as file name witch seems to be a problem for
the animator too.

Our environment is OS/2 Warp and Micro Focus Object Cobol 4.0. We
compile our programs to .obj/.exe format. With other languages and
environment I have used to that debuggers just execute routines for
witch they don't have debugging information available. Does anyone have
an advice for me?

Reijo Koivisto
Finland
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems animating subroutines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n9rj9$3oo@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37971D44.7AF18AA6@hotpop.com>`

```
A reply from one of my "usually reliable sources",

"Animator support for debugging GNT/OBJ was introduced in the 4.0 product
(though you could accomplish the same thing in the 16-bit world several
years earlier by using Xilerator).

As to this customer's question, the reason the animator will be prompting
for the library routines written in COBOL will be because those programs
were compiled with the ANIM directive. Also, the animator does not prompt
for any source when calling operating system APIs as it knows nothing about
them, so that statement is a bit of a mystery - are they sure they are Win32
APIs that are being called ?"
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems animating subroutines

- **From:** Patrick <pwm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932825852.25774@www.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<37971D44.7AF18AA6@hotpop.com>`

```
Below is a Batch file I use to Animate programs with
Workbench 4.0.xx under Windows95/98/NT.

Animit.bat
@echo off
::Animit.bat
echo ganim>cobol.dir
cbllink gdfs.cbl kernel32.lib
del cobol.dir
set cobanim_2=animate
animate gdfs.exe
set cobanim_2=

The program must be Cbllink'd with the ganim compiler
directive.
The environment variable cobanim_2 must be set to the word
animate.
You can then animate the executable.
The environment variable needs to be unset when finished as
this setting will conflict with animation of Int programs.
This techniqur only works from the command line and the
graphical Animator, Anim2, can be used as an alternative.



* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n77dm$51l$1@hfc.pacific.net.hk>`

```
 HI

      I  have a project need  to use two languages Cobol and C in MVS
mainframe
env.  Does anyone know how to pass data between cobol and C.  What happen if
 their datatype is incompatable? How can C program pass back  the return
code to
cobol.  What I need to pay attention to in handling such data interchange
bwteen
two different lang.  I will appreciate for any help or hint. MY E-mail
address is

Wilho@HK.SUPER.NET
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and C

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n7n5g$520@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7n77dm$51l$1@hfc.pacific.net.hk>`

```
The best place to start for this (assuming MVS or OS/390 running LE) is with
the IBM documentation aimed at this question.  Please see:

   http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA406/CCONTENTS

which is (for the "web-less")

 "Title: OS/390 V2R7.0 Lang Env Writing ILC Application
   Document Number: SC28-1943-06"
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and C

- **From:** "Roland Schiradin" <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n8521$pjq$1@black.news.nacamar.net>`
- **References:** `<7n77dm$51l$1@hfc.pacific.net.hk>`

```
Wilson,

it depends!!!
The following code run fine only under
OS/390 V2R6 c-compiler and Cobol for OS390 andVM

 Identification Division.
 Program-Id. COBMAIN1.
******************************************************************
* 06/98 First version                                            *
******************************************************************
 Environment Division.
 Configuration Section.
 Data Division.
 Working-Storage Section.
 01                      pic x(12)       value "**Working**".
 01 CUPRO-Dynamic        pic x(8)        value "CUPRO1".
 01 Result               pic x(20)       value Spaces.
 Linkage Section.
 Procedure Division.
     Display "CobMain1:This is the Cobol-Main-Program and we test"
     Display "CobMain1:the inter language communication"
     Display "CobMain1:=========================================="
     Display "CobMain1:1. Dynamic call of C-Program CUpro1"
     Call  Cupro-Dynamic using Result
     Display "CobMain1:   back from C-Program CUpro1"
     Display "CobMain1:   Feedback:" Result
     Move Zero to Return-code
     Goback
     .
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
/* the entry point (Binder) have to set to CUpro1 */
void CUpro1(char *pBuffer)
{                                                                   
                                                                    
   strcpy( pBuffer, "CUpro1 RC=0");                                 
   fprintf(stderr,"Cupro1  :   --> Dynamic call of CUpro1 done\n"); 
}                                                                   

On older C-compiler you need a special #pragma

Posting the compile/link/LE-setting would be to much.

Try my sample and contact me offline

Roland




wilson <wilho@hk.super.net> schrieb in im Newsbeitrag: 7n77dm$51l$1@hfc.pacific.net.hk...
> HI
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Wilho@HK.SUPER.NET writes ...



>HI
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>two different lang.  I will appreciate for any help or hint. 

Hi, yourself, Wilho.

I recently announced a course that covers all the questions you ask and a lot
more. The three-day course InterLanguage Communication in OS/390 covers
cross-language calls in four languages: Assembler, COBOL, PL/I, and C.

We discuss static calls and dynamic calls; passing and receiving data of all
data types; omitted parameters; variable parameter lists; the linkage editor
and the program binder; DLLs; and even more.

Check out our website: www.trainersfriend.com

Follow the links to Topics -> LE -> Inter-Language Communication in OS/390;
this page is a course description; at the bottom of the page is a link to the
course objectives; at the bottom of that page is a link to the detailed course
outline.

Also, from the home page or the course description page you can find a link to
our public offerings and see that we are teaching the class in Denver Sept.
14-16.

Come join us. Or, we'll be happy to teach in HongKong.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
Cheesle wrote:

> Come on, why not be honest enough to admit you blew it this time. I agree

I skipped the question because I have no idea what he's asking.  I'm
familiar with MVS Cobol II, but there isn't an MVS Cobol I.  So he's
obviously talking about some other environment.  It seems unlikely that
a teacher would ask him the difference between the Cobol II & Cobol I
without specifying the environment.

What's the difference between dBASE I and dBASE II?

I am familiar with ANSI 68 & ANSI 72 which were both used by IBM before
it decided to rename their next version of a standard ANSI compiler to
COBOL II (and mess up ignorant people hiring people from other ON
environments).
```

```
> >What's the difference between dBASE I and dBASE II?

There is no dBASE I.  Ashton-Tate decided that people would prefer a
more mature product, so they named their first product dBASE II.
 
> Good question, I have no idea. I can tell you the difference of Borland
```

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
>What, I *didn't* offend you?... dear me, I'll have to try harder next
>time.

Absolutely :-)

>'What is the difference between an excuse and a reason?'

Good question, I would say;

That an excuse is expressed to apologize for something that is said/done
without the purpose of producing the effect that it actually did. While a
reason is used for something that is said/done on purpose, knowing the
concequences and accepting them.

Does this sound good?

Cheesle
```

```
On Sun, 25 Jul 1999 23:29:09 -0700, "Cheesle"
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
>>Asked and answered... next?
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Just my 2 cents.

Considering just about everybody refered to the COBOL-85 standard as
"COBOL II" becuase IBM called it that.  So anyone seeing that phrase
and not of our archane order would naturally assumed there was a
"COBOL I".  Actually it was the COBOL-74 standard, refered to in
IBM-land as "OS/VS COBOL".  Going back even further in time there was
a COBOL-68 standard, a COBOL-64 (?) standard and a COBOL-60 (?)
standard. 

But I could be wrong,
Boyce G. Williams, Jr.
```

```
Steve Beattie wrote:
> 
> I suggest you check your facts before replying in such an ill mannered way.
> I've been programming in Cobol for over six years, I've just never had to
> code in Cobol I.  So, I thought it would be a good idea to ask those nice
> people in the new group for some help.

Assuming an IBM mainframe environment, there was never a COBOL I
compiler. The COBOL compiler IBM offered before COBOL II was OS/VS
COBOL. Regarding the differences, I've always thought Ann Prince's
"COBOL II" book did a good job. I might not have the title exactly
right, but I have the book (at work) and have referred to it many times.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol I and Cobol II

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nadr3$7iu@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7na80u$uvc$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Assuming that you mean "OS/VS COBOL" when you say "COBOL I" and assuming
that you mean "VS COBOL II" when you say "COBOL II" (there are some other
possibilities, but this is the usual mis-nomer), the one thing they both
have in common is that they are
   BOTH obsolete (non-strategic) IBM products

VS COBOL II, R4 is still supported (and will be until March 2001) but it was
LONG ago superceded by COBOL/370 which in turn was superceded by IBM COBOL
for MVS & VM which in turn was superceded by IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM.
(Either of the last 2 products *can* be supplemented by MLE - Millennium
Language Extensions)

Having said all of that, the best place to see an introduction to the
difference between OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II (R4) is in the IBM
publication that can be seen on the web at:

    http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYM1101/CCONTENTS

which (For the web-less) is:
  Title: VS COBOL II Migration Guide for MVS and CMS
  Document Number: GC26-3151-00

If you want something that is more detailed, then several people (myself
included) have written entire books on this.  If you REALLY want to know the
differences between OS/VS COBOL and one of IBM's *strategic* COBOL products,
check out the web for the IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM Migration Guide.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol I and Cobol II

## Thread: Tables and Moves — 25 msgs

### Tables and Moves

- **From:** "Josua S." <fmff@esoterica.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt>`

```
Hi all. This is a small portion of a program that keeps me getting crazy. It
compiles ok but during execution it just abend It uses several tables with
depending on and also some moves from table to table. Can anyone please tell
me if there  is something  wrong with this statements ?

in the working i have :

05 TABLE-SRC.
     20 FILLER OCCURS 100 TO  1000 DEPENDING ON TBSRC.
          25 FILLER     PIC X.

05 TABLE-DEST.
    10 REG-DEST OCCURS 1 TO 10 DEPENDING ON TBDEST.
         20 FILLER PIC X(99).

05 TABLE-TMP.
     10 REG-TMP OCCURS 1 TO 10 DEPENDING ON TBTMP.
          20 FILLER    PIC X(80).
          20 MY-VAR PIC X(19).
          20 FILLER    PIC X.


Assuming TBSRC,TBDEST and TBTMP are defined, the following move statements
are valid ?
1)    MOVE TABLE-SRC TO TABLE-TMP
2)    MOVE REG-TMP(I) TO REG-DEST(I) (truncate the last byte)

Thx in advance
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au>`

```
I too will try and be a little helpful.  I want to know exactly what you
mean by,

  "Assuming TBSRC,TBDEST and TBTMP are defined"

If by that, you mean that there is a data definition for them in
Working-Storage (or anywhere else in the Data Division) - *BUT* you haven't
explicitly put any values in them, then *THAT* is probably your problem.
If, on the other hand, you mean that they all have "correct" (and current)
integer values, then I think that all the MOVEs should be "legal" - whether
they do what you want or not.

My guess (as I recall the original question), is that you have defined the
fields, but not (correctly) initialized them - but this is only a guess.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

- **From:** "Josua S." <fmff@esoterica.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932833211.356212@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Well, guess they have the correct value.
1) TBSRC is allways a multiple of 100 but not greater than 1000. so possible
values are 100,200,300.....1000
2) As for TBDEST and TBTMP their value is computed as follows :
COMPUTE TBDEST = TBSRC / 100
COMPUTE TBTMP = TBSRC / 100
All these variables are defined in working as pic 9(4) comp.
Also these variables are initialized prior the moves take place...

Thx for you reply

news:7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3799DEE7.D92EB14B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <932833211.356212@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>`

```
"Josua S." wrote:
> 
> Well, guess they have the correct value.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is the thought that I had.  I will take issue with 'initialized'.

In order for a group move to work the length of the receiving field
must be set before the move or else the length will be 'depend' upon
zero length and not do anything.  I did this myself recently
simplified example:

   read input
   perform until eof
         move input-rec to output-rec
         move input-rec-len to output-rec-len
         write output
         read input
   end-perform

Guess what my first record was rubbish, my second was partly rubbish,
the third was OK, and so one.

Moving the 'len' move moved the problem right out :->

If this is not the problem (as you seem to think it is not can we see
and bit more code.

Ken
www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Josua S." <fmff@esoterica.pt>

```
05 TABLE-SRC.

05 TABLE-DEST.
    10 REG-DEST OCCURS 1 TO 10 DEPENDING ON TBDEST.
         20 FILLER PIC X(99).

05 TABLE-TMP.

          20 FILLER    PIC X(80).
          20 MY-VAR PIC X(19).
          20 FILLER    PIC X.

05 TBSRC             PIC 9(04) COMP:
05 TBTMP             PIC 9(04) COMP.
05 TBDEST            PIC 9(04) COMP.
05 WS-COUNT     PIC 99.

TBSRC and the table TABLE-SRC are 'filled' with a call to another program.

Then, after calling it , i have the following in procedure :

        COMPUTE TBTMP = TBSRC / 100
         MOVE TBTMP  TO TBDEST
         MOVE TABLE-SRC TO TABLE-TMP
         MOVE 1 TO WS-COUNT
         PERFORM MODIFY-RECORD TBTMP TIMES.

MODIFY-RECORD.
          MOVE '0000000000000000000' TO MY-VAR
          MOVE REG-TMP(WS-COUNT) TO REG-DEST(WS-COUNT)
          ADD 1 TO WS-COUNT.

modify-record crash the program ? ...
```

```
In article <932842906.937363@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>, Josua S. wrote:
> 
> TBSRC and the table TABLE-SRC are 'filled' with a call to another program.

Here is the problem: the length of the table TABLE-SRC is re-calculated only 
when your program (not another program) changes explicitly TBSRC.

I am quite sure that if you insert ADD 0 to TBSRC after your call, all will be 
fine...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379C62C7.EBF613E1@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <932833211.356212@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <3799DEE7.D92EB14B@zip.com.au> <932842906.937363@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <379A74F4.3D7DC0BB@zip.com.au> <932921516.27493@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>`

```
"Josua S." wrote:
> 
> Sems you are right about the level one. It works with level one it
> seems ! Can explain me why ?
> 

OK,  This is difficult but I will try in English, easier with a white
board.

The problem is that you are saying that you have 100 bytes plus 100
bytes plus 99 bytes if all the sizes are 1. If all the sizes are 2 it
is 200, 200, 198.

The problem is that the start of the second 05 level has moved from
byte position 100 to byte position 200 by changing the size of the
first field.

What happens when you reference the first entry of the second 05. The
'real' values were stored starting at byte position 100 but you are
now looking for them at byte position 200.

Help me out here someone,
Ken

PS:  Are you getting an email copy as well?  I have upgraded to
Netscape 4.61 and it appears to be doing this of it's own volition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379CCB0C.9306EC22@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <932833211.356212@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <3799DEE7.D92EB14B@zip.com.au> <932842906.937363@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <379A74F4.3D7DC0BB@zip.com.au> <932921516.27493@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <379C62C7.EBF613E1@zip.com.au>`

```
The  non-subordinate fields within a group  that follow an  Occurs
Depending On are variably located. Their address is determined at the
time of reference. An 01 level following an ODO is not affected.  For
example:

01  ws-depends   PIC S9(4) COMP.
01 the-group.
    02  a-byte OCCURS 1 TO 10 DEPENDING ws-depends
                    PIC X.
    02  a-field-that-follows PIC X.

MOVE 10 TO  ws-depends
MOVE 'Z' TO a-field-that-follows .
DISPLAY a-field-that-follows               <<< displays a 'Z'
MOVE '1234567890' TO the-group.   <<< a-byte(ALL)  contains the entire
string and a-field-that-follows is located in position 11 of the group.
MOVE 3 to ws-depends.                     <<<<only 3 bytes of a-byte( )
are available, and the COBOL thinks a-field-that-follows is now in
position 4.
DISPLAY a-field-that-follows               <<< displays a '4'

Unless you intend to write an ODO table to an external medium, there is
no advantage to using an ODO in working storage.  As I stated earlier,
the table will still occupy the maximum size, only not all bytes will be
able to be referenced,  and the fields that follow will keep slip sliding
away.

Hope this helped.

Ken Foskey wrote:

> "Josua S." wrote:
> >
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Netscape 4.61 and it appears to be doing this of it's own volition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nijmt$bcd$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <932833211.356212@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <3799DEE7.D92EB14B@zip.com.au> <932842906.937363@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <379A74F4.3D7DC0BB@zip.com.au> <932921516.27493@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <379C62C7.EBF613E1@zip.com.au> <379CCB0C.9306EC22@ix.netcom.com>`

```
John Trifon wrote in message <379CCB0C.9306EC22@ix.netcom.com>...

>The  non-subordinate fields within a group  that follow an  Occurs
>Depending On are variably located. Their address is determined at the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>



Ummmm...  I'm not convinced.  ANSI X3.23-1985 Page VI-22 Paragraph 5.8.3(10)
states:

"A data description entry that contains format 2 of the OCCURS clause may
only be followed, within that record description, by data description
entries which are subordinate to it."

While my July 1997 copy of the draft indicates that this requirement will be
relaxed if/as/when that standard is adopted, was this limitation also
relaxed in the 1991 amendment?

If not, this declaration of "the-group" (in particular, the presence of
"a-field-that-follows" in its current location within "the-group")  violates
that rule of ANSI standard COBOL, ISTM.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379d3545.20396838@netnews>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <7ncfhc$iva@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <932833211.356212@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <3799DEE7.D92EB14B@zip.com.au> <932842906.937363@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <379A74F4.3D7DC0BB@zip.com.au> <932921516.27493@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <379C62C7.EBF613E1@zip.com.au> <379CCB0C.9306EC22@ix.netcom.com> <7nijmt$bcd$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 26 Jul 1999 14:25:18 -0700, when "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> John Trifon wrote in message <379CCB0C.9306EC22@ix.netcom.com>...
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >    02  a-field-that-follows PIC X.
 
> Ummmm...  I'm not convinced.  ANSI X3.23-1985 Page VI-22 Paragraph 5.8.3(10)
> states:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> relaxed in the 1991 amendment?

No and it's not in the current draft.  From CD 1.5, p 310, 13.16.36.2 SR
12:  "A data description entry that contains format 2 or format 3 of the
OCCURS clause may only be followed, within that record description, by
data entries that are subordinate to it."
```

```
I think that this is where usage overrides the standard.  I believe it
should be in the record description so that we can shrink records
based on that sort of coding.

records on an MVS mainframe.  It has multiple subgroups with occurs
clauses (0..1 and 0..n)
```

```
This thread did mention CICS earlier, so it is PROBABLY safe to believe what
it says about fixed data following an ODO (table), i.e. it is correct for
IBM mainframe COBOLs.  However, just in case anyone cares, this is VERY MUCH
a "no-no" in Standard COBOL and those implementations that do allow it often
implement it differently.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

- **From:** "Josua S." <fmff@esoterica.pt>
