```
Thx for your replies.

No, its not homework, and yes i would like to be educated.

The idea is this : i get a large stream of chars, in fact 100 to 1000. This
strem is logically divided in groups (records if you like) of 100 chars
each. So with my first move from table-src to table-tmp i get a table of
records instead of a table of bytes, so to say. In fact , and i didnt
mention it on my first post, the lenght of table-src is allways a multiple
of 100 ( because each record has 100 bytes and there is a max of 10
records). Now,the 19 bytes starting from position 81 of each record must be
changed to '0' (i ommited that move on my first post), and at last this
modified record (with 100 chars and with '0' betten position 81 and 99) must
be moved to a table where each entry has not 100 lenght but 99, so this new
table has the same number of entries as table-tmp but each entry has a byte
less.Now, the real problem is this statements are not part of a standart
cobol program but of a cobol/CICS program . Im new to cics and have no idea
on on to debug it ( pehaps someone can tell me how to display working
storage on terminal when a transaction is running ?).

Thx in advance



Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au...
> "Josua S."
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nci5g$bsj@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <932828613.867366@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>`

```
Josua S. <fmff@esoterica.pt> wrote in message
news:932828613.867366@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt...
  <much snippage>
>Now, the real problem is this statements are not part of a standart
> cobol program but of a cobol/CICS program . Im new to cics and have no
idea
> on on to debug it ( pehaps someone can tell me how to display working
> storage on terminal when a transaction is running ?).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Check with your site as to what interactive debugging tools they have for
CICS applications.  This might include Xpediter, IBM's "Debug Tool",
Intertest (I think they are still in existence) - if nothing else, you
should have CEDF (the "built-in" debugger for CICS).  None of these are
completely "intuitively obvious" - so you will need at least a little
training to get started.

You have mentioned "reading in a record" which under CICS *should* mean that
you are doing EXEC CICS commands.  This too can SIGNIFICANTLY impact what
and how ODOs (Occurs Depending ON) work (depending on which type of command
and which operands you are using).

This may not be homework, but it does sound to me (personal opinion only)
like you are at least "learning" CICS and how it is used at your shop.  I
would hope that you either get some "formal" training or have someone
locally to help you.  There really are LOTS and lots of differences between
"batch" COBOL programming and CICS programming.  (They all seem minor once
you know them, but you do need to learn them sometime - somehow.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3799fa93@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <932828613.867366@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>`

```
Joshua,

Had you given us this information up front,
nobody would have thought of homework
and everyone would have been spared
several unnecessary postings.

No need to worry about what is legal  and
what CICS does.

Why not code a single straightforward solution:

01  larger-stream.
     02  source pic x(100) occurs 10 times.

01  smaller-stream.
     02  result occurs 10 times.
          03  result-1   pic x(80).
          03  result-2   pic x(19).

      perform set-result
          varying idx from 1 by 1
            until idx > 10 (or whatever)
      .

set-result.
     move source(idx) to result-1(idx)
     move  zeroes        to result-2(idx)
     .

Maybe you can even get by by a simple redefinition of
the larger stream (smaller stream redefines larger-stream),
so you can avoid moving to result-1.

Josua S. <fmff@esoterica.pt> wrote in message
news:932828613.867366@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt...
> Thx for your replies.
>
> No, its not homework, and yes i would like to be educated.
>
> The idea is this : i get a large stream of chars, in fact 100 to 1000.
This
> strem is logically divided in groups (records if you like) of 100 chars
> each. So with my first move from table-src to table-tmp i get a table of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> records). Now,the 19 bytes starting from position 81 of each record must
be
> changed to '0' (i ommited that move on my first post), and at last this
> modified record (with 100 chars and with '0' betten position 81 and 99)
must
> be moved to a table where each entry has not 100 lenght but 99, so this
new
> table has the same number of entries as table-tmp but each entry has a
byte
> less.Now, the real problem is this statements are not part of a standart
> cobol program but of a cobol/CICS program . Im new to cics and have no
idea
> on on to debug it ( pehaps someone can tell me how to display working
> storage on terminal when a transaction is running ?).
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379AED62.6304D857@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<932767446.462055@nurn.esoterica.pt> <379976F8.84A51818@zip.com.au> <932828613.867366@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>`

```
Since these tables are in working storage, there is nothing to be gained by
defining them as variable. This will only cause COBOL to recalculate the length
of the table each time it is referenced. It will still occupy the max size no
matter what the contents of the object of the 'depending on' clause (although
all the occurrences of the  table may not be able to be referenced). I will
assume you intend to write the contents of table-dest out to a file using the
table-dest as the record. In this case the 'depending on' is fine. There are
efficiencies that can be gained by using indexes rather than subscripts. I will
leave that to you.

Anyway, try these changes -

05 TABLE-SRC.
     20 FILLER OCCURS 1000.
          25 FILLER     PIC X.
05 TABLE-TMP redefines TABLE-SRC.
     10 REG-TMP OCCURS 10.
         20 FILLER    PIC X(80).
          20 MY-VAR PIC X(19).
          20 FILLER    PIC X.

05 TABLE-DEST.
    10 REG-DEST OCCURS 1 TO 10 DEPENDING ON TBDEST.
         20 FILLER PIC X(99).

05 TBSRC             PIC 9(04) COMP:
**05 TBTMP             PIC 9(04) COMP. << not needed
05 TBDEST            PIC 9(04) COMP.
05 WS-COUNT     PIC 99.

        COMPUTE TBdest = TBSRC / 100
**     MOVE TBTMP  TO TBDEST                <<not needed
**     MOVE TABLE-SRC TO TABLE-TMP  << not needed - use redefine
         MOVE 1 TO WS-COUNT
         PERFORM MODIFY-RECORD TBTMP TIMES.

MODIFY-RECORD.
          MOVE zero  TO MY-VAR (ws-count)   <<my-var needs an index/subscript
<< use figurative constant - you won't have to count zeros.
          MOVE REG-TMP(WS-COUNT) TO REG-DEST(WS-COUNT)
          ADD 1 TO WS-COUNT.



"Josua S." wrote:

> Thx for your replies.
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Josua S." <fmff@esoterica.pt>

```
I think i have fixed it !
I got the fujitsu compiler from Internet
and tried to compile the program but with some displays.
It compiled and runned ok but after i set the length of table-dest some of
the displays of TABLE-DEST, TABLE-SRC and TABLE-TEMP were wrong !!
It puts garbage on some fields, change contents in anothers... a really
mess.
Now i tried this : changed all level 5 tables by level 01 so :
01 WORKING-FIELDS.
      05 TABLE-SRC.
           10 etc
      05 TABLE-DEST.
          10 etc
  etc
Becomes:
      01 TABLE-SRC.
           10 etc
      01 TABLE-DEST.
          10 etc

Well it works now fine, i mean at least the displays are all fine .
Probably it crashed because i only wrote a small subset of the program ; the
real program has a lot of numeric variables and hopefully they get garbage
and crash the program.

Now, I dont understand why this works fine this way :)
Can anyone please explain me ?

Thx in advance
```

## Thread: GUI uisng COBOL and ocx — 12 this month / 14 total

### GUI uisng COBOL and ocx

- **From:** "Michael Poon" <mpoon8@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PAjm3.6481$um6.74355@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I purchased the book called Mastering COBOL. This book is included a free
version of Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 and I found it out that this is a nice
reference book for COBOL programming. Actually, I am not a COBOL programming
and I want to know more GUI programming by using COBOL. So, I tried out one
of the section in that book and realized that there is a timer control
object is missing on that Free version of Fujitsu 4.0. I feel that I can
import the timer object from VB or other languages. If anyone know the
answer, pls. help me out. I think we need to deal with how to register the
object and how to import OCX  into the Cobol application.

MP
```

#### ↳ Re: GUI uisng COBOL and ocx

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nf6fu$ope$3@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<PAjm3.6481$um6.74355@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I have been watching this post for a day or so, and I notice that no one has
answered.  If I understand you correctly, you want to use the OCX object in
a standard Cobol program.

As designed, those objects are meant to be compatible with Power Cobol.
Power Cobol is a separate system that writes Cobol code. It is a full GUI
Cobol program builder.

I stopped using Power Cobol, largely because I could not maintain the code
that I wrote in it.  It is scattered all over, and kept in a proprietary
format so that you cannot even search it with a text editor.

I also found that the tools I built with it were not callable from the
standard Cobol ... you cannot write a Power Cobol subroutine, though you can
call Cobol subroutines from Power Cobol. (maybe you can, but nobody knows
how).

However, this particular topic is quite an interesting idea.  From a
theoretical standpoint, we should be able to use any object from any OO
capable language, and Fujitsu Cobol Version 4.0 is just that.

So I have a question for all those OO fanatics.  Given that I have a DLL
containing an object, written in any object oriented language including
Cobol, how can I invoke it from a standard Cobol program? How do I set up
the linkage, etc., so that it finds the appropriate code?

Michael Poon wrote in message ...
>I purchased the book called Mastering COBOL. This book is included a free
>version of Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 and I found it out that this is a nice
>reference book for COBOL programming. Actually, I am not a COBOL
programming
>and I want to know more GUI programming by using COBOL. So, I tried out one
>of the section in that book and realized that there is a timer control
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I won't speak for its success, but I will say that one of the GOALS of the
OO COBOL in the draft Standard is that it WILL allow you to call (invoke)
non-COBOL objects and vice versa. On the other hand, several of the
"built-in" features of the draft that allow this haven't been implemented in
any current compiler that I know of.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI uisng COBOL and ocx

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ng4t8$cqs@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<PAjm3.6481$um6.74355@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <7nf6fu$ope$3@news.igs.net> <7nffqe$68h@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7nfusk$777$3@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:7nfusk$777$3@news.igs.net...
> I hope at the very least all Cobol objects will be compatible at some
level.
>
  <much snippage>

Well, yes and no.

There was an interpretation request against the '85 Standard - concerning
what happens in "'85 Standard COBOL if a COBOL program compiled with
compiler-xyz calls a COBOL program compiled with compiler-abc - do the rules
of Standard COBOL apply - or do the rules of 'inter-language' CALLs apply".
The bottom-line (according to J4) was that the rules of inter-language CALLs
apply.  For example, EXTERNAL data defined in one program need NOT be
"recognized" by the other COBOL program.

One of the goals of ALL "OO" theory (as I understand it) is to "hide" the
internals of an object - but to use only "externalized interfaces" to invoke
methods on such objects.  Therefore, such invocations SHOULD work from COBOL
program to COBOL program to COBOL object to COBOL object - regardless of
which compiler is used (or even if it is a COBOL compiler) as long as the
"appropriate" external interface is used - and you don't rely on any
"internals" being the same in the multiple objects (such as sharing EXTERNAL
data, files with the same formats, DISPLAY statements going to the same
devices, etc, etc).

Does this help explain what I think you will and will NOT be able to count
on - in such cases (assuming the draft ever becomes approved)?
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Does this help explain what I think you will and will NOT be able to > count on - in such cases (assuming the draft ever becomes approved)?
> 
Bill, it only gets worse. You used to say "if", "but" "maybe"; seems to
me, "assuming the draft ever becomes approved" is definitely a minus for
COBOL's future. What ARE the Standards people doing ! 

If they said "Yes" to the existing draft tomorrow, realistically you've
got to allow a two-year time-frame for people to re-code their
compilers. 2000-Standard = 2002-compiler, ........., 2010-Standard =
2012-Compiler - Don't worry Warren, (take another Prozac), we can both
discuss it with Grace before 2012 comes out.

Jimmy, Calgary AB 


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Standard - WHEN (was: GUI uisng COBOL and ocx

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ngk41$j95@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<PAjm3.6481$um6.74355@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <7nf6fu$ope$3@news.igs.net> <7nffqe$68h@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <7nfusk$777$3@news.igs.net> <7ng4t8$cqs@dfw-ixnews13.ix.netcom.com> <379BCA36.25AEBF30@home.com>`

```
So far, I haven't heard a summary much less seen the minutes of the J4
meeting that just took place.  To be fair,  it is my impression that the
current voting members of J4 really do believe that they will be delivering
an "acceptable" draft from J4 to WG4 in January 2000.  Then assuming all
goes well, there really, REALLY may be an "approved" Standard at the end of
2000 or beginning of 2001.

I have personal doubts about each and every step of this schedule - but
those who believe it SHOULD know better than I do.

As far as implementations go, you might want to look at the extensions (OO
and non-OO) in the latest release of Micro Focus (MERANT) NetExpress.  It is
my understanding that they have already implemented a "good-sized chunk" of
the draft Standard. Similarly, both IBM and Fujitsu most definitely have
produced OO products that match at least some variation of the draft
Standard.  In theory, many of the COBOL vendors have said to J4 that they
have been looking at the spec and "estimating" the work involved in
implementing it.  Again, I have personal doubts - but implementations may
well be sooner than some might think/fear.

ON THE OTHER HAND, my biggest worry is that NO vendor will EVER implement
this next Standard - even after it is "fully" approved.  In the US, the fact
that there are no longer people maintaining (much less enhancing) the NIST
certification test; the fact that there is significant evidence that there
will never be another FIPS Standard for COBOL (historically required when
selling compilers to the government); and the fact that there seems to be a
diminishing interest in COBOL in general among the major software/hardware
vendors - all make me question whether there ever will be a "conforming"
implementation of this next Standard - again assuming there is a next
Standard.

I do need to re-iterate that I am well known as the "extreme pessimist"
among the COBOL Standards committees, so anything that I say needs to be
taken with a LARGE grain of salt. But on the other hand, so far I really
think that my Cassandra-like pessimism has proven to be depressingly
accurate.
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
>

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

You have hit the nail on the head.  There is no standard of
interchange between disparate object types.  Dare I mention the
difference of COMP-3 definitions in Cobol ;-}

The powers that be have invented Corba or Microsoft has built COM to
take care of this issue.  They create a wrapper to allow cross machine
and cross compiler calls.  Have I actually done this, no.  There is a
free micro corba available, read an article about it a while ago.

Ken
```

```
as OO, object code.  OOP's programming only exists as source code ... once
you compile it it is no longer sharable?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI uisng COBOL and ocx

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Steve Biggs <steve_biggs@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7njshr$r6d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<PAjm3.6481$um6.74355@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <7nf6fu$ope$3@news.igs.net> <379B245F.56F37309@zip.com.au> <7nfusk$777$2@news.igs.net>`

```
"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
> What you are telling me is that there are OOp compilers, but no such
thing
> as OO, object code.  OOP's programming only exists as source code ...
once
> you compile it it is no longer sharable?
>
> <he says wide eyed>

Well, if what you mean is "is there no single standard for OO languages
to call each others objects?" then I don't believe that there is. Each
language has their own default way of exporting objects and methods
"natively" - for Java it is JNI, for C++, mangled signatures etc.

The solution, as Ken suggested, is Microsoft COM or CORBA ORBs, which
were designed to be suitable for this job (and include remote object
connections as a bonus). These match up quite well with the OO
definition for the new COBOL standard (which, as Willian Klein said,
was designed towards this), and I'm sure that several companies have
products that already exploit this (including my own, MERANT, which has
COM/OLE/ActiveX and CORBA support) or will do so.

Steve.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic
- **Message-ID:** `<mInm3.469$ha4.16116@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
On July 19, 1999 I posted a request for volunteers to read and comment on a
document I will be making available (free)  on COBOL datatypes and
converting that data for use in BASIC or C/C++ programs; and the
ASCII/EBCDIC ramifications of these conversions/data movements.

I received a double-digit number of replies, and just wanted to say that I
sincerely appreciate and thank all who volunteered.

I have selected a group of three who, collectively, possess the exact mix of
skills I sought, and the paper is now in their hands.

Thanks also to Bob Wolfe of Flexus, who had earlier graciously agreed to
supply web space.

Give me two or three weeks to get feedback and clean it up, and when
questions come up about converting datatypes, there will be, in essence, a
"FAQ" for those who need it.

(PS: I have recently discovered the "save as HTML" feature of both my
word-processors. Would this be a desirable format for this document?)

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

## Thread: All — 2 msgs

### All

- **From:** "Ludo Knaepen" <ludo.knaepen@planetinternet.be>

- **Message-ID:** `<7nbonh$4sr$1@news.planetinternet.be>`

```
Hello All,

I was planning to use netexpress, can someone tell me he's impressions about
netexpress
difficulty on a scale frome one to ten.

Please help

Ludo Knaepen
rvcomp@unicall.be
```

#### ↳ Re: All - Using NetExpress

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379A0DCF.4ACE9B1D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7nbonh$4sr$1@news.planetinternet.be>`

```
Ludo Knaepen wrote:
> 
> Hello All,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> rvcomp@unicall.be

Depends upon your answers to the following :-

(a) Are you evaluating N/E against the other PC Compilers - if so then
you should go to their respective sites and make your own evaluation of
what each has to offer and determine the best fit to your needs

(b) If you have concluded that N/E is the product for you - what is your
existing background, are you comfortable with Windowing techniques etc.
How or what do you want to use N/E for :-

- OO/GUI - Frankly OO is a snip - and I'll give it a 2 - providing you
read a couple of background books - go to objectz.com

 - GUI - 'particularly' if you are not into Windows already - it hits 9
or 10, (lack of examples - have to keep referring back to the C text
books for examples) - if you persist with a combination of OO/GUI you
can do a lot more than the other approaches - but it's that dreaded 9/10
factor.

For the remainder contact the following users. Please don't bother them
if you are just evaluating.

- Windows APIs - and uses Fileshare  

  Ken Mullins KenMullins@mindspring.com

- Dialog System - MicroFocus toolkit to design screens

  Raymond W. Leech rleech@ecsi.net

- OO plus I believe, some D/S and structured :-

  Calin Macrinici cmac@pathcom.com

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
****************************************************************************
***
POSITION Consultant
SECURITY Enhanced Reliability

PROFILE

As a Programmer/Analyst, I have been involved in the support and enhancement
```

```
No one has volunteered for this service.  I offer to create a page of
comments on all cobol compilers.  The information will be very simply
presented and supplied by email by you...

I reserve the right to not post anything I deem irrelevant.  I will
suppress any poster's id who requests it but you must supply me with
public address information (must contain country of origin)  and
private address information (real name, basic locality info, email
contact).  I have absolutely no economic reason to Spam anyone, I get
paid for 40 hours and don't consult.

Vendors are advised to submit a a URL to post on this page.  I also
request a working support email, so that I can forward controversial
issues to you for clarification to also post on the page.

All updates will be handled by email or newsgroups, no frills.  Debate
may be taken up with newsgroups if you deem my deeming incorrect.

I have no axes to grind and I fully support Fujitsu as the best
compiler for PC's.  (That's bait Cheesle :-})

To quote Bill:  It's your turn.

Ken
That's one way to get my click counter above 10.
```

<a id="t-a4a9abacab"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and ASP — 4 msgs

### COBOL and ASP

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379a95e8.87583881@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I was visiting my book in a B&N and picked up an ASP book and read
about it some.   What I found was that for CGI a separate instance of
the application is started - including any runtime setup overhead -
but for ASP a DLL is loaded in memory and used.  Has anyone here done
any COBOL and ASP work?  I'd like the hear more about it.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and ASP

- **From:** "J.Reynaert" <newsreader@kjsgroup.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<932906836.704.65@news.remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<379a95e8.87583881@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane

Yes, I have done extensive work in this area.  Did you have
some specific question ?

Overhead, IHMO, not as much as running an SQL server.
COBOL compilers/runtimes tend to be very compact.
Convenience, well SQL or Access has some advantage here.
Reusable, if you have a lot of COBOL code (which presumably
if you are here, you do) then creating ASP front ends is not
that difficult depending of course on the code.

I found the simple Visual Object Cobol product from Microfocus
to be the cleanest to work with for this type of work.
 (I have looked at the eval of their Netexpress product,
but in the end didn't buy it. I felt I was not treated properly
on the Vis Ob Cob product. Since they abandoned it without
warning about the same time they cashed my cheque.)
There are some issues with the runtime of VisObCob.
I also have their full Version 4 WB.

As I don't visit here with any schedule, if you want to
know more e-mail me   kjs at kjsgroup dot com

John
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and ASP

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GdNm3.4893$OP.53387@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:379a95e8.87583881@news1.ibm.net...
> I was visiting my book in a B&N and picked up an ASP book and read
> about it some.   What I found was that for CGI a separate instance of
> the application is started - including any runtime setup overhead -
> but for ASP a DLL is loaded in memory and used.  Has anyone here done
> any COBOL and ASP work?  I'd like the hear more about it.

the DLL in this case is likely to be plugged into the webserver (Microsoft's
IIS) as an ISAPI DLL (it's essentially a multi-threaded DLL that stays
loaded vs. something that is process based that is loaded and unloaded each
time).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and ASP

- **From:** "Robert Kovacic" <rjk@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<owQm3.13543$yD2.29961@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<379a95e8.87583881@news1.ibm.net>`

```
If you have an ODBC driver for your COBOL data files then you can program
with ASP without restriction.

If you are interested in "Internet programming" and COBOL you should have a
look at what NetExpress 3.0 could offer you. For example, you could write
COBOL Internet programmes which take advantage of the ISAPI interface to the
MS Internet Server to give the efficiency advantages which I think you are
alluding to.
```

- **From:** you@somehost.somedomain (AJG)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ynum3.312$Fe4.43710@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
I am currently working on a project that required that I provide "packed 
fields" in data file dump.  I believe packed data to a compress numeric field?!

Question: Is it possible to provide "packed fields" in either a binary or ASCII
file from SUN (Solaris/Unix) machine?  

Thank you in advance for your help.

PS... I might have work for the individual that answers this question.


-AJG


Please email your answer to:


andy@conetic.com

Thanks!!!!!  ALOT.
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP! Packed Field Question

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7neqv0$ifs$3@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Ynum3.312$Fe4.43710@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
You sound like a student fishing.  You can "pack" data on any platform, in
any language.  Why would a project "require" a dump?  A dump is usually done
in desperation, when one has a project that does not work and you are trying
to find out why not.

Packed data, in Cobol, refers to numeric data stored two nibbles per byte.

PS - not interested in a job, have one.
```

```
Why, yes, it is.
```

## Thread: A thought — 25 this month / 42 total

### A thought

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net>`

```
It occurs to me that rather than a "Cobol Standard", we need a "Standard
Cobol".  The former seems unobtainable without politics, the latter only
with politicians.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

```
Ken Foskey wrote in message <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au>...
>donald tees wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>not serving our interests.

Perhaps what we should really try to do is to put together a decent
programming specification, and live with it.  It *is* possible to write
standard Cobol that is cross platform compatible, and within the standard.

The real problem with vendor specific stuff is not that it is there, but
that we use it, and when we do, we do not isolate it into subroutines. That
was reasonable five years ago.  Machines were smaller, and eventually one
just has to make it work. To-day, it would be almost impossible to write
code that will not fit even on a micro. (Unless you are Thane, of course,
and make 14 megabyte arrays<G>).  The overhead of subroutine calls is just
not great enough to justify the conversion costs of going through thousands
of lines of code every time a new platform comes out.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Well, this thread is exciting as it involves thinking.
To add my 2 cents, here are some memories.

A vendor set us up with a method for running our applications.  We used it
extensively.
We built procedures around it.

The vendor sold us another model of hardware for another site.  They did not
support the
same method for running our applications thus setting up a set of problems
we had to overcome.
One way would have been to design a new set of procedures which meant that
people at one site could not understand the methods at the other site
without training.

So, we said, even if your different hardware does not easily support the
method you provided on the first computer, we insist that you find a way for
that method to work on the other computer.

It took awhile, and market influences, but they figured out how to do it.

Another vendor built a system with RPN (reverse Polish notation) as the
hardware method.
Their marketing thought that the system would appeal to a certain group of
potential users.
The market proved to be driven by Data Processing types, and the hardware
while good, was not
as good as it could have been in it's handling of COBOL programs. So, when
the next generation came along, their Engineers, and Marketing determined
that it was in their best interest to revise the system in other ways.  As a
result, the COBOL move was accomplished with one instruction vs. 90+ in the
prior model of the computer.

The computer industry is replete with examples where user "influences" via
independent development or market pressure of one sort or another made
significant changes in the hardware
and the software.

This has happen a lot to accommodate COBOL even in the attempted hardware to
reduce instruction sets.

So, I believe, and wish that users would see that the things that they now
have to go to VB, C or other languages to get are reasonable features to add
to COBOL.  And based upon the OOP discussion currently going on, I see not
reason to add the OOP to the name of the language because certain behavior
results from features added to the language.  The tower of Babel has to stop
somewhere.

Warren Simmons
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net...
>
> Ken Foskey wrote in message <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au>...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> that we use it, and when we do, we do not isolate it into subroutines.
That
> was reasonable five years ago.  Machines were smaller, and eventually one
> just has to make it work. To-day, it would be almost impossible to write
> code that will not fit even on a micro. (Unless you are Thane, of course,
> and make 14 megabyte arrays<G>).  The overhead of subroutine calls is just
> not great enough to justify the conversion costs of going through
thousands
> of lines of code every time a new platform comes out.
>

>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```

Warren Simmons wrote in message <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>...
[...]
>... And based upon the OOP discussion currently going on, I see not
>reason to add the OOP to the name of the language because certain behavior
>results from features added to the language.  The tower of Babel has to
stop
>somewhere.

When I and others use OO or OOP in combination with a language
name, I mean (and I understand others to mean) "(use of) the OO
features of the language." Hence the term OO COBOL refers to
the OO features of COBOL or to the use of those features.

There are more than 2300 language names, including this interesting
jewel.

A0 or A-0 - Grace Hopper's team at Remington Rand, 1952, for the
UNIVAC I or II.  Possibly the first compiler ever.  Later internal versions:
A-1, A-2 ("The A-2 Compiler System", Rem Rand, 1955), A-3, AT-3.
AT-3 was released as MATH-MATIC.  Sammet 1969, p.12.

A list is available from:
    < ftp://wuarchive.wustl.edu/doc/misc/lang-list.txt >.

As long as there are computers, new languages will be invented
and re-invented to suit the needs of the inventor. A few of these
languages will also fit the needs of users. Many of these languages
will have a name and each will add one more brick to the tower.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> There are more than 2300 language names, including this interesting
>> jewel...........
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>all these 'neat' new languages

There is no reason to be appalled!

Many of these languages are old and only worked on specific
hardware; some were used only for research or teaching and
never became production languages; some never ran on any
computer, like the following excerpt, chosen especially for you,
from the language list:

ADD 1 TO COBOL GIVING COBOL - Bruce Clement.
Tongue-in-cheek suggestion for an object-oriented COBOL.
SIGPLAN Notices 27(4):90-91 (Apr 1992).

One important use for new languages is in the area of
research with existing languages. Take and idea, add it to
a language, and see what happens. Without such research,
there would be few if any advances in language design or
implementation.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com>...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> implementation.

I buy into the above statement. But take a p-u-r-e-l-y hypothetical
situation. COBOL is an all-singing all-dancing language continually
updated, say every 2 years/18 months, (even better if it was every
year), new data types, new techniques etc.... based on
suggestions/gripes from us the developers, and it is also Open
Source/Open COBOL.

Now suppose such a situation prevailed in 1970. Along come the Xerox
PARC team - they want to do simulation, (Simula/Smalltalk). Here's this
magnificent COBOL animal which already works, with the ability to be
flexible. Why not just add new data types, routines, or OO classes to
achieve what they want ? Given this 'ideal' situation - I wonder what
would have been the reaction of Adele Goldberg and other 'Smalltalkers'.

Interestingly, I saw, I think, in "OO Programming Languages....", a
reference by a 'Smalltalker' that SmallTalk was overkill for GUIs.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <37A13777.64835D30@home.com>...
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Source/Open COBOL.

I think I read somewhere (or it was implied) that the minimum time
between ISO standards was five years. Even if that is not quite
correct, there are about two years worth of mandatory review periods.
This means that an update to the COBOL standard, more frequently
than that, is not possible.

The open source movement is not about having others make
changes for us. It is about us being able to make changes for
ourselves. The license for open source code generally requires
that changes made to open source code also be made freely
available to others. It is the Others who will decide whether any
individual's extension to the language will appear in the Other's
open source implementations. As such, each individual or group
that maintains an open source implementation is equivalent to
a vendor. Those who use the implementation unchanged are
equivalent to customers, with no assurance of support.

This lack of assurance of support is what has prevented large
users from embracing the open source movement. Red Hat
and other companies are beginning to overcome this reluctance
by providing that support. In the absence of organizations that
are willing to guarantee support for various implementations
of open source COBOL, there will be no wide-spread adoption
of the language extensions among large users and no pressure
for current vendors to accept those extensions into their
implementations. Without this wide-spead adoption, there is
little chance that the extensions will become part of the COBOL
standard.

I think open source code is a great idea; well, I thought it was
a great idea, until I downloaded the GNU distribution. Maybe
I could work with open COBOL source code; but I couldn't
work with the GNU C source code. Obviously, it works and
works well, I even ported flex (equivalent to Unix lex) to VC 5.0;
but I couldn't get past the programming style of the source
code enough to determine how it actually worked. I made a
decision very quickly. I decided that since that style was a
requirement of participation in GNU, I would not participate!
Considering how much reaction coding style opinions create
in this newsgroup, I wonder how many different open source
groups would need to be created just to satisfy those style
differences and how many more to cover implementation
differences.

>Now suppose such a situation prevailed in 1970. Along come the Xerox
>PARC team - they want to do simulation, (Simula/Smalltalk). Here's this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>would have been the reaction of Adele Goldberg and other 'Smalltalkers'.

Of this I am certain, all of the great and marvelous technological
advances come from the minds of individuals or from small groups
led by an individual with a strong sense of purpose. And that to
achieve those advances, the individual and group must ignore
convention. That is, to achieve great advances in COBOL one
must ignore COBOL standards and standard COBOL. It is only
after the advance has been made and the concept proven that
one may then integrate the advance into the language proper.

Were there no Simula, Smalltalk, or other OO languages, a new
language would have to be invented to add OO features to the
COBOL standard.

Does this mean that COBOL could not serve as a basis for that
new language? No! The situation is similar to C versus C++ or
Pascal versus Modula. If the advance cannot be accomodated by
the language as determined by the standards committee, then
users will determine whether the advance is great or merely
interesting.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
news:7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net...
  <snip>
>
> I think I read somewhere (or it was implied) that the minimum time
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
  <more snippage>

Interestingly enough "5 years" is the *MAXIMUM* not the minimum that there
is suppose to be between Standard.  There is a procedure for getting a 5
year extension (which does require a public review - to make certain that
the Standard is still relevant) - but IN THEORY, you aren't suppose to EVER
go over 10 years.

Notice the use of "in theory" and then compare it with COBOL's history.  The
'85 Standard (amended by the '89 addendum) did go thru a "re-affirmation" in
'91 (I think) but they have been "playing games" since '96 when that
reaffirmation was suppose to be "up".

(This is the ANSI rules.  I think the ISO rules are similar, but I can't
swear to it.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> interesting.

Rick,

Now you've hit the nail on the head. Warren Simmonds wrote earlier about
'putting COBOL into the public domain'. Ken Foskey was a little more
blunt :-

> We programmers need to take control, the politicians and vendors are
> not serving our interests.

I accept Warren's reluctance to leave retirement and dive right in, but
from the little nuggets he has given me privately, he could illustrate
his involvement with some of the early COBOL standards.

I don't think I've said so specifically before - but as regards
Standards, now is the time to move on to a new approach.

Let me re-cap on the English language. Developed over the centuries and 
freely hijacks from other languages to express ideas.  Dr. Johnson I
believe, was the first to create an English Dictionary - this is how it
is written, this is how words are spelled. Our modern versions are the
Oxford in the UK and the premiere (?) in the States, Webster's.

Oxford/Webster are the standards, but they play catch-up. Word usage
evolves from every day use and these tomes then add them to the
language, after the event.

I think that COBOL Standards (all those funny initials), have done a
remarkable job cobbling together a language 40 years ago, which still
stands the test of time. Our 'adjudicator' Bill Klein keeps us in line
on syntax and conveys info about coming standards. The latter with a
great feeling of depression, for somebody who is retired from COBOL but
still loves the language. He refers to himself as maybe a Cassandra - I
think not; he is reading it as it is, and from his experience knows all
the stumbling blocks associated with COBOL standards, ISO, ANSI,
whatever.

Advances in COBOL are just not happening FAST ENOUGH to keep up with
technology. I don't know the inner-workings of our standards but suspect
that a pecuniary interest, (Can we make bucks ?), is heavily weighted in
the decision making process. Consequently COBOL programmers have had to
turn to other languages for missing data types or to do specific things.

The phrase "The customer is always right", applies in this instance. As
consumers we are just not getting what we want. And as Ken Foskey
implies, with or without the co-operation of compiler vendors, as
consumers we have to take charge. Possibly GNU-COBOL might be a starting
point, albeit with imperfections, as you have noted. Let me be categoric
- under no circumstances should such an approach deviate from our
current syntax, but merely add to it.

> Of this I am certain, all of the great and marvelous technological
> advances come from the minds of individuals or from small groups
> led by an individual with a strong sense of purpose. And that to
> achieve those advances, the individual and group must ignore
> convention. That is, to achieve great advances in COBOL one.....

That really says it, doesn't it. To hell with convention! We need a
'leader' on this. No, not yours truly, too old and not bits/bytes
oriented. Don't mind taking a smack at the canvas with a large brush, or
even contributing - but I make no bid to be our new leader.

And please note, you sceptics, I haven't used "OO" once in the above
blurb !

Any thoughts, Ladies/Gentlemen ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ntdgn$8j6@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com>`

```
A few points on this thread (not including anyone's posts so far - but
starting on a "fresh" tack).

1) Whatever problems I have with the members of the various Standards
committees, I often repeat that they are ALL remarkably "unbiased" in their
input and votes.  I do *not* think that it is either trying to "protect"
their proprietary products or extensions that delay the Standard (and its
enhancements).

2) One of the biggest "problems" in COBOL development is the STRONG (almost
absolute) requirement that "new things" must be "upwardly compatible" with
the last Standard.  I understand the reason for this, but for anyone who has
worked with the existing Standard, it is INCREDIBLY full of bugs,
inconsistencies, and ambiguities.  Every time ANY of us try to write a
proposal for "new stuff," we run into problems with the existing Standard -
and its errors.  Unfortunately, not all vendors have made the same
assumptions about what the (existing) Standard means in these situations -
so each time we try and "fix" a problem (while adding new stuff) - we run
into at least one implementation where the users would KILL us if we went
that way.  (Compare the frequent posts in this NG about IBM and File Status
39. This never SHOULD have been a problem, but users had grown accustom to
and learn to code to a "loop-hole" in earlier IBM implementations.)

3) Since the '85 Standard, there have been a  number of "consortia" created
which attempted to create new COBOL Standards.  If anyone in this NG ever
read the X/Open COBOL Standard, you would just about "die" trying to figure
out what it was trying to say (I know that the implementors did).  With the
US Federal Government (FIPS and NIST) getting out of the COBOL business, I
don't know what force will be able to give an appropriate "stamp of
approval" on any variation of a new specification to make it popular.
(Personally, I just do not think that major corporations are going to "bet
their business-critical" applications on a programmer-driven definition ...
But I could be wrong.)

4) Once upon a time and far away ... When IBM ruled the Mainframe world
(well sort-of) and where COBOL ruled the IBM mainframes (well sort-of), it
was possible for a single vendor to create a "de facto" Standard.  That is
why COMP-3 means Packed-Decimal in so many implementations and why GOBACK is
a useful verb in those same products.  Again, I don't see this happening
with COBOL now or in the near future.  (Remember my post a few weeks ago
where I was asking if *ANY* reader of this NG knew of a company with more
than 5 programmers that used Fujitsu COBOL?  So far, I have yet to hear of
ONE.  On the other hand, IBM, CA-Reallia, and Micro Focus seem to have just
"written off" the individual COBOL developer.  Likewise, I haven't seen
PerCOBOL making a huge dent yet - but time may tell.  Without a market
leader (dare I say it - verging on a monopoly), I don't see any one
implementation (sold or free) taking the lead in defining the language.
Again, I could be wrong - but that is my impression.

5) Finally, we do need to talk about the "reputation" that COBOL has among
senior management *and* academics.  The former view it as archaic and the
latter view it as irrelevant.  (A generalization - but not far from true).
Again, I don't see this changing and as long as it doesn't, the future for
COBOL is pretty similar to that of Assembler - it won't die, but it will
hardly thrive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A24C35.2A3312ED@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com> <7nrnci$7ua$1@news.hitter.net> <37A20E3C.7F633397@home.com> <7ntdgn$8j6@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
...snipping some excellent stuff about standards and ensuring change
works...

Bill,

This post hits the nail on the head.  Let us look at the success of
GNU C.

1.  It really is the leader in language advances.  It has led the
charge in language changes.

2.  It is maintainable in the language that the language is written
in.

3.  It is a very complete implementation that runs on every platform. 
It becomes the defacto portability standard (e.g. My program is
written in Borland C but compiles with GNU so it will go everywhere).

The GNU cobol:

Requires maintainers to know C and Cobol.  This requires them also to
keep BOTH language definitions active in their minds when writing.

Isn't there yet and has been going a fairly long time.

Cobol source compiler:

Possible but it not really it's forte.  Yes I know there are compilers
written in it.

It is not the 'norm' for cobol programers, now is it.  Compilers are
written by C programmers.

Vendor cobol:

IBM has a policy of not fixing a problem on the first report.  This is
due to the fact that change destabilizes the product and if it only
for one report then don't change it, it might break something else.  I
can understand the idea and actually agree but it still frustrates me.

I cannot redefine the language elements to try a new process, issue a
patch to the world to test whether it is generally accepted.  It then
becomes an industry defacto standard.

As you pointed out there is no longer a clear leader.

The standards process itself:

I have been reading Constantine on peopleware (good stuff worth a
read). He talks about standards and how they evolve and talks about
motor cars. They evolved naturally e.g. tillers gave way to round
steering wheels, etc.  We need backwards compatibility but would it be
easier to allow a specific command in Cobol to change the language
form from old to new.  It may make it more complex for the vendors but
it would allow growth not stagnation.


51.5 cents (48.5 cents to the tax man)
Ken
```

```
Capt. Nemo wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

I take your point. But in my case being the CEO, programming team and
the chief bottle washer, and having put out the bucks for
Visoc/NetExpress - I am very definitely the 'customer'.

I discussed this topic with my end-user yesteraday afternoon. He is
technically very competent, and is the one who reads all the magazines.
He still gets update letters from Liant/RM which we no longer use. He
would dearly love to get involved in programming, (COBOL has stood him
and me in good stead over the years), but just doesn't have the time.

About two months ago he made the observation to me "COBOL is free" i.e.,
the original concept, "It's the compilers we have to pay for". Not just
restricted to COBOL, he also made the comment that there is too much
tunnel-vision, people in technical areas just don't seem to appreciate
looking at the big picture. (Capt. Nemo - I hope you haven't got your
thoughts wallowing 2,000 leagues under the sea).

A comment he made yesterday afternoon, "Windows is the de facto
standard". Others in the oil patch say to him, "Haven't you tried Linux
yet ?"  To which he replies, "Until such time as it can offer me the
same support, interface between packages such as word processors,
AutoCad etc., why should I change". He is also competent enough to
realize that Windows is a bugger to program for - and that within
existing COBOL there are features/data-types desperately needed - and
God knows when we will get them with the current style of introducing
enhancements to COBOL.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS: Thane or any other Fujitsu user - my end-user told me the full-blown
price for F/J was $3,500 (Cdn ?). What are full-blown prices in US $ for
both Fujitsu and NetExpress ? (Full-blown - if you include standard tech
support, not various levels they may offer).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A thought

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7o0h99$bbe$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7nj0q6$4bo$1@news.igs.net> <379EF3CB.2CD2D3E6@zip.com.au> <7nnfvv$moc$1@news.igs.net> <7nnqje$sak$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7npdaa$h5m$1@news.hitter.net> <37A0D25C.2CE8BC1C@home.com> <7nqj7o$1dhg$1@news.hitter.net> <37A13777.64835D30@home.com>`

```
More history. Not invented here is a major social problem, not just in
computer
languages. IBM invented the 1401.  Honeywell tried to go into direct
competition with
their 200.  However, the engineers were too mathematically oriented <G> and
did not allow for blank card columns to be treated as spaces.  A quick
engineering change had to be made because of the large "in place" methods of
computing on tab systems.

RCA tried to go head to head with IBM with their 360.  The RCA engineers
knew better ways to do certain things in the hardware, and some companies
with both
vendors hardware in the same installation found they could NOT run the same
software.

There are lots of illustrations of that.  Likewise, if as a member of the
COBOL Committee you had a serious interest in a particular extension, it
became your baby to get specified, gain interest, sell it to the whole
committee, and work it through the system of the committee.  My experience
in this area includes, Sort/Merge, Data Base, and a guy from Esso liked the
IBM Report Writer and so, he sold that. To get Data Base, I had to plot with
Grace to publish our first spec from the Task Group rather than from the
committee.  It took six damn years, and major help from Bell Labs.  In my
view, the only difference now is that there are some who can look back and
see how progress could be a lot faster if the method for doing maintenance
were better.  The idea of OPEN appeals to me because I do see some headway
in the OS area doing that, and it's got to be more difficult in my view.

Warren Simmons

news:37A13777.64835D30@home.com...
```

```
Welllll James,

Grace would listen to the description of some "new" feature/technique... and
when she was satisfied she understood what was being described, she usual
said,
"Oh, we do that all the time. We just never had a name for it."


Myself, the OO like stuff we did on the UI and II resulted from the extreme
shortage
of memory.  Before UI the tab systems forced certain formatting, and users
accepted it.  With the UI, users learned that we could synchronize and
justify, and place DB CR symbols etc.  With a 1000 words of memory for
everything, zero suppression et all
was done as a subroutine you passed an operand to, and retrieved it in the
desired
form.  I/O was pre-coded, and we just copied it until we memorized it.

I'm still enjoying the walk down memory lane.

Warren Simmons
```

## Thread: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 and offline printer — 7 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 and offline printer

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990726152900.17079.00002232@ng-fs1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

If I compile my program with the CALLFH"EXTFH" and then run my program and try
to print to an off-line printer then my program is able to trap the fact that
the printer is offline by the status that is returned from the write verb.  If
i remove the CALLFH"EXTFH" recompile and then run my program trying to print to
an offline printer I get the "Printer not available - Cancel, Retry or Ignore".
 I get this message even with the COBSW=L which i thought would by pass all
critical errors.  I would prefer not having the CALLFH"EXTFH" compile directive
as it impacts the size of my program.  Any suggestions ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

```

Not the answer you were looking for. Assuming you are still working on

> as it impacts the size of my program.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 and offline printer

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)

```
Hi Jimmy and Bill,

Thanks for the replies.  I have tried the "P" switch it prevents the "Cancel,
Retry , Ignore" message, but just locks up the system until i bring the printer
back on-line.  Regarding Jimmy's suggestion currently the POS DOS package is in
12 seperate modules that get chained back and forth.  I seem to run into a
memory issue when moving from the largest module (sales program) to the
reporting modules (build and report).  This problem goes away if i am able to
compile with out the "CALLFH"EXTFH" directive.  By not using this directive the
program is about 25K smaller.  I do not want to spend a great deal of time on
this project because the product life cycle has come to an end with the release
of our NetExpress/Flexus Sp2 product.  

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

```
You might try CALL "PC_TEST_PRINTER" USING ...

For MF WB 3.2, It is described on page 13-96 of the COBOL
System Reference, Volume 1. I have never used it.

The 'Selected' bit should tell if the printer is on-line.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-162cb9782c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL, IEEE, EBCDIC and ASCII — 4 msgs

### COBOL, IEEE, EBCDIC and ASCII

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic
- **Message-ID:** `<iL%m3.118$QE1.11353@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
July  24, 1999

As I have been "advertising," I am working on creating a "Convert COBOL
datatypes to IEEE datatypes" function I could put into a standard 32-bit
Windows DLL to be called from any language which supports them. (Freeware
for non-commercial use).

Yes, I realize doing the documentation before doing the code is a strange
concept, but it works for me.

After initial comments and some really sophisticated design ideas which I
pitched because I felt them too difficult to deploy, I think this will be
best. (PS thanks to Cheesle for the starting point).

The proposed DLL will provide two distinct functions:

1. The PARSE function.  This will take a series of literals and return
information needed in the conversion phase. Programmers already familiar
with COBOL datatypes, or if there are special alignment or SYNC
considerations, may elect to skip this phase and just table up the data for
the calls to the conversion phase.

The user supplies literals as shown in the ParseParmsType structure below,
and is returned a Status code, ErrorMessage if applicable, and a
DataInfoType structure. The information in the DataInfoType may be used by
the programmer to determine the location (offset) and size of the data
buffer to retrieve from the input and pass to the conversion function.

2. The Convert Function.

This will actually convert the data to a numeric value, or simply effect a
character set conversion from EBCDIC to ASCII. The user supplies a
DataInfoType structure, an input buffer and an EBCDIC flag.
The function returns decimal mantissa and exponent for numeric data types,
or the output text buffer if text (COBOL picture X(nn) or A(nn)).

The function will perform EBCDIC to ASCII conversions if the flag is set and
the datatype qualifies for conversion (e.g., will not convert binary or BCD
numerics).

"Plain old" EBCDIC-ASCII conversions can be effected by passing data as
character type, desired length and setting the EBCDIC flag.

The user is responsible for casting the numeric values returned into the
desired output and must handle the multiply or divide by ten "exponent"
times.

One last thought I had (see comments below re usage from a COBOL program):
might it be better to pass and return numeric parameters in character
(DISPLAY) format? BASIC users could get numeric values using VAL, C/C++
users could get values using scanf, COBOL, of course, could just use USAGE
DISPLAY to read the values. (Just a thought)

Comments?

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net

NOTES:
- ASCIIZ is a null-terminated string
- STRING * whatever is a fixed-size buffer. (Well, so is ASCIIZ, but we
can't very well use ASCIIZ if there is binary data, now, can we?)
- The BASIC "TYPE" is a C/C++ "struct" or COBOL "group item."
- Input and output buffers are only valid up to BufferLength characters.
- If calling from a COBOL program, the user is responsible for supplying
any required numeric parameters in IEEE format. (Although why you would call
this from a COBOL program eludes me at the moment).
- I am not planning on supporting "Edited Numeric" datatypes.

TYPE ParseParmsType
  Picture      ASCII Z   * 32     '"S9(04)V9(2)"  "X(15)", 999999V9999"
  Usage        AS ASCIIZ * 256   ' space/null (display), "DISPLAY"
                                 ' "COMP" "COMP-3","COMP-5" etc.
  SignIs       AS ASCIIZ * 256  '"LEADING" "SEPARATE" or both;
' space/null to use default
END TYPE

TYPE DataInfoType
   DataSize     LONG    ' may be used to figure out how much of the
                        ' input data to retrieve and pass to conversion
   DataType     LONG
   Digits       LONG
   Decimals     LONG
   IsSigned     LONG   'yes/no 0 = no, else yes
   SignLeading  LONG   'yes/no
   SignSeparate LONG   'Yes/No
END TYPE

TYPE ConvertParmsType
  EBCDIC       AS   LONG     ' yes/no
  BufferLength AS   LONG
  BufferIn     AS   STRING * 1024
  BufferOut    AS   STRING * 1024
  Mantissa     AS   LONG
  Exponent     AS   LONG
END TYPE


TYPE ParmsType
  Action       AS LONG            ' 1 = parse, 2 = convert
  DataInfo     AS DataInfoType    ' supply for convert; returned on
  Convert      AS ConvertParmsType
  Status       AS LONG             ' 0 = OK, not 0 = error
  ErrorMessage AS ASCIIZ * 256     ' when non-zero status
END TYPE


Error Examples:

Invalid input data (e.g., x'0A' found in DISPLAY NUMERIC field)
Overflow/Underflow
One or more characters of EBCDIC input buffer do not have ASCII equivalent
Unsupported Picture
Unsupported Function

** ENDS ***
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL, IEEE, EBCDIC and ASCII

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic
- **Message-ID:** `<7nic4o$2k5$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<iL%m3.118$QE1.11353@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>- STRING * whatever is a fixed-size buffer. (Well, so is ASCIIZ, but we
>can't very well use ASCIIZ if there is binary data, now, can we?)

This is an interesting issue, consider how it would be used. Say, I have an
accounting software done in C, I would like to check the customer database
against a credit check from AcmeCreditCheck Inc and they sent me a mainframe
cobol file.

How would I read the file prior to putting the data into the DLL?

Would I read it binary or as text?

As for ASCIIZ, it will hold binaries as well as text, problem is if there is
an ascii 0 inside.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL, IEEE, EBCDIC and ASCII

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic
- **Message-ID:** `<0I6n3.957$QE1.30504@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<iL%m3.118$QE1.11353@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7nic4o$2k5$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote in message <7nic4o$2k5$1@news.cerf.net>...
>Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>>- STRING * whatever is a fixed-size buffer. (Well, so is ASCIIZ, but we
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>against a credit check from AcmeCreditCheck Inc and they sent me a
mainframe
>cobol file.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The embedded binary 0 was my specific concern when I wrote this. You will
note that all the non-ASCIIZ buffers have a separate, explicit field to say
how much of the data are valid. (Not unlike an "occurs depending on"
clause).

As far as reading the file, you would probably do something like build a
table containing the data picture, usage and sign info with space for the
other fields returned by the parse function (size, sign flags, etc).

Call the "parse" function of the DLL for each field; save the returned data
sizes, types, etc  in your table.

Now to convert:

FOR each record
   READ Input-Buffer
   FOR each field
      Calculate offset in record (using accumulated sizes of previous
fields)
      Move portion of buffer for this field, and the data statistics
gathered during the parse to the   Datainfo portion of the parameter block
     call the dll
     convert numeric output to desired data type, build/write output buffer.

COBOL: Reference Modification is the way you could move the data to this
buffer:

01  THE-RECORD             PIC X(1000).

MOVE  Field-Length  Parms-BufferLen
MOVE  The-Record (startpos:Field-length) to Buffer-In
CALL "the dll" using Parms-Block.

In BASIC, you use the MID$ function (a lot like reference modifier in
COBOL).

In C, I think you use a character array (table) and do a memory copy from
the address of the source element to the address of the destination.

I guess this means I should write a sample conversion program, one in BASIC,
one in COBOL, and find someone to write one in "C".

FWIW, I have added another parameter: alignment. You will be able to specify
byte, word or dword alignment when parsing and I'll use that in the size and
value calculation later. (Also to mask off any slack bytes resulting from
ALIGN or SYNC).

I have the parse part working except for some extra editing I want to do,
and the data conversion is a snap, so I may actually be looking for
volunteer testers next week.

MCM
```

## Thread: blocksize efficiency in os/390 — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### blocksize efficiency in os/390

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Job running out of space... 500 lrecl * 400,000 records.  When I code 
blocksize=0, I have to code space=(0,(45000,4500),rlse) and it almost blows
up.  What's going on?  According to our Roscoe calculator, that dataset
shouldn't take more than about 11,000 blocks with a blksize of 18888.

Why is this?  Should I not code blksize=0?

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: blocksize efficiency in os/390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nj3j2$nng@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com...
> Job running out of space... 500 lrecl * 400,000 records.  When I code
> blocksize=0, I have to code space=(0,(45000,4500),rlse) and it almost
blows
> up.  What's going on?  According to our Roscoe calculator, that dataset
> shouldn't take more than about 11,000 blocks with a blksize of 18888.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

Do you really mean that you have coded
   BLKSIZE=0
in your JCL - or do you mean you have coded
   BLOCK CONTAINS 0
in your COBOL program.  There is a big difference between the two.

Personally, I have never used (or SEEN) BLKSIZE=0 in JCL.   Is this supposed
to mean something special?

If what you have coded is really "BLOCK CONTAINS" in your COBOL program,
then you should NOT be using that "0" as the first parameter in your space=
statement (JCL).  Actually, I can't remember any GOOD reason for ever
allocating space in blocks - rather than tracks or cylinders - but your
application may have one.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: blocksize efficiency in os/390

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379d3cf1.261443577@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com> <7nj3j2$nng@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`

```

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:


>Do you really mean that you have coded
>   BLKSIZE=0
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

He should do both.

>Personally, I have never used (or SEEN) BLKSIZE=0 in JCL.   Is this supposed
>to mean something special?

Yep.  With our little OS/390 box I have found that if you let QSAM do
what it wants it does an efficient job - let it choose the most
efficient blocksize for the device.  I have also found the same to be
true of VSAM - leave off the CI SIZE - and that just reminded me, I
was supposed to rebuild a LARGE file at 8:00 - I'm late!


>
>If what you have coded is really "BLOCK CONTAINS" in your COBOL program,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: blocksize efficiency in os/390

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379D8D30.E44072E@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com> <7nj3j2$nng@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`

```
BLKSIZE=0 (or omitting the BLKSIZE parameter) allows the system to determine the
optimal block size (optimal space usage) depending on the storage medium.. A
3380 will have a blocksize of roughly 23K, a 3390 28K, and cartridge 32K. This
is dependent on the program also having coded BLOCK 0.  When a data set is
opened, the DCB is filled first from the program, next the JCL, and finally the
data set itself. If the COBOL program had not coded BLOCK 0, it will default to
BLOCK 1. Not very efficient. One big advantage is that the JCL/program
blocksizes becomes device independent. Moving from  one device type to another
does not mean recalculating blocksizes. There are certain requirements for a new
dataset  to allow the system to determine the BLKSIZE:
(quoting IBM)
Block size is not available or specified from any source. BLKSIZE=0 can be
specified.
You specify LRECL or it is in the data class.
You specify RECFM or it is in the data class. It must be fixed or variable.
 You specify DSORG as PS or PO or you omit DSORG and it is PS or PO in the data
class.

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
> news:7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: blocksize efficiency in os/390

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nlur1$ksi@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com> <7nj3j2$nng@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <379D8D30.E44072E@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Well, that answers it.  I was only coding recording mode is f.  That 
explains why blksize=0 in the jcl wasn't working.  I'll try it tomorrow.

Still a rookie,
Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: blocksize efficiency in os/390

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379d806f.1459138@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
On Mon, 26 Jul 1999 20:32:55 -0400, "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

>Job running out of space... 500 lrecl * 400,000 records.  When I code 
>blocksize=0, I have to code space=(0,(45000,4500),rlse) and it almost blows
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jeff

A Blksize of 18888?  With a record length of 500?  Weird.....


If your program does not include BLOCK CONTAINS 0, then the blocksize from the DCB in your
program will be used when creating the file.  Code a zero block size in the JCL AND in the
program.

If your shop uses SMS, you can specify the record length and number of records, instead of
the block size, and use the AVGREC as a counter multiplier, like this

//MYFILE DD DISP=(,CATLG,DELETE),DSN=WHATEVER,
//                              SPACE=(500,(400,10),RLSE),AVGREC=K,LRECL=500,RECFM=FB


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: blocksize efficiency in os/390

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379D8A6D.AA73CE58@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7niul5$1sb2@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
You code BLOCK {CONTAINS} 0 (RECORDS) in the program and either omit the
BLKSIZE paramater in JCL or code BLKSIZE=0.  The space parameter depends
on whether SMS is active. If it is, code space in terms of AVGREC with
an LRECL of 500 or megabytes (20 meg).  Otherwise code space either in
cylinders or as (20000,(10000,1000),rlse) (or rlse,,round).  Check your
JCL manual or JCL reference.  

Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> Job running out of space... 500 lrecl * 400,000 records.  When I code
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff
```

## Thread: Ghost breakpoints in ANIMATOR (MF COBOL 3.4) — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Ghost breakpoints in ANIMATOR (MF COBOL 3.4)

- **From:** renfrew@erols.com (Dave Miner)
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ni6kd$qpb$1@autumn.news.rcn.net>`

```
Environment: Novell Netware 4.11 server, with WIN98 client.  Micro-Focus COBOL 3.4.
 
I am trying to run a rather large program in Animator mode, but when I try to "zoom" the 
execution to a specific event it keeps stopping at EARLIER events.  It apparently thinks that 
these earlier events are breakpoints because it says "program breakpoint" at the bottom of the 
animator screen.  But these are NOT breakpoints!?!  To make sure, I even tried to unset a 
breakpoint at these places, but it then confirms that there is no breakpoint to unset.  

I know that the first tree one should bark up when having Animator problems is to look for an 
old/obsoleted AIF file, but I have already done this.  Any suggestions?  

1KTIA, 
DaveM
```

#### ↳ Re: Ghost breakpoints in ANIMATOR (MF COBOL 3.4)

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379d3b3f.261008887@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7ni6kd$qpb$1@autumn.news.rcn.net>`

```
On 26 Jul 1999 17:41:33 GMT, renfrew@erols.com (Dave Miner) wrote:

>Environment: Novell Netware 4.11 server, with WIN98 client.  Micro-Focus COBOL 3.4.
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>old/obsoleted AIF file, but I have already done this.  Any suggestions?  

Windows will "FIND" an old AIF file if it is in your PATH.  Something
interesting about Windows few people know, but many have been bit by.
Try writing a program.  Don't EXPLICITLY specify a path for the file,
just assign it to "INPUT.FIL" - then put that file somewhere in your
path, different from your current directory and run the program.  WALA
- windows finds the file.  Very bad.  Anyway, scour your hard drive
for old AIF files and make sure the ONLY think on your system for this
program ANYWHERE is the source - recompile then try animating.

Still have a problem, when the first break hits perform the "unset all
break points" function, then reset your break and continue.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379C8822.9ADCBD1@ix.netcom.com>`

```
My applications people have been trying to track down a nasty little 1 (that's ONE) penny
calculation problem between an Online CICS/VSE program and a VSE Batch program.

Is there any difference between FCOBOL Batch and Online that could possibly create this ?
```

#### ↳ Re: FCOBOL calc in batch vs CICS

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379CF997.7438480E@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<379C8822.9ADCBD1@ix.netcom.com>`

```
If you have switched compilers and/or run time, it is possable. 
Of course, you are calculationg everything in pennys, right? 
Turns out the MVS COBOL has a better arithmetic runtime, but this
will only apply to mathematical things in floating point (and
possibly some display to comp binary conversions).  Don't forget
the old options TRUNC and NUMPROC...
```

<a id="t-30b7b4ce7d"></a>
## Thread: MF 3.4 Animator bug using Report Writer with nested copy-procs — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### MF 3.4 Animator bug using Report Writer with nested copy-procs

- **From:** renfrew@erols.com (Dave Miner)
- **Date:** 1999-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nib3v$t0m$1@autumn.news.rcn.net>`

```
(Environment: Novell Netware 4.11 server, with WIN98 client, using Micro-Focus level 3.4)
 
(Note:  This is an FYI, and is unrelated to my previous inquiry about ghost breakpoints.) 

While trying to animate a rather large program, the Animator kept totally freezing up.  I was 
forced to kill the dos session after each attempt, and it was driving me absolutely bonkers.  But 
after many hours of progressively whittling down the code, I was finally able to isolate the 
problem.  THE BUG IS AS FOLLOWS:  If a program uses the same copy-proc in more than one RD, and 
if that copy-proc itself uses one or more subordinate copy-procs, then Animator goes into a 
coma.

Once I understood it, I was then able to work around it.  Victory is mine!...Bwa Ha Ha...!

Don't know if this bug also exists in earlier or newer versions.  Don't know if I can justify the 
time to find out.  But if I ever do I will be glad to post the results.  

DaveM
```
