<a id="t-c4a6131efd"></a>
## Thread: Migrating to COBOL for OS390 — 12 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** Peter Marshall <marshall.peter@canadatrust.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com>`

```
Hello all!

We are in the process of evaluating a move from our current COBOL, VS
COBOL II (version 1.3.0), to the new COBOL for OS390 (version 2.1).  Our
shop has some 16,000 programs and are currently running under LE version
1.5 (we are also looking to change that to version 1.8).

We are curious to know if anyone else has done this type of migration
and if so, have run across any problems/pitfalls.

IBM recommended that we regression test ALL programs, however, we simply
cannot afford to do so both from a time perspective, as well as money.
If the business plan gets too expensive we will have to remain at our
current unsupported version.

Any advice/tips are greatly appreciated.

Thanks,
Peter Marshall
Senior Systems Analyst
Canada Trust
```

#### ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nkllg$dii@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com>`

```
The first thing I would say is why Oh WHY - would you want to recompile all
your existing programs?  There is no reason that you can't have VS COBOL II
and COBOL for OS/390 & VM object code "happily" co-existing for as long as
you want.  Therefore, my first suggestion would be to NOT do a "mass
recompilation" - but instead to gradually recompile your programs with the
newer compiler - as your resources allow (including appropriate testing).
Often it does make (business-internal) sense to recompile an entire
application at one time - but almost never to recompile all the programs in
your shop.

The next thing I want to say is that you are on a VERY old version of VS
COBOL II.  I can't remember when release 3.0 went out of service, but it was
IBM's very 1st attempt at providing an ANSI'85 compiler - and there have
been many, MANY bugs "fixed" since that product.  (VS COBOL II, R4 is the
currently supported product - and it is scheduled to go out of service March
of 2001.)

If you were using VS COBOL II, R4, then I would say that you could
"reasonably" expect most (almost all) of your existing programs to recompile
fine and run as expected with the newer compiler.  But from where you are
coming, I would definitely expect some "problems".

The next question for you is whether you are using RES, NORES, or MIXRES in
your current VS COBOL II environment.  If you are already RES, then this
will significantly reduce your migration effort getting to COBOL for
this-and-that.  If you are NORES *and* have been using the LE library
(SCEELKED) in your link-edit step, then this too should reduce (but not as
much) your migration effort.  If your are currently using MIXRES - or you
have been using the VS COBOL II run-time library (rather than the LE one)
either at run-time or link-edit time, then you are in for some SIGNIFICANT
migration issues (often sever performance impacts if you don't do everything
exactly right).

Again, just so you know, IBM does *fully* support VS COBOL II (even OS/VS
COBOL) object code that uses the LE run-time library - even after March
2001.  So I just don't understand the "goal" of your planned migration.
Therefore, my recommendations are:

1) Do NOT migrate (recompile) all your programs at once.

2) Do make certain that all your VS COBOL II programs are compiled (and
tested) as RES and use the LE library (and 2nd choice are NORES and use the
LE library for link-edit).

3) Do plan for a "phased-in" migration to COBOL for OS/390 & VM - by
recompiling programs (or applications) when you actually NEED to change them
(and when you have the resources to test them).  Pay particular attention to
"performance" issues and compile and run-time options.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379DE632.5A15BEA4@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <7nkllg$dii@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> The first thing I would say is why Oh WHY - would you want to recompile all
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> your shop.

You should have a strategy for making sure that called programs and
calling programs work together though.  Cobol II can be compiled above
or below the line.  I have seen COBOL II programs stop short when
calling a COBOL for MVS program - plan your gradual conversion with this
in mind.   Eventually, converting the remaining programs can simplify
maintenance and control though.

Changing compilers, operating systems versions, and even compiler
options can break programs - sort of like changing Java libraries.  And
running in test environments may not catch all of the socket and other
interface needs which are pointing to production, unless you are
carefully looking for these.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nlals$f5c@dfw-ixnews21.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <7nkllg$dii@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <379DE632.5A15BEA4@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
news:379DE632.5A15BEA4@NOSPAMhome.com...
  <snip>
>
> You should have a strategy for making sure that called programs and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Actually, this is true for VS COBOL II calls to VS COBOL II; COBOL for
this-and-that CALL to other COBOL for this-and-that programs; OR any
combination of the 2.

You to need to understand both the AMODE and RMODE link-edit attributes of
both the programs you are using - as well as the DATA compiler setting - and
for the COBOL for this-and-that the RMODE compiler setting (which can be
different from the RMODE link-edit attribute).  When you understand all of
these AND know whether your calls are dynamic or static, you can "easily"
predict whether they will work or not.  If you don't know all of this- you
are in for problems - even without a change in compilers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** "Tim Hillock" <hillock@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o1rn3.188$m%3.457565@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com>`

```
Peter, I agree with William.  There's no need to recompile all your programs
at once.  As long as you still have your old runtime for the programs, you
should be find.  As to the other things that William said those I don't know
too much.  3 yrs ago , I worked at one place where we just started to move
to Cobol II.  I wasn't involved with that.  At my current job the complier
is at OS390, and I wasn't around during that conversion.  I don't know what
little changes are required.

If this is in relation to Y2K, you could be finding a few more problems than
need be.  What you could do is convert a few of your major/main programs and
test those against your production programs.  If everything looks ok, then
do another bunch of programs.  You could test those and then do some more or
if you feel confident with the first batch of compling and testing, then
just do your conversions.  From experience sometimes that works and
sometimes it doesn't.  It's your call.  Best bet, convert those that must be
done and do the others when normal maintenace is required.

The unsupport complier should still run after 2001.

Tim.

Peter Marshall <marshall.peter@canadatrust.com> wrote in message
news:379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com...
> Hello all!
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** Peter Marshall <marshall.peter@canadatrust.com>

I thank you all for your answers and concerns.

We do not want to recompile all of our programs and are looking at a 'phased-in'
approach as each of you suggest.  I probably did not state it well initially,
but it was IBM who suggested we recompile the shop.  Not exactly what we wanted
to hear.

We have an interesting environment where we currently use LE in our online CICS
world, but a mix of LE and COBOL II run times in batch.  We are RES as well.

Over the past few months we have taken a few of our more monster programs and
have recompiled and experimented with them.  This includes chains of programs
where Assembler calls COBOL, COBOL calling Assembler, Fortran, etc.

The feeback I have gotten from this newsgroup has been great!!  Keep it coming!!

Thanks,
Peter Marshall
Senior Systems Analyst
Canada Trust

Tim Hillock wrote:

> Peter, I agree with William.  There's no need to recompile all your programs
> at once.  As long as you still have your old runtime for the programs, you
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nnqqi$54k@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <o1rn3.188$m%3.457565@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net> <379EF791.178B8C7@canadatrust.com>`

```
If you have RACF (or ACF or any other comparable product), I would say that
your next step is to put the VS COBOL II run-time libraries into "warn"
mode, so you can get an ACCURATE report on exactly which
programs/applications are still using it (in batch).  Those are the
applications that I would be targeting first.  Get them into using the LE
run-time library (killing off the VS COBOL II library as QUICKLY as you can)
and you should be in pretty good shape. (If nothing else, this will have you
"supported by IBM" even after March 2001.)

If you (or someone in your shop) has access to IBMLink, I would get them to
search ALL (and it is large number) of APARs that closed DOC or PER
(documentation change or code change) after VS COBOL II, R3.0.  This list
will give you an idea (database) of what type of "changes" in either
behavior or "supported syntax" you can expect between VS COBOL II R3.0 and
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM.  When I wrote my book ("OldBOL to NewBOL: An IBM
COBOL Migration Tutorial") there were many, MANY, pages of such APARs that I
included in an Appendix - that was several years ago, so things can only
have gotten worse since then.

It is worth noting that the IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM Migration Guide (from
IBM) *does* include a little information on migrating from VS COBOL II (not
just from OS/VS COBOL) - so I would sure make certain that you have looked
at/read that too.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379F9C71.2F3@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <o1rn3.188$m%3.457565@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net> <379EF791.178B8C7@canadatrust.com> <7nnqqi$54k@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
<massive snip!>

For our phased in approach, we added a home-grown source code converter
into the checkout phase of our change control system.  After running
this way for almost 2 years, we found that most of our stuff was
converted, and we kicked off a small project to complete the job.  But
then, we only have 2,000 programs. :-)  Developers did not enjoy those
two years, but hey, it HAS to be done.

Another reason for wanting to upgrade, is that OS/VS and COBOL-II
applications can not co-exist under a TSO session in split-screen
without causing memory management problems.  I ran into this last week
with two vendor products that have not been upgraded to OS390 yet.  :-(

Some of the conversion issues we had were;

* Working storage no longer initialized itself or initializes
differently.  You have to code VALUE statements where you didn't need to
before.

* The new SSRANGE compiler option is wonderful and should always be
turned on, but some old code can't run with it.  In some cases we
re-engineered the application, but usually we turned off this compile
option for these programs.

* The JCL PARM card under LE now assumes the last slash(/) is a
delimiter between your program parm and LE parms.  So PARM=(01/01/99)
would only pass "01/01" into the application.

* As mentioned before by others, we had to upgrade subroutines to 31/ANY
before the application could be upgraded to 31/ANY.

* "NEXT SENTENCE" behaves differently.  We convered them all to
"CONTINUE" to maintain consistent behavior.

This list is just off the top of my head....  I've probably missed some.

Pete Wirfs
Salem Oregon
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7noa6s$3jb@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <o1rn3.188$m%3.457565@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net> <379EF791.178B8C7@canadatrust.com> <7nnqqi$54k@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <379F9C71.2F3@saif.com>`

```
See comments below (about differences and non-differences in a COBOL
migration).

Note:
  Much of the note to which I am replying was talking about a migration from
OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II.  The original note in this thread was talking
about VS COBOL II to COBOL for OS/390 & VM.  These 2 migrations have VERY
different concerns and issues (although the "methodology," planning, and
testing are often quite similar).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Bill Turner, WB4ALM" <wb4alm@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A08820.DC7339C1@gte.net>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <o1rn3.188$m%3.457565@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net> <379EF791.178B8C7@canadatrust.com> <7nnqqi$54k@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <379F9C71.2F3@saif.com>`

```
I agree with most of the hints that have been listed above - been there, saw
what happened, and had to argue for days on why it happened. (Programmers
shooting themselves in THEIR feet, while trying to get mine.)

We are "mostly" converted away from VS COBOL II, but still find some people
having problems.

Many are caused by OLD linkedit JCL that people hardcoded AMODE=24 or
RMODE=24 into the Linkedit parm statement.

If you don't know that you need to "force" a particular AMODE/RMODE, than do
nothing - and let the binder do its job.
I still have to tell people to remove "defaulted" parameters from compiler
and link edit PARM= statements, unless they
really know that the MUST specify that particular value.

/s/ Bill Turner, wb4alm



Pete Wirfs wrote:

> <massive snip!>
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> Salem Oregon
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Migrating to COBOL for OS390

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A12191.93E13904@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<379D9BF5.B56A6C2D@canadatrust.com> <o1rn3.188$m%3.457565@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net> <379EF791.178B8C7@canadatrust.com> <7nnqqi$54k@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <379F9C71.2F3@saif.com> <37A08820.DC7339C1@gte.net>`

```
I strongly agree --- Let the Binder do Its Job!

But look at the cross reference for a couple of things...

some old csects have no AMODE/RMODE specification with them.  This
makes them 24/24 which might not be true.  Check them out as they
may be the only thing which is keeping your load module 24/24.

For example, I found one shop carring around a DFHxxx module which
is just glue to reach the CICS routines.  Can't remeber the exact
name, but its only a handfull of bytes and in always needed for
Command Level CICS.  Anyway, this guy was from CICS V3 (or was it
V2?) and had no amode/rmode.  The module form CICS V4 had exactly
the same instructions, plus any/any as it could run anywhere in
any mode.  After I got it out of the library, a lot of COBOL
online programs suddenly moved above the line.

Also look for some old ILB0xxx stuff and see if they are residules
from old COBOL.  There are replacements in newer runtimes for
them.  Better yet, change the source to call the IGZxxxx modules. 
The new ILBxxx replacements are just glue to the IGZ stuff.

Bill Turner, WB4ALM wrote:
> 
> I agree with most of the hints that have been listed above - been there, saw
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> /s/ Bill Turner, wb4alm
```

understanding of where to start.  Sorry I don't have knowledge of any

<a id="t-3b3ca1d93e"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Data File Reader Technology and Other CobolTransformer News — 3 this month / 5 total

### Cobol Data File Reader Technology and Other CobolTransformer News

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379E6AF9.F6C66800@siber.com>`

```
Siber Systems is pleased to announce release 3-5-2 of 
CobolTransformer http://www.siber.com/sct/ suite of products.

The news are:

* Cobol Data File Reader technology is introduced.
It allows you to read and interpret Cobol data files 
(sometimes also called ISAM files). 

A number of specific converters from Cobol data files to 
non-Cobol data formats was built using this technology:

- data2flat: Cobol Data File to Flat Data File converter.
It converts Fujitsu Cobol and Micro Focus Cobol data files
to flat text file format. Flat files can be easily loaded 
into Microsoft Excel and most modern databases.
Download Evaluation Copy now http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/.

- data2dbf: Cobol Data File to dBase-IV Database converter.
Converts FSC/MF data files to dBase-IV files.
To be distributed soon by Siber Systems.

- fsc2cr: Data access DLL that is used by Seagate Software 
Crystal Reports to read Fujitsu Cobol data files.
To be distributed soon by Fujitsu Software Corp.


The Cobol Data File Reader technology consists 
of the following infrastructure items:

- cbl2fdd: Cobol File Descriptor Extractor.
This programs parses Cobol program that can read or write
a specific Cobol data file. It extracts File Descriptor
information necessary to read this Cobol data file 
from SELECT and FD statements for the file and 
puts it into machine-readable FDD and RDD files.
Download Evaluation Copy now http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/.

- DataAccess: DataAccess library takes the File Descriptor
data stored in FDD and RDD files and it uses this data
to read the actual Cobol data file. RDD and FDD files can be 
automatically generated by Cbl2Fdd or created manually.

You can license Cobol Data File Reader technology to add 
Cobol data file reading capability to your application.
Please inquire about pricing and licensing at info@siber.com.

More on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/.


* Approximately 150 new functions and classes were added 
to CobolTransformer Toolkit API. These convenience functions
make writing Cobol converters and analyzers a breeze.
These additional functions were developed while building 
several large commercial Cobol converters and preprocessors.

Download CobolTransformer Toolkit Evaluation Copy
from http://www.siber.com/sct/lib/ and request 
a new version of the CobolTransformer Toolkit
manual from info@siber.com, if you are interested.


* A number of small bugs and memory leaks was fixed, so
expect the latest versions of converters to run faster
and take less memory.

Please proceed to Cobol Tools http://www.siber.com/sct/tools/
or Cobol Converters http://www.siber.com/sct/convs/ pages
to get new versions of the tools.


Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

```
Hi Cobol-Specialists,

I have an ugly problem, for which I already spent hours of time, and I
really hope, anyone can help.

My Cobol program calls functions, that reside in a 32Bit-DLL. Some code
segments might tell you, how:
--- snip ----------
      $set ans85 noosvs mf defaultbyte"00" case
        .....
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           CALL-CONVENTION 74 IS WINAPI
        .....
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        .....
           SET DLL-POINTER1 TO ENTRY 'CIBMRG32.DLL'
           CALL WINAPI  'cibmerge'  USING
                          BY VALUE ANZ-PARAM
                          BY REFERENCE PARAM-POINTER(1)
               RETURNING RC
           END-CALL.
        .....
--- snip ----------

Within my development environment (MF Cobol Workbench 4.0.32), everything
works phantastic, but I don't succeed LINKING.
When I invoke
   CBLLINK -G MIMERGE
I get error-messages like
   mimerge.obj : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol "_PrintRtf@4"
where the corresponding CBL-Code is
   call winapi "PrintRtf" using ...

Does anyone have an idea?

Thank you in advance
and greetings from germany
Frank Scholz
```

<a id="t-db2e8af1e9"></a>
## Thread: Need help with COBOL MVS functions — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Need help with COBOL MVS functions

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<933077114.24511@www.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<7n5d40$6f1$1@news.tdh.state.tx.us>`

```
No, the LENGTH function as well as the LENGTH OF special
register returns the current amount of bytes that are used
in main storage. This function does not know which
characters you want to exclude. Try the INSPECT verb
instead (INSPECT xxx TALLYING yyy FOR LEADING SPACES)!

Instead of SUBSTRING, you can use the reference modification
in COBOL. Its sysntax is xxxx(m:n) where m and n are numeric
literals or numeric variables. You can use this statement on
both sides of a MOVE, IF or EVALUATE. m means the starting
position, n the lenght. in your sample, WS-NAME(2:2) would
be 'OT'.

And to answer your third question, no, you cannot define
your own functions. AFAIK, this will be allowed in the next
release of the standard, but I don't know whether IBM has
planned such functions for the next releases fo the COBOL
compiler. The most current is 'IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
Version 2 Release 1 Modification 1' and this one doesn't
allow you to write your own functions.

You might want to read the Language Reference. If you don't
have it in printed form, you could consult the following
URL:
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGY
LR204/CCONTENTS
(all on one line!)
Or you could order the manual in printed form. It's the
COBOL Language Reference, Document Number SC26-9046-03.

Cheers

Daniel



* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help with COBOL MVS functions

- **From:** "Vic Hariton" <vic.hariton@cpa.state.tx.us>
- **Date:** 1999-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nkhbj$3kp$1@news.tdh.state.tx.us>`
- **References:** `<7n5d40$6f1$1@news.tdh.state.tx.us> <933077114.24511@www.remarq.com>`

```

being provided in COBOL and wanted to try them out.  Got to say I was a
little disappointed not to see the same functionality as other languages
have.  Maybe it's a performance thing.

I will try using the "reference modification" approach for substring.

Thanks for your help,
 Vic
```

### Variable length output on OS390

- **From:** "icare" <icare@wanadoo.be>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nnikg$4mf@news3.euro.net>`

```
used :
Record varying
block contains 0 characters

But at open output time i get a message stating the the file attributes
mismatch with the allocated file.
I used no DCB on the DD card.
It seems that the file is being by default set to FB at catalogue time ???

Dioxine.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length output on OS390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nns97$qou@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7nnikg$4mf@news3.euro.net>`

```
It sounds like you have done everything correctly - but ...

Can you show us the COMPLETE FD and the complete JCL for this file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length output on OS390

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379FCF8E.E3D335B1@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7nnikg$4mf@news3.euro.net>`

```
It looks like you are accessing a pre-existing file. When a file is OPEN'd,
the DCB for the file is filled first from the program, then the JCL (if any
DCB parameters) and lastly the catalog. In your case,  your program says it
has  a VB format and loads that info into the DCB. The  catalog knows it to be
FB, but the program takes overrides the catalog, and you get your mismatch.
Either change  your program to a FB format, or delete/redefine the file with
the appropriate attributes.  The system does not default an RECFM.

Regards
John

icare wrote:

> used :
> Record varying
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dioxine.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length output on OS390

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379FD904.45856513@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<7nnikg$4mf@news3.euro.net>`

```
If the output file already exists and has ever been opened for
output, it's attributes must match the COBOL program.  Use PDF 3.2
to see what the file attributes are.  If the file has DCB
parameters somewhere in the job preceeding your program, the file
will pick them up and carry them to your program.


icare wrote:
> 
> used :
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dioxine.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>

- **Message-ID:** `<7nsds4$dqd$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Tim Hillock wrote in message ...
>But I will admit before the world, that I Tim, did and still own Reel to
>Reel tapes, 8-tracks and their respective record/play devices.  Also
>including many cassettes which include music and ......gulp.......basic
>programs(recorded live by a cassette recorder).

Cool. I once had one of these home computers, a Dragon, which had the
enormous amount of 32Kb memory. My friends thought I were insane, finding
joy in programming a computer. But, I thought it was cool, and still is,
when I make a computer do what I want. Unfortunately, it was often problems
with the tape in the cassette recorder. Sort of charming though. Imagine,
todays youth, they grow up without listening to these cassette recorders,
barely learning audio conversion in the last of the modems.

Soon we're digitalized all together. Then when we sit down there in the
retirement community, in the dawn we say;

Hey pal, I got some good recordings of Basic from a Commodore Vic20, wanna
hear?

Cheesle
```

## Thread: a little motivation, anyone? — 3 msgs

### Re: a little motivation, anyone?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379F2FB0.7B57DA79@home.com>`
- **References:** `<19990728052257.10608.00002253@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Medley8086 wrote:
> 
> Ok, I'm running a win98 partition and a linux partition.  I have all sorts of
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> E. Leron Culbreath

As a generalization, I would suggest most of the people here learned 
COBOL at 'school' and use it for their jobs. Having reached the magic
'45' and the Catch-22 situation 'You don't know the oil/gas industry, so
we aren't interested', I taught myself COBOL out of necessity. But I had
to have a goal in mind, so I developed a Point-of-Sale system and then
it moved on from there.

Understand your point about motivation. Why learn German (a) unless you
are going to use it in business or (b) you are planning on taking a
holiday in Germany. I get the impression you are a 'hobbyist' so far as
computer languages are concerned. Given your access to Linux, and
there's lots of software, can you identify a small application, (like
say, a telephone and name/address book that you could improve on).
Alternatively is there something in Linux that you think you could do a
better job on. Don't pick yourself too large a task. Are you doing well
- the only answer to this is to compare to the program source of others
using your language of preference.

Having answered you - may I ask ? I've got Red Hat Linux - but I'm leery
about screwing around partioning my hard drive. Did you encounter any
major problems. Your unbiased reaction to Linux v Windows as regards
being 'user friendly'.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: a little motivation, anyone?

- **From:** medley8086@aol.com (Medley8086)
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990728144410.25870.00003005@ng-bg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<379F2FB0.7B57DA79@home.com>`

```
James, thanks a lot for your anwer.  I use red hat also.  I didn't run into any
major problems.  I'd say Windows is more "user friendly", but I dont really
care for a level of user friendly that hides memory leaks and keeps me from
challenging myself and learning more.  I just installed redhat 5.2 on my
father's computer two days ago, after shifting some files around and stealing
his second hard drive from windows.  I did have to reinstall linux and windows
a few times the first time i installed linux, but I guess it depends on how
crucial the programs you have in windows are and if you could get windows back
up in time to not hurt your productivity.  I can get windows back up to its
current configuration(which i usually have very far from the default) within an
hour and a half(including the time to reinstall) and that came from screwing up
so many times.  I believe that it's the recovery process that you learn from. 
Sorry for rambling.

Leron
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: a little motivation, anyone?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Leron

I have RH 6.0 as well.  I have installed Turbo Linux (Very easy), SUSE
linux (pretty darn good but a bugger to set up), Caldera (the most
intelligent set up), Debian (The hacker's choice but really esoteric).

Linux is maturing it's installation stuff really quickly (comparing RH
4 with 5 with 6).  It really is a pain to set up still, but generally
(unless you change old crappy hardware as much as I do) you only
should do it once.

Ken
Where do want to go tomorrow.

<a id="t-7ee333e2ce"></a>
## Thread: interesting bug — 5 this month / 13 total

### interesting bug

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nngcl$n9a$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Found the following under Fujitsu 4.0 today.


    the statement

        PERFORM OPEN-THE-FILE UNTIL
                FILE-STATUS-WORD IS EQUAL TO "00".

        OPEN-THE-FILE.
                OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE.
                IF FILE-STATUS-WORD (is correctable)
                        (fix the error)
                ELSE
                        (sit in loop showing error).

Craps out the *second* time it runs with a "divide by zero!" error. Not the
open statement itself, but the perform. I was able to fix it by changing the
UNTIL to a flag check, and setting the flag in the IF statement of the
OPEN-THE-FILE paragraph, but a divide by zero error on a PERFORM/UNTIL
through me off for about an hour.
```

#### ↳ Re: interesting bug

- **From:** walterm@rose.hp.com (Walter Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nqgv3$kam$1@ocean.cup.hp.com>`
- **References:** `<7nngcl$n9a$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees (donald@willmack.com) wrote:
: Found the following under Fujitsu 4.0 today.
:     the statement
:         PERFORM OPEN-THE-FILE UNTIL
:                 FILE-STATUS-WORD IS EQUAL TO "00".
:         OPEN-THE-FILE.
:                 OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE.
:                 IF FILE-STATUS-WORD (is correctable)
:                         (fix the error)
:                 ELSE
:                         (sit in loop showing error).

: Craps out the *second* time it runs with a "divide by zero!" error. 
: [snip]

This looks mildly suspicious.  Your PERFORM statement doesn't include
a TEST AFTER phrase, so will not execute OPEN-THE-FILE if 
FILE-STATUS-WORD happens to be 00 initially.  Are you setting
FILE-STATUS-WORD to a non-zero value somewhere in order to force
OPEN-THE-FILE to be executed?

Disclaimer:  I know nothing about Fujitsu COBOL.

Walter Murray
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: interesting bug

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ns470$lmt$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7nngcl$n9a$1@news.igs.net> <7nqgv3$kam$1@ocean.cup.hp.com>`

```
Yes.

#### ↳ Re: interesting bug

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A075B5.B5E421E6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7nngcl$n9a$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> Found the following under Fujitsu 4.0 today.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> through me off for about an hour.

Donald,

I think your example illustrates very well the benefit of using 88s to
determine end-of-routine and making file-status a separate check :-

	set FileNotFinished to true
	perform Open-the-file until FileFinished

	Open-the-File.
	   open output Report-File
	   if file-status <> "00"
              set FileFinished to true
	   else ......
	   End-if

	 ... and as a result of the file-status error do something else
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: interesting bug

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nq8hl$f3e$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7nngcl$n9a$1@news.igs.net> <37A075B5.B5E421E6@home.com>`

```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B01442E51@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
Yea, but then it would just have the last length in it.....

-----Original Message-----
From: francois millet [mailto:francois.millet@dial.oleane.com]
Sent: Saturday, July 24, 1999 10:26 PM
To: comp.lang.cobol@list.deja.com
Subject: Re: Tables and Moves


 Message from the Deja.com forum: 
 comp.lang.cobol
 Your subscription is set to individual email delivery

In article <932842906.937363@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt>, Josua S. wrote:
> 
> TBSRC and the table TABLE-SRC are 'filled' with a call to another program.

Here is the problem: the length of the table TABLE-SRC is re-calculated only

when your program (not another program) changes explicitly TBSRC.

I am quite sure that if you insert ADD 0 to TBSRC after your call, all will
be 
fine...






 _____________________________________________________________
 Deja.com: Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
 http://www.deja.com/
 * To modify or remove your subscription, go to
 http://www.deja.com/edit_sub.xp?group=comp.lang.cobol
 * Read this thread at
 http://www.deja.com/thread/%3CVA.00000066.00477629%40francoim%3E
```

#### ↳ Re: Tables and Moves

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A19A9B.6C512186@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<90491CB93918D21189BD00805FBE300B01442E51@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
"Thompson, William" wrote:
> 
> Yea, but then it would just have the last length in it.....
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> will be fine...

1. I am fairly certain that the optimizing compiler will remove an
explicit 'add 0 '.

2.  If the call explicitly passes a value into the call then the
returned value should be checked 'by the compiler' upon return.  If
this caused an error I would consider it a bug.

3.  What I would not be sure of is if you set a longer value inside
the module than was set externally does the size of the array
automatically grow.  I would consider this undefined (even if it is
defined in the standard I would consider it risky).

The answer has been delivered on this one BTW.
Ken
```

---

<a id="t-a713de59a6"></a>
## Thread: Two AcuCOBOL Questions — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Two AcuCOBOL Questions

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <zimboon @ hotmail . com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379f7e17$0$19185@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
I have two questions for the gurus.
My compiler is Acu-COBOL, my environments are both WinNT and SCO UNIX.

Question 1.
A fellow programmer and I were wondering about the vutil utility.
Is there a way to 'undelete' vision records that were deleted.
We wonder this, since a 'vutil -i' will reflect the number of deleted
records in a file.  Does the delete action cause the record to be
flagged out until a 'vutil -r' comes along and rebuilds the file
without the 'flagged' records?
If this is accomplishable, what is the command syntax.
We have never needed this, but it could be handy nevertheless.

Question 2.
I wrote a little program that is threaded into a main application.
This little program displays a status bar that starts at 0% and ends at
100%.
It is exactly like any typical windows application status window.
To do this I had to 'vutil32 -i ' the data file from the windows client
across the mapped drive onto the UNIX server. (my data is on a UNIX box)
Then with a reference modifier I read the number of records
that 'vutil32 -i' reported.  I used this information to determine my
percentage of the file processed as it was read.
The issue here is that, when the 'vutil32' command is run from
within the little program by "CALL SYSTEM USING "C:\SUPPORT\VUTIL.BAT".
I see a DOS window displayed for a second or two.
I do not want this window to appear.
I have tried adding "NO I-O" to the CALL SYSTEM.
There is an '@echo off' in the DOS batch file.
Still I see the freakin' window.

It is impossible for me to execute the threaded application
with a 'wrun32 -b' since a different runtime does not support
GLOBAL variables in use here.

Any suggestions?
If you need code, I reckon I can get some across to you.

Thanks,

Robert Annandale
Vancouver, BC
```

#### ↳ Re: Two AcuCOBOL Questions

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7npu4f$ikl$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<379f7e17$0$19185@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Robert Annandale > wrote in message
<379f7e17$0$19185@fountain.mindlink.net>...
>Is there a way to 'undelete' vision records that were deleted.

Sounds useful indeed, I don't know of any way to do that. I do however
presume it would be a problem considering that the deleted record, may have
been replaced and if it was an indexed file, a recovery may cause duplicated
key condition?

>records in a file.  Does the delete action cause the record to be
>flagged out until a 'vutil -r' comes along and rebuilds the file
>without the 'flagged' records?

Yes, that is quite common for databases/filesystems. If one were to
physically delete a record immediately, you would turn out to be drinking a
lot of coffee while waiting for the update sequence :-)

>If this is accomplishable, what is the command syntax.
>We have never needed this, but it could be handy nevertheless.

Agree, make sure it gets posted in the NG if you get an answer.

>Question 2.
>I do not want this window to appear.
>I have tried adding "NO I-O" to the CALL SYSTEM.
>There is an '@echo off' in the DOS batch file.
>Still I see the freakin' window.

I have another approach for this one, in the header of the vision file, are
both the total number of records and the number of deleted records stored,
open the file binary and read in the first block and calculate it yourself.
I cannot show you how I did this because it is depending on restricted
information from Acucorp. You should ask them if you could have access to
their documentation of the vision system and then you could write your own.
Dealing with it all from Cobol.

>It is impossible for me to execute the threaded application
>with a 'wrun32 -b' since a different runtime does not support
>GLOBAL variables in use here.

I am not sure what you are trying to do here, what is the purpose?

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Two AcuCOBOL Questions

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <zimboon @ hotmail . com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a08b70$0$4485@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<379f7e17$0$19185@fountain.mindlink.net> <7npu4f$ikl$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
> >It is impossible for me to execute the threaded application
> >with a 'wrun32 -b' since a different runtime does not support
> >GLOBAL variables in use here.
>
> I am not sure what you are trying to do here, what is the purpose?

I have to use a "CALL "SYSTEM" in my program.
It calls a .bat file that runs a vutil on my data file.
The vutil is to see the number of records in the file...
When the .bat executes, a DOS window appears
for a few seconds until it reaches the 'exit' command
that I included in the .bat.
The .bat looks like this

@ECHO OFF
vutil32 -i u:\5root\usr\support\testdata\winunres.sup >
u:\5root\usr\support\testdata\xxx
exit

Then I read 'xxx' and use reference modification to see the # of records.
It all works great, but the brief appearance of the window is annoying
and totally unecessary.

Anyway to prevent it from happening?

RA.
```

```
Since there is only one program, why use a .BAT file?  You can execute it
directly.  You might also try starting it with START.EXE/W rather than with
command.com.  START has a switch for minimizing
the window.

Robert Annandale > wrote in message
<37a08b70$0$4485@fountain.mindlink.net>...

>> >with a 'wrun32 -b' since a different runtime does not support

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two AcuCOBOL Questions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <zimboon @ hotmail . com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37a0ac0e$0$4485@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<379f7e17$0$19185@fountain.mindlink.net> <7npu4f$ikl$1@news.cerf.net> <37a08b70$0$4485@fountain.mindlink.net> <7nq8hn$f3e$3@news.igs.net>`

```
Thanks for the suggestion.
I found a solution in the manual.
CALL "C$SYSTEM" USING CMD-LINE 32.
This will run CMD-LINE in a hidden window.
...works like a charm.

RA.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Two AcuCOBOL Questions

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nsd6c$dkp$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<379f7e17$0$19185@fountain.mindlink.net> <7npu4f$ikl$1@news.cerf.net> <37a08b70$0$4485@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Robert Annandale > wrote in message
<37a08b70$0$4485@fountain.mindlink.net>...
>I have to use a "CALL "SYSTEM" in my program.
>It calls a .bat file that runs a vutil on my data file.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>exit

Well, because of the batch file, you have execute the interpreter. I don't
know if the following samples would do any good, but, you could give it a
try;

First, how to do it with the Shell API:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. SHELLAPI.
       REMARKS.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 SHOW-OPTION         UNSIGNED-INT.
       01 H-ACU-WND           SIGNED-INT IS EXTERNAL.
       01 EXEC-RESULT       SIGNED-INT.

       01 ERROR-STR.
          03 FILLER           PIC X(18) VALUE "An error occured: ".
          03 EXEC-ERR         PIC S9(02).
       01 ERROR-STRING REDEFINES ERROR-STR PIC X(21).

       01 PARAMS.
          03 TASK             PIC X(30).
          03 APP              PIC X(30).
          03 APP-PARAMS       PIC X(30).
          03 DIRECTORY        PIC X(30).

       SCREEN SECTION.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
       MAIN-001.

           SET     ENVIRONMENT      "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 1.
      *We want the application minimized, and our current window to stay
active
      *we do so by setting SHOW-OPTION to 7.
    DISPLAY INITIAL          WINDOW
            TITLE-BAR        WITH SYSTEM MENU
            AUTO-MINIMIZE
            SIZE             40
            LINES     20
            TITLE            "Demo of the Shell API".
    STRING  "TEST.TXT"       LOW-VALUES DELIMITED BY SIZE
            INTO             APP.
    STRING  "open"           LOW-VALUES DELIMITED BY SIZE
            INTO             TASK.
    STRING  LOW-VALUES       DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO APP-PARAMS.
    STRING  LOW-VALUES       DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO DIRECTORY.
           MOVE    7                TO SHOW-OPTION.

    CALL    "SHELL32.DLL"    ON EXCEPTION GO TO MAIN-900.
      *Important note: All use of NULL is to terminate strings.
    CALL    "ShellExecuteA"  USING
            BY               VALUE H-ACU-WND
      *The following command instructs the function to open the application,
      *other options are: "print", "edit", "explore", "properties"
      *You may sett this one to NULL, the default action then is"open"
            BY REFERENCE     TASK
      *The file to open
            BY REFERENCE     APP
      *If we were to open an executable, any parameters may be sent here, if
      *the file is a document, we may not send parameters, thus setting to
      *NULL.
            BY REFERENCE     APP-PARAMS
      *Here we specify the directory of the application / document
      *NULL makes use of the current directory
                   BY REFERENCE     DIRECTORY
      *Finally we decide how we would like to application to launch,
      *See below for a list of possible options here
                   BY               VALUE SHOW-OPTION
                   GIVING     EXEC-RESULT
            ON               EXCEPTION GO TO MAIN-900.

           IF      EXEC-RESULT      < 33
               MOVE             EXEC-RESULT TO EXEC-ERR
                   DISPLAY          MESSAGE BOX
                                    ERROR-STRING
                                    TITLE "Error"
                                    TYPE 1
        ICON 3
    ELSE
            DISPLAY          "Done..."
            ACCEPT           OMITTED.

           CANCEL  "SHELL32.DLL".
           SET     ENVIRONMENT      "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 0.

       MAIN-900.
       MAIN-EXIT.
           GOBACK.

      *Possible show options:
      *SW_SHOWNORMAL       1 Launches, displays in standard mode and
activates
      *SW_SHOWMINIMIZED    2 Launches, minimizes and activates
      *SW_SHOWMAXIMIZED    3 Launches, displays maximized and activates.
      *SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE   4 Launches, displays and keep you window active.
      *SW_SHOW             5 Launches, activates and show it 'as is'.
      *SW_SHOWMINNOACTIVE  7 Launches, minimizes and keep your window
active.
      *SW_SHOWNA           8 Launches, displays, but keep your window
active.
      *SW_SHOWDEFAULT      10 Launches, activates and show window as it is
      *         programmatically set do to.

      * There are more options as well, these are however the most common
ones.

      *Error messages
      *                        0       System out of memory or resources
      *SE_ERR_FNF              2       file not found
      *SE_ERR_PNF              3       path not found
      *SE_ERR_ACCESSDENIED     5       access denied
      *SE_ERR_OOM              8       out of memory
      *SE_ERR_DLLNOTFOUND      32      Did not find the DLL (SHELL32.DLL)
      *SE_ERR_SHARE            26      Sharing violation (some other use the
      *                                file)
      *SE_ERR_ASSOCINCOMPLETE  27      Incomplete association of the
document
      *                                Control the association.
      *SE_ERR_DDETIMEOUT       28      Dynamic Data Exchange time out. Your
      *                                computer is either overloaded or your
      *                                network has a problem
      *SE_ERR_DDEFAIL          29      Could no complete DDE services
      *SE_ERR_DDEBUSY          30      Dynamic Data Exchange conquestion,
wait
      *                                and try again
      *SE_ERR_NOASSOC          31      There is no association defined for
this
      *                                file.

      * There are more errors as well, these are however the most common
ones.

Cheesle
```

- **From:** "Phylis DeWeese" <pdeweese@highland.net>

```
Hi everybody,
A gentleman who posts to this group sometimes referred me to y'all thinking
you may be able to help me.

I work on a Wang Vs and have made changes to our date routines to become y2k
compliant.  Everything was going great with testing until I ran into some
assembly programs  that are called.  Within the assembly program some macros
are used that deal with adding and subtracting julian dates. The real
problem comes when I am unable to find the source for these marcros.  I am
not an assembler programmer, so I don't feel that I can recreate these.  I
was hoping that maybe someone has some that they might share or know where I
might get some help!?

Phylis DeWeese
```

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Phylis DeWeese

For simple stuff like date mathematics I would suggest rewriting the
code in Cobol and using that Assembler for run time efficiency is just
not an issue any more with optimizing compilers and faster machines.

I think it was Judson that had a series of date modules on his home
page for download if you don't want to reinvent the wheel.

Ken

<a id="t-dfdc344297"></a>
## Thread: COBOL to IEEE datatypes — 6 msgs

### COBOL to IEEE datatypes

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic,comp.lang.c++
- **Message-ID:** `<NFGn3.360$ds6.10198@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
7/28/99

As you know, I am working on a conversion tool to convert COBOL datatypes to
IEEE datatypes so BASIC and C/C++ programmers can read COBOL data files.
(Free for personal use tool).

I have got the COMP (binary) datatypes working, and have run into a problem;
a problem which I will also have on the BCD and DISPLAY types.

Returning a long (32-bit) integer mantissa (the significant decimal digits),
we get nine (sometimes ten) significant digits. My test data includes data
up to 15 significant digits, and I think COBOL can handle sixteen or
seventeen.

A. Is this 'significant digit deficiency syndrome' going to be a problem in
the real world?

B. If so, what is the best solution?
  1. A 64-bit mantissa (can Visual BASIC handle 64-bit integers? How about C
compilers?)
  2. 64-bits the hard way: two thirty-two bit data items.
  3. A display (character) output, to be used with BASIC's "VAL" or C's
'scanf' functions?
  4. If oversize, use an IEEE double-precision real (float) for the value
(16 significant digits)
  5. Some combination of above? Fielder's choice?

Also, for the COBOL-enabled:
I have pretty-well exhausted my test data and would like some "real world"
data to test with. Yes, it can be "fabricated", but I've always found "live"
data includes some of those items which make our jobs so much fun: illegal
sign nibbles in BCD data, unexpected binary zero in the middle of character
data (i.e., leftover MOVE LOW-VALUES TO GROUP-ITEM). PICTURE S9(6)
containing "DOGGY ", etc.

If you can contribute a data file (does not need to be too big, please), the
matching COBOL FD, and some kind of file or printout showing me what the
values should be, I will be very appreciative.
(Also let me know if the data are EBCDIC or ASCII. This tool will also
convert the appropriate data from EBCDIC to ASCII if you tell it to).

Yes, you, too, get a credit. (No cash, no merchandise, which is exactly what
I get, too!). (Unless someone wants to do something commercial. Then it
ain't free).

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to IEEE datatypes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic,comp.lang.c++
- **Message-ID:** `<7nnq4b$4qq@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<NFGn3.360$ds6.10198@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
COBOL requires that you be able to store 18 "significant digits" (in the
current ANSI/ISO Standard).  The draft of the next Standard will require 31
digit precision (and this was added because - at least some - users actually
said that 18 wasn't enough).

  Probably NOT the answer that you wanted to hear.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to IEEE datatypes

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic,comp.lang.c++
- **Message-ID:** `<190o3.251$m95.11511@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<NFGn3.360$ds6.10198@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7nnq4b$4qq@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <7nnq4b$4qq@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>...
>COBOL requires that you be able to store 18 "significant digits" (in the
>current ANSI/ISO Standard).  The draft of the next Standard will require 31
>digit precision (and this was added because - at least some - users
actually
>said that 18 wasn't enough).
>
>  Probably NOT the answer that you wanted to hear.


Wrong, this is what I wanted to hear - facts. Right, I did not enjoy hearing
it.

What is important is "more than ten siginificant digits."

As long as it's "more than ten" it really makes no difference if it's 18 or
31 digits .... Well, 31 could be a challenge, as even a sixty-four bit
integer only has 18 (sometimes 19) significant digits and I'd have to dream
up some way to do the math.

Now, how should I return these values? Display? Since a 64-bit float
(COMP-2) only has 19 digit accuracy (and more importantly, is subject to
implementor variations) that does not seem the best way. Inventing a 96-bit
binary kind of defeats the purpose of the exercise, as the user will end up
re-doing all the work the software does. (BTW, I know have the BCD and the
binary working and all I have left is the display input). (Except for
expanding the output digit count).

Well, inspiration should come....

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to IEEE datatypes

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.lang.basic,alt.lang.powerbasic,comp.lang.c++

> Now, how should I return these values? Display? Since a 64-bit float
> (COMP-2) only has 19 digit accuracy (and more importantly, is subject to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

An array of characters works fine to return any size number.

I like a language such as REXX which allows virtually any size number -
and think COBOL should do the same thing (recognizing that efficiency
drops when your number gets larger than what the processor handles
natively).  Maybe have a special usage clause which allows for super
large numbers.

An interesting excercize is to write a COBOL program to calculate PI to
100 places. (without searching the web to find one which already
exists).
```

<a id="t-2b31c2b0e1"></a>
## Thread: Allocate a PCB dynamically via AIBTDLI INQY — 2 msgs

### Allocate a PCB dynamically via AIBTDLI INQY

- **From:** "icare" <icare@wanadoo.be>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nnir8$51c@news3.euro.net>`

```
does any one have experience and example code to get a PCB dynamically via
CALL "AIBTDLI " with INQY and use it further.
Code should demonstrate usage POINTER.
I am confused.
```

#### ↳ Re: Allocate a PCB dynamically via AIBTDLI INQY

- **From:** stever@sjrnet.u-net.com (Steve R)
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379f623e.6570288@news.u-net.com>`
- **References:** `<7nnir8$51c@news3.euro.net>`

```
Wow, an IMS question here !!!
Haven't got the manual to hand, but here goes.

First thing you need to do is ensure that you've put a name on the PCB in the PSB,
otherwise with the exception of the IOPCB this will never work.

Assuming you're coding in Cobol (use of POINTER implies this).
Then code up the AIB in working storage in your program as per the manual.
There's a field to fill in the PCB name that you've set up in the PSB, and also a PCB
address field - which you should define as POINTER.

Code the database PCB in your linkage section - (i.e.  01 LS-PCB-MASK) as if it was being
passed from IMS in the traditional way, but DON'T include it in the PROCEDURE DIVISION
USING .... or in an ENTRY xxxx USING .....

Then it's CALL 'AIBTDLI' USING WS-INQY
                                                    WS-AIB-IOAREA
                                                     
Check the return & reason codes as per the manual and then, assuming the call worked you
use the SET verb to
SET ADDRESS OF LS-PCB-MASK TO <aib-pcb-address-dataname>

Once you've done this you can continue to use the AIB to make calls against the database,
or having SET the address, you can do all the other calls in the module using the more
traditional CBLTDLI.

Best use of using the AIB is when you're changing a common routine used by lots of
programs where you don't particularly want to have to change the linkage through all of
them to when the common routine needs to access a new database.  Use the AIB and then all
you need to do is update the PSBs - no code changes needed along the calling path.
AIB return/reason codes can also be more useful than the DL/I status codes, but are
somewhat complex.

 
SteveR

On 28 Jul 1999 18:40:40 GMT, "icare" <icare@wanadoo.be> wrote:

>does any one have experience and example code to get a PCB dynamically via
>CALL "AIBTDLI " with INQY and use it further.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nn641$d2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I need to know how to reverse this command.

vutil -R -A +C -F variableA variableB



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: vutil

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7nnqic$r0c$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7nn641$d2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
jrogers42@hotmail.com wrote in message <7nn641$d2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I need to know how to reverse this command.
>
>vutil -R -A +C -F variableA variableB

I assume this is for Acucobol vision?

-r is nothing to reverse.

-a is nothing to reverse.

+c, the opposite of compression on, is compression off, hence: -c

Compression off, does not need a factor, hence nothing to reverse of -f.
Then no variableA, I assume variableB is your filename, this is constant.

The I would do:

vutil -R -A -C variableB
```

#### ↳ Re: vutil

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ikGn3.407$_3.10479@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7nn641$d2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I assume you are talking about AcuCobol 85.  If so,
1) Perhaps you don't need to do anything except give the world permission to
read the file.  You created a new file with your default permissions while
the old one was deleted;  your new file was renamed automatically (the -a
operator).  Just do a

  chmod 666 variableA variableB

Before trying the next two (if necessary), back up your existing file first.
2) You might have to rebuild the file based on something other than it's
primary key.  If that is the case, specify -k n  when you run vutil against
that file again where n is > 0.

3) The file is FUBAR.  If you can restore from a backup, try to do so.
Otherwise unload the file and recreate (generate) the indexed file, then
reload it.  The vutil program is documented in the AcuCobol-85 "User's
Guide".

Good luck.

jrogers42@hotmail.com wrote in message <7nn641$d2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I need to know how to reverse this command.
>
>vutil -R -A +C -F variableA variableB
```

#### ↳ Re: vutil

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <zimboon @ hotmail . com>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379f4630$0$19193@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7nn641$d2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Your files are gone.
They were automatically replaced when you used the -a command.
It should always be a habit to make a copy of a file before a rebuild.

Assuming the rebuild was successful (no broken chains)
then all you would of lost is 'deleted records' that Vision
hangs on to until a rebuild clears them.

As for the reversal, you can still reverse the compression you stipulated.

May I suggest you do the following;

- backup the current file (This should be second nature, like 'rm -i
filename'!!!!)
- vutil -c filename (This will reverse compression).

Why do you want to reverse it anyways?
What is it you wish to undo?

Robert Annandale
Vancouver, BC

> I need to know how to reverse this command.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

> Ok, I'm running a win98 partition and a linux partition.  I have all sorts of
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> E. Leron Culbreath


I'm big on playing computer games.  So to get me goin back in 1980 I
purchased some books (that are so old they should all be out of print)
that contained program listings of simple games written in good old
BASIC.  I then took on the challange of translating these programs into
other languages.  The translation process forces creative thinking,
which is a real asset in this business.  In the real world of business
programming, you always know WHAT you need to do, but you must think
creatively about HOW to do it.

I still have some of these BASIC books on my office shelf.  One of them
is:
   MORE BASIC COMPUTER GAMES
   Workman Publishing, New York,  c. 1979
   
Pete Wirfs
Salem Oregon
```

As reported in the J4 document at
 http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/99-0385.doc

The next (and probably applies to this) draft of the draft Standard will
have the following 2 paragraphs on its cover.

"This is a draft proposed standard of ISO/IEC and Accredited Standards
Committee NCITS.  As such this is not a completed standard.  ISO/IEC
JTC1/SC22/WG4 and NCITS/J4 may modify this document as a result of comments
received during review periods before its approval as an international
standard.  Use of the information contained herein is at your own risk.

Permission is granted to members of ISO, IEC, and JTC1 and to their member
bodies, subcommittees, and working groups to reproduce this document for the
purposes of standardization activities without further permission, provided
this notice is included.  Permission is granted to members of NCITS, its
technical committees, and their associated task groups to reproduce this
document for the purposes of NCITS standardization activities without
further permission, provided this notice is included.  All other rights are
reserved. Any commercial or for-profit duplication is strictly prohibited."


Comments (if any) should go to the chair of J4 (who also was the author of
that paper) at
   doncobol <at> mediaone.net
```

## Thread: ratings... — 1 msgs

### ratings...

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<379EFD1D.8BE257CC@zip.com.au>`

```
Ok,  I am working up a cobol ratings site.  I want to throw a design
out for critique:

My ISP will support Linux Perl and TCL CGI scripts.  No database and
no frills site.  I have never done this so a volunteer would be
appreciated but not necessary.  I suppose I should learn this stuff.

Design constraints:

1.  It must reside in sequential files and be low tech
2.  It must be automatic to add a response file
3.  Must run on my ISP (obviously).

There is a POST page that e-mail's me with the entry from the screen.
There are a series of CGI's that read files and generate themselves.

1.  There is a file containing a list of vendors and compilers

2.  There is a file containing each compiler and the OS it supports

3.  There is a subdirectory named after each compiler

4.  Each compiler file contains compiler.html which contain compiler
information (all OS's etc).

Vendor file:

1.  Vendor name   |
2.  Compiler name |
3.  Vendor web page

Compiler file

1. Operating system |
2. Compiler name

Compiler directory contains:

1.  Index file (basic compiler details)
2.  A series of response sheets formatted as HTML tables.

(*****  scripts ****)

1.  Script 1 will read the vendor file and throw a simple page list
and point into the compiler subdirectory for INDEX CGI.

Script 2 will create a form allowing a drop down of unique operating
systems and a second drop down with Vendor.  Drop boxes have {ALL} as
default.

Script 3 lists the results of the above query.  With hyperlinks to the
various folders index files.

Script 4 will glue the index and the response files into a single page
for the return.  May have optional version / OS selection if I can
figure this out.

Post page:

.  Compiler id  (from drop down or new value)
.  Version
.  OS and version
.  Date used
.  Anonymous report (show email address?)
.  Elapsed time used
.  Support response (if any)
.  Good points
.  Bad points

*  I was considering requesting the real name of the reporter for
legal reasons, if I was challenged would I need to show the responses
came from real people or would email addresses suffice?

Anything else.  Put on your analysis hats.

Ken
```

No, its not homework, and yes i would like to be educated.

The idea is this : i get a large stream of chars, in fact 100 to 1000. This
strem is logically divided in groups (records if you like) of 100 chars
each. So with my first move from table-src to table-tmp i get a table of
records instead of a table of bytes, so to say. In fact , and i didnt
mention it on my first post, the lenght of table-src is allways a multiple
of 100 ( because each record has 100 bytes and there is a max of 10
records). Now,the 19 bytes starting from position 81 of each record must be
changed to '0' (i ommited that move on my first post), and at last this
modified record (with 100 chars and with '0' betten position 81 and 99) must
be moved to a table where each entry has not 100 lenght but 99, so this new
table has the same number of entries as table-tmp but each entry has a byte
less.Now, the real problem is this statements are not part of a standart
cobol program but of a cobol/CICS program . Im new to cics and have no idea
on on to debug it ( pehaps someone can tell me how to display working
storage on terminal when a transaction is running ?).

Thx in advance

<a id="t-93dc4390e0"></a>
## Thread: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes — 3 this month / 10 total

### Re: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes

- **From:** "Asbj���rn Danielsen" <asbjornd@ifi.uio.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.object,comp.object.logic,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35E5198E.CAEF927B@ifi.uio.no>`
- **References:** `<01bdccb3$82bbcd00$c0e6e6c8@mnfala> <35DD6728.A4A0EA62@ifi.uio.no> <35E2C60F.1FE1@bt.com> <35E3DE3F.B333437@ifi.uio.no> <35E41E3F.762C@bt.com>`

```
Patrick Herring wrote:

> ...
> > a) oft-seen mistake (1) : network databases are more flexible than
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> included it as the lead-in to:

I'm happy to see we agree :-)

> > b) oft-seen mistake (2) : "applications using a network database has
> > to be changed every time the structure of the database changes"
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> weird).

Why SQL, is it because it can easily be translated into navigational
operations?

> ...
> > c) Real World .. no such implications
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> committing to knowledge of them.

I agree with you that to be able to perform inserts/updates/deletes in a
RDBMS the programmer would need to know about referential integrity, table
layout, keys, etc. to be able to develop the program. That was not my
point, it was merely an intro (establishing some properties of an RDBMS
program) to the next sentence:

> > The relational model represents an abstraction of the physical
> structure,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> physical knowledge being committed to.

Except the existence of the set.

> 'Abstracted' doesn't mean 'hide entirely'! In fact I see relational
> DBMSes as basically being cut-down networked DBMSes with a 4GL front-end
> (which also exists for IDMS for eg). By 'cut-down' I mean, for eg, that
> foreign keys correspond to single record-type sorted sets in IDMS,
> whereas IDMS has several other styles of set connection.

Well, I have to disagree. I find them conceptually different. The only
three things being common to both:1: They support multi-user concurrent
access to data while ensuring the ACID properties of transactions, logging
and recovery.
2: Both have a DML defining access to data (CODASYL DML defining how access
should be done, SQL defining what access should be done) and a DDL defining
the structure of the database.
3: Both DMLs can be accessed using a general purpose programming language

The abstraction is ofcourse not of the RDBMS, rather on the structure of
the data in the database, and yes it does not imply "hide entirely". If it
was totally hidden, how could it then be accessed? The only kind of
"database system" being able to hide such info are persistent programming
languages supporting orthogonal persistence.

> ...
> > Two simple points:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> advantage of relational over network in this matter.

This was not either my point, as you see in my quote, I do not express any
difference between RDBMS vs. any other kind of DBMS (maybe the word "point"
was badly choosen because it was merly meant to point out that in almost
any database you need to change both program and database if you add
attributes to the database and want to access them).

> There are some optimisations that can't be done in the relational model
> precisely because the only possible interface to the DB is so abstract.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> processing.

Agreed, RDBMS do not offer this kind of optimization, and the optimization
would ofcourse only work if the record-size of all referenced records fit
within one page. Most OODBMS on the other hand offers this kind of
clustering. Some RDBMSs supports dynamic or static arrays (which may be
included in SQL3). The arrays are defined as relations themself, but
usually contained within the same page(s) as the row they belong to (if it
fits within a page). This would probably be a part of SQL3 since SQL3
should be able handle objects.

> AFAIK an indexed read in most relational DBMSes involves at
> least two physical reads, one for the index & one for the data, and
> there just isn't an option to use hashing explicitly (though I daresay
> they use it behind the scenes).

I am afraid you are wrong about this. E.g. Ingres (Computer Associates)
offers tables with hashed keys, and I suspect some other RDBMS offer it as
well. What the RDBMS vendors do behind the scenes, I do not know.

I find that I do not have sufficient knowledge about implementation
mechanisms of the different RDBMS to be able to discuss them.

> > d) ... wondering whether undergrads actually get taught this
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> physical stuff, which is the impression I usually get.

Well, I can't answer for everyone, only for what is lectured at University
of Oslo, but I suspect that UiO is no different than any other
university. The lectures/courses do not consider network DBMS to be a minor
improvement of hierarchical ones. They are considered as two separate DBMS
concepts with different properties, as is RDBMS, ORDBMS, OODBMS, and
persistent languages as well. No lecturer in his/her right mind would say
that one of them are better than the other because it would all depend on
the problem domain and the universe of discourse, and in the end it all
comes down to application and database design.I think that the RDBMS is by
many considered to be the "best" because they are more common (even though
network and hierarchical may be the most frequently used and definitly the
major DBMSs found in legacy systems, or the biggest DB in the world is
based on an OODBMS) than other DBMS, and the RDBMS vendors are very good at
marketing and showing off their view of the world.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: compare( Network, Relational) DBMSes

- **From:** "Asbj���rn Danielsen" <asbjornd@ifi.uio.no>
- **Date:** 1999-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.smalltalk,comp.object,comp.object.logic,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35E67297.5170397F@ifi.uio.no>`
- **References:** `<01bdccb3$82bbcd00$c0e6e6c8@mnfala> <35DD6728.A4A0EA62@ifi.uio.no> <35E2C60F.1FE1@bt.com> <35E3DE3F.B333437@ifi.uio.no> <35E41E3F.762C@bt.com> <35E5198E.CAEF927B@ifi.uio.no> <35E580CF.5EC4@bt.com>`

```
Patrick Herring wrote:

> Asbjï¿½rn Danielsen wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the existence of a foreign key relation. Same thing?

No, not really. The set is accessed explicit (physical) when used in an
application using network DBMS. In a relational DBMS the foreign key is
only defined when the table is defined and is not used by the application
explicit, though the programmer should ofcourse know about it. As a
consequence the "foreign key" in network DBMSs are logical and physical to
both application and database, in relational DBMSs the foreign key is
logical to the application and logical/physical to the database.

> > > 'Abstracted' doesn't mean 'hide entirely'! In fact I see relational
> > > DBMSes as basically being cut-down networked DBMSes with a 4GL
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agreed, the implementation is something quite different. E.g. Chris Date
claims that none of the RDBMS vendors have implemented the relational model
as defined by Codd. Take a look at "The Third Manifesto" by Darwen & Date,
available at
http://cdr.cs.uiuc.edu/cgi-bin/cred/security/credentials/record/95-3/papers/manifesto.ps,
or Date being interviewed by DBMS Magazine in October '94 at
http://www.dbmsmag.com/int9410.html, last section "What is the greatest
misconception about relational technology?".

> The theory was always that SQL would make it possible for non-techies to
> access their own data, thus freeing them from the need to go through the
> DP department all the time. The last time I worked with DB2 we found our
> user couldn't get his head round the concept of a file so he couldn't
> even enter his SQL statements. So we did it for him...

:-)No, I think your user represent the common user. Take a person, not
familiar or understanding the relational model, but able to write
syntacticly correct SQL-statements, and in a matter of mininutes he will be
able to create a query (using two or more tables) resulting in a cartesian
product. At least this is my experience :-))

> ...
> So whence came the view from your page, that programmers of networked DBs
> need to know more about the physical representation of the logical
> structure than their counterparts in the relational world?

I haven't said much about the logical structure of a network DBMS. In the
essay I merly present the mechanism of navigation in a network DBMS. The
only thing I mention is that whenever the structure of the database is
changed (meaning e.g. new sets added), the application need to be changed.
For relational DBMSs I say:

"The relational approach separates the program from the physical
implementation of the database, making the program less sensitive to
changes of the physical representation of the data by unifying data and
metadata in the database. This makes the development of programs more
effective and less dependent on changes in the physical representation of
data ".

Because metadata is not "programmed" in both application and database using
the relational model, the code needed, in the program, to be able to access
the data in the database and map them into memory of the application, is
bigger (not necessarily more complex) using a networked DBMS versus a
relational DBMS.
E.g. If you in a network DBMS access a record, and on this record you are
using a set to access a number of other records, you would end up with
something two or three explicit calls to the database and perform the calls
in some kind of controlled loop. In the relational model you would write
one explicit call, a query, and the DBMS would execute it (hiding how the
actual accesses to the data are performed). To put it in one sentence; It
is easier/shorter (for most people, including application developers) to
tell what they want, than expressing the algorithm to be used to achieve
what they want.

> And that network DB designs are more likely than relational ones to
> evolve themselves into an un-upgradeable corner because the interface
> navigates the DB structure explicitly by named connections rather than
> implicitly by foreign key connections? I still don't get it.

Well, to be honest, neither do I. I have never expressed such a view
neither verbally nor in any written form (e.g. the "Essay"). Any database
using any model may get into problems if the model is "wrong" (the only
problem is to find out what "wrong" is. AFAIK no metric exist to detect a
right or wrong data model, only "rules" on how things should be done to get
a "good" model). The most common reason for a model to become "wrong" are
changing requirements. If some DBMS-concept should be able to handle such
changing requirements well, I would suspect it to be network DBMS and
OODBMSs. They would prbably handle such changes better than RDBMSs and
hierarchical DBMSs, due to the fact that their data-models are able to
express relations which the others can't.

If you find any expressions in my Essay suggesting/implying that the
networked data-model are more likely to evolve into an un-upgradeable
corner or that the network data-model is less flexible than some other,
please let me know, because it have never been intended (if it exist in the
Essay, which I find hard to believe)
```
